python.title: Python Syntax and Basics
python.description: Learn Python syntax, data types, control flow, and how to set up a virtual environment with pip. This layer establishes the foundation for writing readable, idiomatic Python code.

resources.label: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python

python.challenge: Build a command-line number guessing game that tracks attempts and uses a virtual environment with a requirements.txt file.

children.title: Variables are references
children.description: Every Python variable is a label pointing to an object in memory, not a container — reassigning it just moves the label.

children.title: Mutable vs immutable
children.description: Immutable objects (str, int, tuple) can't change after creation; mutable ones (list, dict) can — this drives how Python copies and hashes data.

children.title: Duck typing
children.description: Python checks what methods an object supports, not what class it belongs to — if it walks like a duck, it is a duck.

children.title: Lists, dicts, sets, tuples
children.description: Each collection has a purpose: lists for ordered sequences, dicts for key lookups, sets for uniqueness, tuples for immutable records.

children.title: Virtual environments
children.description: python -m venv creates an isolated package space per project so dependencies never conflict between projects.

children.title: pip & requirements.txt
children.description: pip installs packages from PyPI; requirements.txt pins exact versions so any teammate can reproduce the same environment.

children.title: F-strings
children.description: F-strings embed any Python expression directly inside a string literal and are the fastest, most readable formatting option.

children.title: Unpacking
children.description: Assign multiple variables from one iterable in a single line; use * to capture the remaining items into a list.

python.title: Functions, Modules, and Packages
python.description: Master Python functions including default arguments, *args, **kwargs, and lambda expressions. Learn to organize code into modules and packages and import them correctly.
python.challenge: Extend the guessing game into a multi-module package with a utils module for input validation and a stats module for tracking scores.

children.title: LEGB scope rules
children.description: Python looks up a name in Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in order — understanding this prevents accidental shadowing.

children.title: Functions as objects
children.description: Functions are first-class values: store them in variables, pass them as arguments, or return them from other functions.

children.title: *args / **kwargs
children.description: *args packs extra positional arguments into a tuple; **kwargs packs extra keyword arguments into a dict.

resources.label: Python Docs — More on Defining Functions

children.title: Lambda expressions
children.description: lambda creates a throwaway one-expression function inline — most useful as a key= argument to sorted() or map().

children.title: Modules & packages
children.description: A module is any .py file; a package is a directory with __init__.py — splitting code this way keeps each file focused and importable.

children.title: Standard library
children.description: Python ships batteries-included: os, sys, pathlib, math, random, json, csv — know what exists before reaching for a third-party package.

resources.label: Enriching Your Python with the stdlib

children.title: Type hints
children.description: Annotations like def f(x: int) -> str tell tools and readers what types are expected without enforcing them at runtime.

children.title: Docstrings
children.description: A docstring inside the function body documents its purpose, arguments, and return value in a format IDEs and Sphinx can parse.

python.title: Object-Oriented Python
python.description: Understand Python classes, inheritance, dunder methods, and properties to model real-world problems. Learn how Python OOP differs from other languages through special method protocols.
python.challenge: Refactor the guessing game into an OOP design with a Game class, a Player class, and a Leaderboard class using dunder methods for comparison.

children.title: Method Resolution Order
children.description: Python's MRO (C3 algorithm) determines which class's method wins in a multiple-inheritance chain — inspect it with ClassName.__mro__.

children.title: Dunder methods
children.description: Implement __str__, __eq__, __lt__ etc. to make your objects work with print(), ==, sorted() and other built-in operations.

resources.label: Enriching Python Classes with Dunder Methods

children.title: @dataclass
children.description: @dataclass auto-generates __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ from annotated fields, eliminating repetitive constructor code.

resources.label: The Ultimate Guide to Python Dataclasses

children.title: frozen=True
children.description: frozen=True prevents attribute mutation and makes the dataclass hashable, so instances can be used as dict keys or set members.

children.title: @property
children.description: @property exposes a method as a read-only attribute — the caller uses dot access and never sees the underlying function call.

children.title: Property setters
children.description: Pair @name.setter with @property to run validation before storing a value, keeping invariants in one place.

children.title: @classmethod factories
children.description: @classmethod receives the class as cls, enabling factory methods like from_json() that construct instances in multiple ways.

children.title: @staticmethod
children.description: @staticmethod groups a pure helper inside a class without needing self or cls — it is a namespace choice, not an OOP one.

python.title: Iterators, Generators, and Comprehensions
python.description: Dive into Python's iteration protocol, generator functions, and the expressive power of list and dict comprehensions. These tools are central to idiomatic, memory-efficient Python.
python.challenge: Add a streaming stats feature to the game that uses a generator to lazily compute running averages and percentiles across sessions.

children.title: Iteration protocol
children.description: A for loop calls iter() to get an iterator then next() repeatedly until StopIteration — implement __iter__/__next__ to make any object iterable.

children.title: yield mechanics
children.description: yield pauses the function and returns a value to the caller; the next next() call resumes from exactly that point with all locals intact.

children.title: Generator expressions
children.description: (x for x in y) yields one value at a time instead of building a full list — critical when processing large files or infinite sequences.

resources.label: When to Use a Generator Expression

children.title: Infinite generators
children.description: Generators can model infinite sequences because they only compute the next value on demand; use itertools.islice to take a finite slice.

children.title: Comprehensions
children.description: [x for x in y], {k: v}, {x} transform iterables concisely and run faster than equivalent for-loops due to C-level iteration.

children.title: Comprehension filters
children.description: Add an if clause to keep only matching elements — equivalent to filter() but more readable inline.

children.title: chain & islice
children.description: itertools.chain concatenates iterables lazily; islice takes a fixed slice from any iterator including infinite ones — no intermediate lists.

children.title: Combinatorics
children.description: itertools.combinations, permutations, and product generate the full combinatorial space lazily — essential for search and test matrices.

python.title: Error Handling, Context Managers, and File I/O
python.description: Learn robust exception handling, the with statement, and reading and writing files including JSON and CSV. These patterns are essential for reliable, production-quality Python programs.
python.challenge: Add a persistent leaderboard to the game that saves and loads scores from a JSON file using a custom context manager for safe file access.

children.title: Custom exceptions
children.description: Subclass Exception with a descriptive name so callers can catch your errors specifically; use raise ... from to preserve the original traceback.

children.title: try/except/else/finally
children.description: else runs only when no exception was raised; finally always runs — use it for cleanup regardless of what happened in the try block.

children.title: __enter__ / __exit__
children.description: with calls __enter__ on entry and __exit__ on exit — even if an exception is raised — guaranteeing resources are always released.

resources.label: Context Managers and the with Statement

children.title: @contextmanager
children.description: @contextmanager turns a generator function into a context manager: code before yield is setup, code after is teardown.

children.title: open() with with
children.description: Always open files in a with block so they close automatically; use 'r'/'w' for text, 'rb'/'wb' for binary data.

children.title: pathlib.Path
children.description: pathlib.Path gives an object-oriented, cross-platform API for file paths, replacing fragile os.path string concatenation.

children.title: json module
children.description: json.dump writes a Python dict/list to a file; json.load reads it back — use indent=2 for human-readable output.

children.title: csv.DictReader
children.description: csv.DictReader maps each CSV row to a dict keyed by column headers, eliminating fragile positional index access.

resources.label: Reading and Writing CSV Files in Python

python.title: Decorators, Closures, and Functional Patterns
python.description: Understand closures and how decorators work under the hood to add behavior to functions without modifying them. Learn functools and common functional programming patterns in Python.
python.challenge: Add a @retry decorator and a @timer decorator to the game engine, and use lru_cache for caching difficulty level configurations.

children.title: Closure capture
children.description: A closure remembers the variables from the scope where it was defined — even after that scope has exited — enabling factories and callbacks.

children.title: nonlocal keyword
children.description: Without nonlocal, assigning to a name in a nested function creates a new local variable instead of mutating the enclosing one.

children.title: Decorator mechanics
children.description: A decorator is a function that takes a callable and returns a new one with added behaviour — @ is just syntactic sugar for wrapping.

children.title: functools.wraps
children.description: @wraps copies __name__, __doc__, and __module__ from the original onto the wrapper so introspection keeps working.

children.title: Decorator factories
children.description: A factory returns a decorator so you can pass arguments: @retry(times=3) adds one extra nesting level but makes the decorator configurable.

children.title: Stacking decorators
children.description: When decorators are stacked the bottom one wraps first, then each outer one wraps the result — order matters for auth and caching.

children.title: @lru_cache
children.description: @lru_cache stores return values keyed by arguments so repeated calls with the same input are instant — no repeated computation.

children.title: functools.partial
children.description: partial creates a new callable with some arguments already bound — useful for adapting a function to a callback interface with fewer parameters.

python.title: Testing with pytest
python.description: Write unit and integration tests using pytest, fixtures, and parametrize to ensure code correctness. Learn test-driven development workflows and how to measure code coverage.
python.challenge: Write a full pytest suite for the game project covering all modules, using fixtures for player setup, mocking for file I/O, and achieving 90%+ coverage.

children.title: AAA pattern
children.description: Split every test into Arrange (setup), Act (call the code), Assert (check the result) — it makes tests easy to read and debug.

children.title: Test isolation
children.description: Tests that share mutable state can pass alone but fail together; each test must set up and tear down its own state independently.

children.title: Red-Green-Refactor
children.description: Write a failing test (red), add just enough code to pass it (green), then clean up the implementation (refactor) without changing behaviour.

children.title: Test-first design
children.description: Writing the test before the code forces you to define the API and expected behaviour upfront, revealing design flaws early.

resources.label: Getting Started with TDD in Python

children.title: @pytest.fixture
children.description: Fixtures provide reusable setup/teardown code injected by name; conftest.py makes them available across a directory without imports.

children.title: parametrize
children.description: @pytest.mark.parametrize runs the same test with multiple input sets, reporting each case separately with a single declaration.

children.title: mock.patch
children.description: @patch swaps a real object with a MagicMock for the duration of the test, isolating the unit from external services and the filesystem.

resources.label: Understanding the Python Mock Object Library

children.title: pytest-cov
children.description: pytest-cov reports which lines and branches were executed, surfacing untested paths before they turn into production regressions.

python.title: Concurrency - Threads, Async, and Multiprocessing
python.description: Learn the difference between threading, multiprocessing, and asyncio, and when to use each for I/O-bound versus CPU-bound tasks. Master async/await syntax for modern asynchronous Python.

resources.label: Python Concurrency from the Ground Up

python.challenge: Add an async leaderboard API client to the game that fetches global high scores concurrently using asyncio and aiohttp.

children.title: The GIL
children.description: Python's GIL allows only one thread to execute bytecode at a time — threading helps I/O waits but cannot parallelise CPU work.

children.title: Concurrency model choice
children.description: I/O-bound tasks: asyncio or threading. CPU-bound tasks: multiprocessing. Pick based on where time is spent, not familiarity.

children.title: Event loop scheduling
children.description: The event loop runs tasks cooperatively — when one awaits I/O it yields control, letting another task run without threads.

resources.label: Async IO in Python: A Complete Walkthrough

children.title: Coroutine vs Task vs Future
children.description: async def returns a coroutine; create_task wraps it in a scheduled Task; Future is a placeholder for a result not yet ready.

resources.label: Python Docs — asyncio coroutines and tasks

children.title: async / await
children.description: async def defines a coroutine; await suspends it until the result is ready; asyncio.run() starts the event loop for the top-level coroutine.

children.title: asyncio.gather()
children.description: gather() runs multiple coroutines concurrently and returns all results as a list once every task completes.

children.title: aiohttp ClientSession
children.description: ClientSession is an async HTTP client that reuses connections; wrap it in async with to ensure the session closes cleanly.

children.title: aiofiles
children.description: aiofiles offloads file reads/writes to a thread pool so the event loop stays free to handle other tasks during disk I/O.

resources.label: Python Docs — run_in_executor for blocking I/O

python.title: Type Hints, Protocols, and Modern Python Idioms
python.description: Apply full type annotations, use mypy for static analysis, and learn structural subtyping via Protocols. Explore modern Python idioms introduced in 3.10+ including pattern matching.
python.challenge: Fully annotate the game project, pass mypy --strict with zero errors, and refactor game state handling to use pattern matching.

children.title: Protocol
children.description: Protocol defines an interface by required methods, not by class hierarchy — any object that has those methods satisfies the protocol.

children.title: TypeVar & Generics
children.description: TypeVar declares a type placeholder resolved at call time — combined with Generic it lets you write type-safe containers and utilities.

children.title: match / case
children.description: Python 3.10's match statement destructures values by shape, replacing long if/elif chains and making control flow easier to read.

children.title: Walrus operator :=
children.description: The walrus operator assigns and returns a value in the same expression — avoids repeating expensive calls inside while or if conditions.

children.title: mypy --strict
children.description: --strict enables all mypy checks; fixing the errors incrementally drives you to fully annotate your codebase.

children.title: mypy config
children.description: [tool.mypy] in pyproject.toml centralises settings like strict, ignore_missing_imports, and per-module overrides.

children.title: TypedDict
children.description: TypedDict describes expected keys and value types of a dict so type checkers can validate dict access without a full class.

children.title: NamedTuple
children.description: typing.NamedTuple creates a typed tuple subclass with named fields — supports unpacking, hashing, and all tuple operations.

python.title: Production Python - Packaging, Performance, and Ecosystem
python.description: Learn to package and publish Python projects, profile and optimize performance, and navigate the broader Python ecosystem for production use. Understand CI/CD integration and dependency management best practices.
topics: Dependency management with pip-tools or poetry
CI/CD with GitHub Actions for Python
python.challenge: Package the completed game project as a distributable CLI tool on PyPI with a pyproject.toml, GitHub Actions CI running pytest, and a profiled, optimized game loop.

children.title: PEP 517 / 518
children.description: PEP 518 standardised build dependencies in pyproject.toml; PEP 517 defined the hook interface any build backend must implement.

children.title: Wheel vs sdist
children.description: A wheel installs without compiling; an sdist ships source and requires a build step. pip prefers wheels for speed.

resources.label: What Are Python Wheels and Why Should You Care?

children.title: cProfile
children.description: cProfile records call counts and cumulative time per function; sort pstats output by tottime to find the real bottlenecks.

children.title: tracemalloc
children.description: tracemalloc snapshots memory allocations and their source locations — compare two snapshots to find what is responsible for growth.

children.title: build & twine
children.description: python -m build produces a wheel and sdist in dist/; twine upload pushes them to PyPI authenticated by an API token.

children.title: Trusted publishing
children.description: PyPI trusted publishers lets GitHub Actions publish without a stored token — it verifies identity via OIDC instead.

children.title: CI matrix strategy
children.description: GitHub Actions matrix strategy runs your test job in parallel across every Python version, catching version-specific regressions automatically.

children.title: pre-commit & ruff
children.description: pre-commit runs linting and formatting hooks before each commit; ruff replaces flake8 and isort at 100x the speed.

javascript.title: JavaScript Syntax and Fundamentals
javascript.description: Learn the core syntax of JavaScript including variables, types, operators, and control flow in both browser and Node.js environments. Understand the dynamic and loosely-typed nature of the language.
topics: Running JS in browser and Node.js
javascript.challenge: Build a command-line to-do list app in Node.js that supports add, list, and remove commands using only core JS and no external libraries.

children.title: V8 JIT compilation
children.description: V8 compiles hot functions to machine code at runtime — understanding this explains why the same code can be fast or slow depending on how predictable its types are.

resources.label: JavaScript Engines — How Do They Even Work?

children.title: Parsing and AST
children.description: The engine parses your source into an Abstract Syntax Tree before execution — tools like ESLint and Babel operate directly on this tree.

children.title: == vs === and coercion rules
children.description: Loose equality triggers implicit type conversion — knowing the rules prevents classic gotchas like `0 == false` being true.

children.title: Truthy and falsy values
children.description: Only six values are falsy in JS — every other value coerces to true, which drives most conditional shorthand patterns.

children.title: Node.js + nvm setup
children.description: nvm lets you switch Node versions per project — essential when working across codebases that require different Node versions.

children.title: ESLint + Prettier
children.description: ESLint catches bugs and enforces patterns; Prettier handles formatting — using both together eliminates style debates and a class of runtime errors.

children.title: Chrome DevTools Console
children.description: The console is a live JS REPL attached to any page — use it to experiment with APIs and prototype solutions in real time.

children.title: Live Server extension
children.description: Live Server auto-reloads the browser on file save, giving you instant feedback during static JS development without a build step.

javascript.title: Functions, Closures, and Scope
javascript.description: Deeply understand JavaScript function mechanics including closures, the call stack, and how scope chains work. These concepts are the bedrock of advanced JavaScript patterns.

resources.label: You Don't Know JS - Scope and Closures

javascript.challenge: Add a module pattern to the to-do app using closures to encapsulate state, with a factory function that creates isolated to-do lists.

children.title: Call stack and hoisting
children.description: Every function call creates a new execution context pushed onto the call stack — hoisting moves declarations to the top of their context before code runs.

children.title: Lexical environment
children.description: Each scope has a lexical environment that stores variable bindings — closures work by retaining a reference to the environment where they were defined.

children.title: Variable capture
children.description: A closure captures a reference to the variable, not its value — this is why the classic loop-with-setTimeout bug occurs.

children.title: Memory and closures
children.description: Closures keep their enclosing scope alive in memory — accidentally capturing large objects in long-lived closures is a common memory leak pattern.

children.title: IIFE pattern
children.description: Immediately Invoked Function Expressions create a private scope on the spot — useful for encapsulating initialization logic without polluting the global namespace.

children.title: Partial application
children.description: Partial application fixes some arguments of a function and returns a new one — useful for creating specialized helpers from generic functions.

children.title: DevTools Scope panel
children.description: The Scope panel in Chrome DevTools shows every variable in every scope at a breakpoint — essential for understanding exactly what a closure has captured.

children.title: console.trace and groups
children.description: console.trace prints the full call stack at any point; console.group nests related log output — both reduce print-debugging noise.

javascript.title: Objects, Prototypes, and Classes
javascript.description: Understand JavaScript's prototype chain, object creation patterns, and how ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototypal inheritance. Learn modern class features including private fields and static methods.

resources.label: You Don't Know JS - this and Object Prototypes

javascript.challenge: Refactor the to-do app into a class-based design with a TodoList class using private fields for the items array and static factory methods.

children.title: [[Prototype]] linkage
children.description: Every JS object has an internal [[Prototype]] link to another object — property lookups walk this chain until they hit null, enabling inheritance without classes.

children.title: Object.create and delegation
children.description: Object.create(proto) creates an object whose [[Prototype]] is proto — the purest form of prototypal delegation without constructor functions.

children.title: enumerable, writable, configurable
children.description: Property descriptors control whether a property shows in for...in loops, can be reassigned, or deleted — Object.freeze uses these internally.

children.title: Getters and setters
children.description: Accessor properties run functions when read or written — useful for computed properties and validation logic without changing the call site API.

children.title: Factory functions vs classes
children.description: Factory functions return objects without new, avoiding this binding issues — classes offer familiar syntax but introduce complexity around inheritance.

children.title: Mixin pattern
children.description: Mixins copy methods from multiple source objects onto a target — a pragmatic way to compose behaviours without deep class hierarchies.

children.title: Four binding rules
children.description: this is determined at call time by four rules in priority: new, explicit (call/apply/bind), implicit (method call), and default — arrow functions skip all four and inherit lexically.

resources.label: YDKJS — this All Makes Sense Now

children.title: Arrow functions and this
children.description: Arrow functions capture this from their enclosing lexical scope at definition time — ideal for callbacks but wrong for object methods.

javascript.title: Asynchronous JavaScript
javascript.description: Master the asynchronous programming model in JavaScript using callbacks, Promises, and async/await. Understand the event loop and how JavaScript handles concurrency in a single-threaded environment.

resources.label: You Don't Know JS - Async and Performance

javascript.challenge: Add async file persistence to the to-do app using Node.js fs/promises, with error handling for missing files and concurrent read/write safety.

children.title: Call stack and task queue
children.description: The event loop moves callbacks from the task queue to the call stack only when the stack is empty — this single-threaded model is why long synchronous code blocks the UI.

resources.label: What the heck is the event loop? — Philip Roberts

children.title: Microtask queue
children.description: Promises resolve through the microtask queue, which drains completely before the next task runs — giving Promise callbacks higher priority than setTimeout.

children.title: Promise states
children.description: A Promise is pending, fulfilled, or rejected — once settled it never changes, and handlers registered after settlement still fire asynchronously.

children.title: Promise chaining vs nesting
children.description: Returning a value from .then() wraps it in a resolved Promise and passes it forward — flat chaining avoids callback-pyramid nesting.

children.title: async/await over raw promises
children.description: async/await makes asynchronous code read like synchronous code and gives you try/catch for error handling — the underlying mechanics are identical to promise chains.

children.title: Promise.all and Promise.race
children.description: Promise.all runs multiple async operations in parallel and waits for all — Promise.race resolves as soon as any one settles, useful for timeouts.

children.title: try/catch with async/await
children.description: Wrapping await calls in try/catch catches both synchronous throws and rejected promises — unhandled rejections crash Node processes.

children.title: AbortController for cancellation
children.description: AbortController cancels in-flight fetch requests or any operation accepting an AbortSignal — essential for avoiding state updates after component unmount.

javascript.title: DOM Manipulation and Browser APIs
javascript.description: Learn to interact with the browser DOM, handle events, and use Web APIs including fetch, localStorage, and the History API. Build dynamic, interactive web pages without a framework.
javascript.challenge: Build a browser-based UI for the to-do app using vanilla JS and the DOM, persisting data to localStorage and supporting drag-to-reorder.

children.title: Node types and traversal
children.description: The DOM is a tree of Node objects — knowing traversal properties (parentNode, children vs childNodes) avoids bugs when walking the tree.

children.title: Reflow and repaint
children.description: Reading layout properties after writing to the DOM forces a synchronous reflow — batching reads and writes avoids costly layout thrashing.

children.title: Bubbling and capturing
children.description: Events travel down from the root (capture phase) then bubble back up — stopPropagation halts travel while capture listeners fire before bubble listeners.

children.title: Event delegation
children.description: Attach one listener to a parent instead of many to children — this works because events bubble up and is more performant for dynamic lists.

children.title: querySelector and modern selectors
children.description: querySelector/querySelectorAll accept any CSS selector — they replace most getElementById/getElementsByClassName usage.

children.title: IntersectionObserver
children.description: IntersectionObserver fires callbacks when an element enters or leaves the viewport — the right tool for lazy loading images and infinite scroll.

children.title: DocumentFragment for batch inserts
children.description: Building a DocumentFragment off-screen and inserting it once causes a single reflow instead of one per element — critical for large lists.

children.title: requestAnimationFrame
children.description: rAF schedules a callback before the next browser paint — the correct way to drive animations synced to the display refresh rate.

javascript.title: Modern ES2022+ Features
javascript.description: Explore modern JavaScript features from ES2015 through ES2022+ including destructuring, spread, optional chaining, and top-level await. Staying current with the language makes code more expressive and concise.
javascript.challenge: Refactor the entire to-do app codebase to use modern syntax throughout - destructuring, optional chaining, and logical assignment where appropriate.

children.title: Proposal stages 0–4
children.description: TC39 advances features through 5 stages before they land in the spec — understanding stages helps you evaluate whether syntax is stable enough to use without a transpiler.

children.title: Reading the ECMAScript spec
children.description: Reading the spec for a feature reveals edge cases and design decisions that MDN summaries often omit.

resources.label: How to Read the ECMAScript Spec

children.title: Optional chaining internals
children.description: Optional chaining (?.) short-circuits and returns undefined on null/undefined — it evaluates the left side once, avoiding double evaluation.

children.title: Nullish coalescing vs ||
children.description: ?? only falls through on null/undefined while || falls through on any falsy value — this prevents 0 or '' from accidentally triggering defaults.

children.title: Array.at(), Object.hasOwn(), structuredClone()
children.description: These modern APIs replace older verbose patterns — at() for negative indexing, hasOwn() over hasOwnProperty, structuredClone() for deep copying.

children.title: Top-level await
children.description: Top-level await lets ES modules await at module level without wrapping in an async function — useful for dynamic imports and initialization logic.

children.title: Babel config
children.description: Babel transforms modern JS to a target environment's supported syntax — configuring @babel/preset-env with a browserslist target is the standard approach.

children.title: core-js polyfills
children.description: core-js provides runtime polyfills for built-ins that transpilation can't handle — used alongside Babel to patch missing APIs in older environments.

javascript.title: Modules, Tooling, and Node.js Ecosystem
javascript.description: Learn ES Modules and CommonJS, understand the Node.js module resolution algorithm, and get comfortable with the npm ecosystem and basic build tooling. These skills bridge browser and server-side JavaScript.
javascript.challenge: Convert the to-do app to use ES Modules throughout, add an npm script for bundling with Vite, and extract shared utilities into a standalone npm-style module.

children.title: CommonJS vs ESM
children.description: CommonJS (require/module.exports) is synchronous and built for Node; ESM (import/export) is the standard, supports tree shaking, and is now native in Node 12+.

children.title: Circular dependencies
children.description: ESM handles circular imports with partially-evaluated bindings; CommonJS returns the incomplete cached export — circular deps are a design smell worth eliminating.

children.title: Tree shaking
children.description: Tree shaking removes unused exports at bundle time by statically analysing ES module imports — it only works with ESM, not CommonJS.

children.title: Code splitting
children.description: Code splitting breaks a bundle into chunks loaded on demand — dynamic import() is the trigger and bundlers handle the chunk graph automatically.

children.title: Vite
children.description: Vite uses native ESM in dev for instant HMR and Rollup for production builds — the fastest dev server available and the recommended choice for new projects.

children.title: npm scripts workflow
children.description: package.json scripts are the standard way to define dev, build, test, and lint commands — no task runner needed for most projects.

children.title: Semantic versioning
children.description: npm uses semver — ^ allows minor+patch updates, ~ allows patch only — understanding this prevents surprise breaking changes after npm install.

children.title: package-lock.json
children.description: The lockfile pins exact dependency versions including transitive deps — committing it ensures every developer and CI environment installs identical packages.

javascript.title: Error Handling, Debugging, and Testing
javascript.description: Write robust error handling strategies, learn to use browser and Node.js debuggers effectively, and write tests using a modern JavaScript testing framework. Reliable code requires intentional error management and test coverage.
topics: try/catch/finally in sync and async code
javascript.challenge: Write a Vitest suite covering all to-do app logic, mock localStorage in tests, and add a global error boundary that logs uncaught errors to a logfile.

children.title: Error hierarchy
children.description: JS has built-in error subclasses (TypeError, RangeError, SyntaxError) — throwing the appropriate subclass gives callers more information to handle errors correctly.

children.title: Stack traces and source maps
children.description: Source maps map minified production code back to original source — without them, error.stack points to unreadable minified code in production.

children.title: Unit vs integration vs E2E
children.description: Unit tests verify a single function in isolation; integration tests verify modules working together; E2E tests verify full user flows — a healthy project has all three.

children.title: Test doubles
children.description: Stubs return preset values, mocks verify calls were made, spies observe real behaviour without replacing it — knowing the distinction prevents misuse.

children.title: Vitest
children.description: Vitest is Vite-native, uses the same config, and is dramatically faster than Jest for Vite projects — it also has a compatible Jest API so migration is simple.

children.title: Jest
children.description: Jest is the most widely used JS testing framework — it includes a test runner, assertion library, mocking, and coverage out of the box.

children.title: Chrome DevTools breakpoints
children.description: Conditional breakpoints, logpoints, and DOM mutation breakpoints let you pause execution precisely without adding console.log to your code.

children.title: VS Code debugger
children.description: VS Code's built-in debugger attaches to Node.js processes or browsers — a launch.json config gives you full IDE debugging without leaving the editor.

javascript.title: Functional Programming Patterns in JavaScript
javascript.description: Apply functional programming principles to JavaScript including immutability, pure functions, currying, and function composition. These patterns lead to more predictable, testable code.

resources.label: Mostly Adequate Guide to Functional Programming

javascript.challenge: Refactor the to-do app state management using pure functions and immutable updates, composing transformation pipelines for filtering and sorting tasks.

children.title: Pure functions
children.description: A pure function always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects — trivially testable and safe to memoize or parallelize.

children.title: Immutability patterns
children.description: Never mutating data and always producing new objects makes state changes explicit and predictable — Immer makes immutable updates ergonomic.

children.title: compose and pipe
children.description: Compose chains functions right-to-left; pipe left-to-right — both build complex transformations from small reusable functions without intermediate variables.

children.title: Currying
children.description: Currying transforms a function of N arguments into N unary functions — enables partial application and makes function composition more flexible.

children.title: map, filter, reduce
children.description: map/filter/reduce are the FP foundation built into every JS array — chaining them replaces most imperative loops with readable transformation pipelines.

children.title: Transducers for performance
children.description: Chained map/filter creates intermediate arrays — transducers compose transformations into a single pass, which matters for large data sets.

children.title: Ramda
children.description: Ramda is a practical FP library for JS — all functions are auto-curried and data-last, ideal for point-free composition pipelines.

children.title: lodash/fp
children.description: lodash/fp is the auto-curried, immutable, data-last version of lodash — a lower-commitment entry point into FP for teams already using lodash.

javascript.title: Production JavaScript - Performance, Security, and Ecosystem
javascript.description: Learn performance profiling, memory leak detection, security best practices like XSS prevention, and how to navigate the production JavaScript ecosystem. Ship code that is fast, secure, and maintainable at scale.
javascript.challenge: Audit the completed to-do app for XSS vulnerabilities, add a Content Security Policy, profile and optimize the render loop, and add a Web Worker for bulk task processing.

children.title: V8 optimization tips
children.description: V8 optimizes functions with stable object shapes and predictable types — mixing types in arrays or adding properties out of order triggers de-optimization.

children.title: Memory leak patterns
children.description: Common JS leaks: forgotten event listeners, closures retaining large objects, and detached DOM nodes held in variables — Chrome Memory DevTools identifies all three.

children.title: XSS prevention
children.description: Always use textContent instead of innerHTML for user-controlled content, and sanitize any HTML you must render — XSS is the most common client-side vulnerability.

children.title: Content Security Policy
children.description: CSP is an HTTP header that whitelists trusted content sources — the most effective defence against XSS in production applications.

children.title: Sentry error tracking
children.description: Sentry captures unhandled exceptions and promise rejections in production with full stack traces and user context — the standard first step for production observability.

children.title: Core Web Vitals
children.description: CWV (LCP, INP, CLS) are Google's metrics for user experience — measuring them with the web-vitals library exposes performance issues that matter to real users.

children.title: GitHub Actions for JS
children.description: A basic workflow runs lint, tests, and build on every PR — catching issues before merge without any external CI service.

children.title: npm publish workflow
children.description: Using a GitHub Actions release workflow with provenance signing is the modern standard for publishing packages to npm.

typescript.title: TypeScript Setup and Basic Types
typescript.description: Install TypeScript, configure tsconfig.json, and learn the basic type system including primitives, arrays, and union types. Understand how TypeScript compiles to JavaScript and how to run TS in Node.js.
typescript.challenge: Build a typed command-line inventory tracker in TypeScript with strict mode enabled, using interfaces to define item shapes.

children.title: Structural typing
children.description: TypeScript uses structural (duck) typing — if two types have the same shape, they're compatible, regardless of what they're called. This differs from nominal typing in Java/C#.

children.title: Type vs value space
children.description: TypeScript has two worlds — types (erased at runtime) and values (kept at runtime). Confusing them leads to errors like using an interface as a value.

children.title: tsc pipeline
children.description: tsc parses TS → type-checks → emits JS. Understanding each phase helps debug cryptic errors and configure the compiler correctly.

children.title: Type erasure
children.description: All TypeScript type annotations are completely removed at compile time — at runtime, you have plain JavaScript with no type information remaining.

children.title: tsconfig.json essentials
children.description: tsconfig controls strict mode, target JS version, module format, and path aliases — getting this right from the start prevents painful migrations later.

children.title: ts-node for scripts
children.description: ts-node runs TypeScript files directly without a build step — perfect for scripts, CLIs, and quick experimentation.

children.title: VS Code TypeScript integration
children.description: VS Code uses TypeScript's language server for autocomplete, refactoring, and inline errors — enabling strict mode maximises these benefits.

children.title: ESLint for TypeScript
children.description: @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin adds TS-aware lint rules that catch issues tsc doesn't — especially useful for enforcing naming conventions and banning unsafe patterns.

typescript.title: Interfaces, Type Aliases, and Enums
typescript.description: Learn to model data using interfaces and type aliases, understand when to use each, and use enums for fixed sets of named values. These constructs are the backbone of TypeScript domain modeling.
typescript.challenge: Model the inventory tracker domain with interfaces for Item, Category, and Warehouse, using string enums for item status and readonly for immutable fields.

children.title: Declaration merging
children.description: Interfaces can be declared multiple times and TypeScript merges them — type aliases cannot. This is why most library typings use interfaces.

children.title: Extending types
children.description: Interfaces use extends; type aliases use & (intersection). Both compose types, but intersection creates a new merged type while extends enforces compatibility.

children.title: Tagged unions
children.description: Adding a literal type discriminant field to each union member lets TypeScript narrow precisely — the standard pattern for modelling state machines and API responses.

children.title: Enum alternatives
children.description: const enums compile away entirely; string union literals (type Status = 'active' | 'inactive') are often more readable and debuggable than numeric enums.

children.title: Type-safe fetch wrappers
children.description: Wrapping fetch with a generic function that accepts a return type and validates with Zod gives you end-to-end type safety from API to component.

children.title: OpenAPI code generation
children.description: Tools like openapi-typescript generate TypeScript types directly from OpenAPI specs — eliminating manual type maintenance for REST APIs.

children.title: Type-safe config objects
children.description: Typing configuration objects with interfaces catches typos and missing fields at compile time — much better than validating at runtime.

children.title: Readonly and const assertions
children.description: Readonly<T> prevents mutation at the type level; as const infers the narrowest literal types — both make immutable data patterns type-safe.

typescript.title: Functions and Type Narrowing
typescript.description: Type function signatures precisely including overloads and return types, and use type narrowing to safely handle union types at runtime. TypeScript's control-flow analysis makes these patterns highly ergonomic.
topics: Typed function parameters and return types
typescript.challenge: Add typed search and filter functions to the inventory tracker using discriminated unions for search result types and type predicates for item validation.

children.title: TypeScript's narrowing algorithm
children.description: TypeScript tracks which type checks you've done (typeof, instanceof, in) and narrows the type in each branch — this is how type guards work automatically.

children.title: never in exhaustive checks
children.description: Adding a default case that assigns to never ensures you handle every union member — the compiler errors if you add a new variant without updating the switch.

children.title: Overload signatures
children.description: Overload signatures let you declare multiple call signatures for a function — TypeScript picks the right one at the call site based on argument types.

children.title: Type predicate functions
children.description: A function returning 'value is Type' acts as a user-defined type guard — TypeScript narrows the type in the if-branch when you call it.

children.title: instanceof and in operator guards
children.description: instanceof checks narrow class instances; the in operator narrows based on property presence — both work automatically with TypeScript's control flow analysis.

children.title: Assertion functions
children.description: Functions that throw on invalid input can be typed as asserts — TypeScript then treats the value as the asserted type for the rest of the scope.

children.title: Partial and Required
children.description: Partial<T> makes all properties optional; Required<T> makes all mandatory — useful for update payloads and configuration merging patterns.

children.title: Pick and Omit
children.description: Pick<T, Keys> selects a subset of properties; Omit<T, Keys> removes them — together they derive DTO types from domain models without duplication.

typescript.title: Generics
typescript.description: Write reusable, type-safe code using generics in functions, interfaces, and classes. Generics are the most powerful feature of TypeScript's type system for building flexible abstractions.
typescript.challenge: Create a generic Repository<T> class for the inventory tracker that handles CRUD operations with full type safety for any entity type.

children.title: Type parameters and inference
children.description: TypeScript infers generic type parameters from arguments — explicit annotation is only needed when inference produces a too-wide type or you're defining a return-only generic.

children.title: Variance
children.description: A type is covariant if it can be substituted with a subtype — TypeScript's structural typing makes most positions covariant by default, which affects generic containers.

children.title: extends keyword in generics
children.description: T extends SomeType constrains what can be passed as T — TypeScript then knows the shape of T inside the function, enabling property access without casting.

children.title: keyof and indexed access
children.description: keyof T produces a union of T's property names — combined with T[K] (indexed access), you can type functions that read arbitrary properties safely.

children.title: Generic React components
children.description: Typing React components with generic props enables reusable list/table/select components that preserve type information through the component boundary.

children.title: Generic API wrappers
children.description: Wrapping fetch or axios in a generic function that takes a return type gives you full type inference from API call to consuming component.

children.title: Array<T>, Promise<T>, Map<K,V>
children.description: All standard JS built-ins have generic TypeScript types — using them instead of any[] or Promise<any> preserves type safety throughout your codebase.

children.title: Record<K,V>
children.description: Record<string, number> is shorthand for an object whose keys are strings and values are numbers — cleaner than { [key: string]: number } and works with keyof.

typescript.title: Classes and Decorators
typescript.description: Use TypeScript classes with access modifiers, abstract classes, and decorators to build structured, extensible code. Decorators enable metadata-driven patterns common in frameworks like NestJS and Angular.
typescript.challenge: Refactor the inventory tracker using abstract base classes and add a @log decorator that records all repository method calls with timestamps.

children.title: TypeScript vs JS classes
children.description: TypeScript adds access modifiers (private, protected, readonly) and parameter properties — these are erased at runtime and enforced only at compile time.

children.title: Abstract classes
children.description: Abstract classes define a contract that subclasses must implement without being instantiable themselves — the right choice when you need shared implementation plus enforced interface.

children.title: reflect-metadata
children.description: reflect-metadata enables storing and retrieving type metadata at runtime — required by decorator-heavy frameworks like NestJS and TypeORM.

children.title: Decorator factories
children.description: A decorator factory is a function that returns a decorator — this allows decorators to accept configuration arguments like @Column({ nullable: true }).

children.title: NestJS decorators
children.description: NestJS uses TypeScript decorators pervasively — @Injectable(), @Controller(), @Get() — understanding decorator factories helps you write custom NestJS decorators.

children.title: TypeORM entities
children.description: TypeORM uses @Entity(), @Column(), @PrimaryGeneratedColumn() to map classes to database tables — decorators + metadata make the ORM mapping seamless.

children.title: Mixin pattern in TypeScript
children.description: TypeScript's mixin pattern uses generic base classes and type intersections to simulate multiple inheritance without the diamond problem.

children.title: Class decorators for cross-cutting concerns
children.description: Class decorators apply logic (logging, caching, validation) to every method of a class without modifying the class itself — the AOP pattern in TypeScript.

typescript.title: Advanced Types - Mapped, Conditional, and Template Literal
typescript.description: Master TypeScript's advanced type-level programming including mapped types, conditional types, and template literal types. These features let you express complex type transformations that eliminate entire categories of bugs.
topics: Conditional types (T extends U ? X : Y)
typescript.challenge: Build utility types for the inventory tracker: a DeepReadonly<T> type, a PickByValue<T, V> helper, and a typed event emitter using template literal types.

children.title: keyof and in operator
children.description: Mapped types iterate over a union of keys with [K in keyof T] — this powers Partial<T>, Readonly<T>, and any transformation that applies uniformly to all properties.

children.title: Homomorphic mapped types
children.description: A mapped type over keyof T preserves optional/readonly modifiers from the original — non-homomorphic mapped types (over arbitrary unions) don't.

children.title: infer keyword
children.description: infer inside conditional types extracts a type from another type — the mechanism behind ReturnType<T>, Parameters<T>, and many utility types.

children.title: Distributive conditionals
children.description: Conditional types distribute over union members automatically — T extends string ? 'yes' : 'no' applied to 'a' | 1 gives 'yes' | 'no'.

children.title: ReturnType, Parameters, Awaited
children.description: ReturnType<T> extracts a function's return type; Parameters<T> extracts its argument types; Awaited<T> unwraps nested Promises — all use infer internally.

children.title: Template literal types
children.description: Template literal types combine string literal unions — type EventName = `on${Capitalize<string>}` — enabling precise string-pattern typing for event systems.

children.title: Builder pattern with generics
children.description: A fluent builder that accumulates type information as you chain calls — TypeScript tracks what's been set so far and prevents building incomplete objects.

children.title: Branded/nominal types
children.description: Branding a primitive type (type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: 'UserId' }) prevents passing a plain string where a UserId is expected.

typescript.title: Modules, Declaration Files, and tsconfig Mastery
typescript.description: Understand TypeScript module resolution, write declaration files for untyped libraries, and configure tsconfig.json for different build targets and project references. Proper project configuration is critical for large TypeScript codebases.
typescript.challenge: Split the inventory tracker into a multi-package monorepo using TypeScript project references, with separate packages for core types, repository, and CLI.

children.title: Node vs bundler resolution
children.description: TypeScript's moduleResolution: 'bundler' matches how Vite/webpack resolve imports — using the wrong setting causes false errors for valid imports.

children.title: paths mapping
children.description: The paths compiler option maps import aliases to real directories — the TypeScript counterpart to webpack/Vite alias config, required for @ path aliases.

children.title: .d.ts structure
children.description: Declaration files describe the shape of JS code without implementation — used by libraries to ship type information alongside their JS output.

children.title: Ambient modules
children.description: declare module 'some-lib' lets you type a library that ships no types — the minimal version silences errors; a full version gives you autocomplete.

children.title: strict mode flags
children.description: strict enables eight checks at once — the most important are strictNullChecks (no implicit null) and noImplicitAny. Enable all of them from day one.

children.title: target and lib
children.description: target controls the emitted JS version; lib controls which built-in types are available — mismatching them causes type errors for APIs that exist at runtime.

children.title: @types packages
children.description: @types/node, @types/react etc. are community-maintained declaration files on npm — install them as devDependencies to get types for untyped packages.

children.title: Writing your own .d.ts
children.description: When a library has no @types package, writing a minimal .d.ts with only the APIs you use is faster than casting to any throughout your code.

typescript.title: Async TypeScript and Error Handling Patterns
typescript.description: Write async/await code with precise typed error handling, use Result types for explicit error paths, and type complex async workflows. TypeScript makes asynchronous code safer when types are applied correctly.
typescript.challenge: Add an async export feature to the inventory tracker using a Result<T, E> type for error handling, with typed retry logic and cancellation support.

children.title: Promise<T> inference
children.description: async functions always return Promise<T> — TypeScript infers T from your return statement, so you rarely need to annotate the return type explicitly.

children.title: Awaited<T> type
children.description: Awaited<T> recursively unwraps Promise types — useful for extracting the resolved value type from deeply nested or conditional async return types.

children.title: Error vs unknown in catch
children.description: TypeScript 4+ types caught errors as unknown — you must narrow to Error before accessing .message, which forces proper error handling.

children.title: Result/Either pattern
children.description: Wrapping returns in { ok: true, value } | { ok: false, error } makes error handling explicit in the type system, preventing silent failures.

children.title: Typed fetch wrappers
children.description: A generic fetchJson<T>(url) that validates with Zod at runtime and returns T at compile time gives you the best of both worlds — type safety + runtime validation.

children.title: tRPC
children.description: tRPC generates TypeScript types from your server router automatically — zero-config end-to-end type safety without a code generation step or shared types package.

children.title: axios with TypeScript
children.description: Axios's AxiosResponse<T> generic types the response data — combined with Zod validation on the response, you get reliable typed API clients.

children.title: React Query with TypeScript
children.description: TanStack Query's useQuery<TData, TError> generics type loading, error, and data states — combined with Zod parsing on the queryFn, you get full type safety.

typescript.title: Testing TypeScript Code
typescript.description: Write type-safe tests using Vitest or Jest with TypeScript, test type correctness with tsd or expect-type, and use advanced mocking with typed stubs. Testing TypeScript code requires understanding both runtime and compile-time correctness.
typescript.challenge: Write a comprehensive Vitest suite for the inventory tracker including type tests for all generic utilities, mocked repository tests, and a CI workflow on GitHub Actions.

children.title: tsd — type assertions in tests
children.description: tsd lets you write type-level assertions (expectType, expectError) as part of your test suite — essential for testing utility types and type inference.

children.title: expect-type
children.description: expect-type provides Jest-compatible type assertions that fail the test if types don't match — the most ergonomic way to test types alongside runtime tests.

children.title: Partial mocks with Partial<T>
children.description: Using Partial<T> for mock objects lets you specify only the properties relevant to a test — avoiding brittle full-object mocks that break when types change.

children.title: jest.mocked helper
children.description: jest.mocked(fn) types a mocked function with jest's mock type — eliminating casts when accessing .mockReturnValue or .mock.calls on typed functions.

children.title: Vitest + TypeScript config
children.description: Vitest works natively with TypeScript via esbuild — add a vitest.config.ts and it picks up your tsconfig paths and aliases without additional setup.

children.title: Jest with ts-jest
children.description: ts-jest transforms TypeScript files for Jest using the TypeScript compiler — ensures your tests and source use identical type resolution.

children.title: Typed test factories
children.description: A typed factory function that accepts Partial<T> and fills defaults creates test fixtures without duplication and stays in sync with type changes.

children.title: Mock Service Worker
children.description: MSW intercepts real network requests at the network level — type-safe handlers replace fetch mocking and work identically in tests and the browser.

typescript.title: Production TypeScript - Build Pipelines, Performance, and Ecosystem
typescript.description: Ship production TypeScript with optimized build pipelines, understand compiler performance tuning, and navigate the TypeScript ecosystem including popular frameworks and toolchains. Mature TypeScript projects require intentional configuration and build strategy.
topics: esbuild and swc for fast builds
typescript.challenge: Publish the inventory tracker core package to a local npm registry with proper declaration files, an optimized esbuild pipeline, and a migration guide doc for JS-to-TS onboarding.

children.title: Project references
children.description: TypeScript project references split a monorepo into sub-projects that compile independently — incremental compilation only rebuilds changed projects.

children.title: Incremental compilation
children.description: composite: true + incremental: true writes a .tsbuildinfo file — subsequent builds only recheck files that changed, dramatically cutting CI type-check time.

children.title: Zod schemas
children.description: Zod validates data at runtime and infers TypeScript types from schemas — the standard way to parse API responses, env variables, and form data safely.

children.title: io-ts
children.description: io-ts uses the type system itself to define codecs that validate and decode data — a more functional approach to runtime validation with full type inference.

children.title: TypeScript project references in monorepos
children.description: Each package in a monorepo gets its own tsconfig with references to its dependencies — tsc --build then handles the correct build order automatically.

children.title: Turborepo + TypeScript
children.description: Turborepo caches TypeScript build outputs across the monorepo — combined with project references, type checking a large monorepo becomes fast.

children.title: tsc --noEmit in CI
children.description: Running tsc --noEmit as a CI step catches type errors without producing output files — faster than a full build and the right gate before merging PRs.

children.title: Type coverage tools
children.description: type-coverage measures what percentage of your code is typed vs any — useful for tracking progress when migrating a JS codebase to TypeScript.

go.title: Go Syntax and Toolchain
go.description: Set up the Go toolchain, understand the module system, and learn core syntax including variables, types, loops, and functions. Go's simplicity is intentional - the language is small and easy to hold in your head.
topics: go mod init and module structure
go.challenge: Build a command-line URL shortener in Go that stores mappings in memory and supports add, lookup, and list commands.

children.title: Simplicity over features
children.description: Go deliberately omits generics (until 1.18), exceptions, and inheritance — forcing explicit, readable code over clever abstractions.

children.title: Compilation model
children.description: Go compiles to a single static binary with no runtime dependencies — fast compilation and trivial deployment are first-class design goals.

children.title: Static typing and zero values
children.description: Every Go variable has a zero value (0, '', false, nil) — no undefined, no null pointer on uninitialized struct fields, which eliminates a whole class of bugs.

children.title: Type inference with :=
children.description: The := short variable declaration infers the type from the right-hand side — reducing verbosity without sacrificing static typing safety.

children.title: go tool commands
children.description: go build, go run, go test, go fmt, and go vet are all you need for most tasks — one unified tool with no external task runner required.

children.title: gopls language server
children.description: gopls provides autocomplete, go-to-definition, and refactoring in any LSP-compatible editor — install it once and it works everywhere.

children.title: go mod init
children.description: go mod init module/path initializes a module with a go.mod file — the module path is the import path prefix for all packages in the project.

children.title: Standard workspace layout
children.description: Go projects conventionally put cmd/ for executables and internal/ for private packages — internal/ is enforced by the compiler to prevent external imports.

go.title: Arrays, Slices, Maps, and Structs
go.description: Master Go's composite types - slices, maps, and structs - and understand the memory model behind them including capacity and append behavior. These types are the foundation of all Go data modeling.

resources.label: Go by Example - Structs and Maps

go.challenge: Refactor the URL shortener to use a struct for the store, add metadata fields (created_at, hit_count) and implement a slice-based history log.

children.title: Length vs capacity
children.description: A slice has a pointer, length, and capacity — append grows within capacity without allocation; exceeding it allocates a new backing array and copies.

children.title: Underlying array sharing
children.description: Slicing a slice creates a new header pointing into the same backing array — mutations through one slice affect the other until an append triggers a copy.

children.title: Hash map implementation
children.description: Go maps are hash tables with O(1) average lookup — the zero value of a map is nil (not empty), and writing to a nil map panics.

resources.label: Go Blog — Go maps in action

children.title: Map concurrency
children.description: Go maps are not safe for concurrent reads+writes — use sync.RWMutex or sync.Map when accessing a map from multiple goroutines.

children.title: append patterns
children.description: Common patterns: append(s, x), append(s, other...), and the copy idiom for removing an element — all avoid re-allocating by pre-computing capacity.

children.title: Struct embedding
children.description: Embedding a struct promotes its methods to the outer type — Go's composition mechanism that replaces inheritance without the complexity of class hierarchies.

children.title: encoding/json and struct tags
children.description: json:"field_name" tags control JSON key names; omitempty skips zero-value fields — no external library needed for most JSON work.

children.title: Custom marshaling
children.description: Implementing json.Marshaler and json.Unmarshaler interfaces gives full control over JSON serialization for types that don't map cleanly to JSON primitives.

go.title: Interfaces and Methods
go.description: Learn Go's implicit interface satisfaction model, method sets, and how to use interfaces for polymorphism and testability. Go interfaces are small and composable - the standard library demonstrates this with io.Reader and io.Writer.

resources.label: The Go Programming Language - Interfaces Chapter

go.challenge: Refactor the URL shortener storage behind a Store interface, implement an in-memory and a file-backed version, and swap them at startup via a flag.

children.title: Structural typing in Go
children.description: A type implements an interface by having the right methods — no explicit implements keyword. This decouples interface definitions from implementations across packages.

children.title: Interface internals — nil pitfall
children.description: Interface values are (type, value) pairs — an interface holding a nil pointer is not nil itself. This is the source of the 'nil interface' bug many Go beginners hit.

children.title: Pointer vs value receivers
children.description: Value receivers work on copies; pointer receivers modify the original. A type's method set with pointer receivers is a superset — you can call pointer methods on addressable values.

children.title: Interface embedding
children.description: Interfaces can embed other interfaces to compose them — io.ReadWriter embeds io.Reader and io.Writer, giving a combined contract in one type.

children.title: io.Reader and io.Writer
children.description: Most Go I/O is built around these two interfaces — any type that reads bytes implements io.Reader, enabling composable pipelines like gzip.NewReader(file).

children.title: fmt.Stringer
children.description: Implementing the Stringer interface (String() string) gives your type a readable representation in fmt.Println — the Go equivalent of toString().

children.title: Small interfaces
children.description: Prefer single-method or two-method interfaces — io.Reader, io.Writer, error. Small interfaces are easier to implement, mock, and compose.

children.title: Mocking with interfaces
children.description: Defining your dependencies as interfaces lets you swap in test doubles — the standard Go approach to testable code without mocking frameworks.

go.title: Error Handling
go.description: Embrace Go's explicit error handling model, define custom error types, and learn idiomatic patterns like wrapping and sentinel errors. Go's approach to errors is deliberate - errors are values, not exceptions.
go.challenge: Add comprehensive error types to the URL shortener (NotFoundError, DuplicateError, ValidationError) with full wrapping, sentinel checks, and user-friendly messages.

children.title: Errors as values
children.description: In Go, errors are just values that implement the error interface — returning them from functions makes error handling explicit at every call site.

children.title: Sentinel errors
children.description: Sentinel errors are package-level error variables (var ErrNotFound = errors.New(...)) that callers compare against — the Go equivalent of typed exception classes.

children.title: fmt.Errorf %w for wrapping
children.description: fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err) wraps an error with context while preserving the original — unwrap the chain with errors.Is or errors.As.

children.title: errors.Is and errors.As
children.description: errors.Is checks if any error in the chain matches a target; errors.As extracts a specific error type from the chain — both unwrap automatically.

children.title: Custom error types
children.description: Implementing the error interface on a struct lets you attach structured data (HTTP status code, field name) that callers can extract with errors.As.

children.title: Panic vs error
children.description: Use errors for expected failure cases; panic for programming errors and invariant violations — recover() catches panics in a deferred function.

children.title: Error handling in HTTP handlers
children.description: A common pattern wraps http.HandlerFunc to return an error, with middleware that maps errors to HTTP status codes — keeping handlers clean.

children.title: errors.Join — Go 1.20
children.description: errors.Join combines multiple errors into one — useful when a function encounters several independent failures that should all be reported.

go.title: Goroutines and Channels
go.description: Harness Go's concurrency primitives - goroutines and channels - to write concurrent programs that are safe and expressive. Go's CSP model encourages sharing memory by communicating rather than communicating by sharing memory.
go.challenge: Add a concurrent analytics pipeline to the URL shortener that processes click events through a channel and aggregates stats in a background goroutine.

children.title: M:N threading model
children.description: Go's scheduler multiplexes M goroutines over N OS threads — thousands of goroutines can run concurrently with minimal overhead compared to OS threads.

children.title: GOMAXPROCS
children.description: GOMAXPROCS controls how many OS threads run Go code simultaneously — defaults to the number of CPUs, which is usually optimal.

children.title: Buffered vs unbuffered
children.description: Unbuffered channels synchronize sender and receiver; buffered channels decouple them up to the buffer size — choosing correctly avoids deadlocks.

resources.label: The Nature of Channels in Go

children.title: Channel direction in types
children.description: chan<- T (send-only) and <-chan T (receive-only) in function signatures make data flow explicit and prevent misuse at compile time.

children.title: Fan-out and fan-in
children.description: Fan-out distributes work across multiple goroutines; fan-in merges their results through a shared channel — the foundation of Go's pipeline pattern.

children.title: Worker pools
children.description: A fixed-size pool of goroutines reading from a jobs channel limits concurrency and resource usage — the standard pattern for CPU-bound parallel work.

children.title: sync.WaitGroup
children.description: WaitGroup.Add(n) before launching goroutines and Done() in each one lets the main goroutine wait with Wait() — the standard pattern for parallel workloads.

children.title: sync.Mutex
children.description: Mutex protects shared state from concurrent access — Lock() before reading/writing and Unlock() after, ideally deferred to prevent forgetting.

go.title: The Standard Library
go.description: Explore Go's rich standard library covering HTTP, JSON, file I/O, time, and strings. Go's stdlib is deliberately comprehensive so you can build production-quality programs with minimal external dependencies.
go.challenge: Expose the URL shortener as an HTTP API using only net/http, with JSON request/response bodies, proper status codes, and structured logging.

children.title: Batteries included design
children.description: Go's standard library covers HTTP, JSON, crypto, templating, and testing without external dependencies — reducing supply-chain risk and keeping binaries small.

children.title: Interface-driven design
children.description: The stdlib uses small interfaces (io.Reader, http.Handler, sort.Interface) throughout — your code composes naturally with stdlib by implementing these.

children.title: Handler interface
children.description: http.Handler is just ServeHTTP(ResponseWriter, *Request) — any type implementing it can be mounted on a server, making middleware trivial to compose.

children.title: ServeMux routing
children.description: Go 1.22 added method+path patterns to ServeMux — more expressive routing without external libraries like gorilla/mux.

children.title: context package
children.description: context.Context carries deadlines, cancellations, and request-scoped values across API boundaries — pass it as the first argument to any function that does I/O.

children.title: time package
children.description: time.Duration, time.Now(), and time.After() are the building blocks for timeouts, rate limiting, and scheduled work — all with nanosecond precision.

children.title: bufio for buffered I/O
children.description: bufio.Scanner and bufio.Writer add buffering to any io.Reader/Writer — critical for line-by-line file reading and reducing syscall overhead.

children.title: io utilities
children.description: io.TeeReader, io.MultiWriter, and io.LimitReader compose readers and writers without copying data — the plumbing of Go's I/O model.

go.title: Testing in Go
go.description: Write table-driven tests, benchmarks, and examples using Go's built-in testing package, and use interfaces for testability. Go's testing tooling is built into the language - no external framework is needed.
go.challenge: Write a full table-driven test suite for the URL shortener including HTTP handler tests using httptest, benchmarks for the store, and coverage above 85%.

children.title: Table-driven tests
children.description: Table-driven tests define input/expected pairs in a slice and loop — adding a test case is one line, and a failing case names itself via the test name.

children.title: Tests as documentation
children.description: Go test names describe behaviour — TestUserService_CreateUser_ReturnsErrOnDuplicate reads like a spec. Readable test names replace comments.

children.title: testing.B and go test -bench
children.description: Functions starting with Benchmark receive testing.B — run with go test -bench=. to measure nanoseconds per operation and allocations.

resources.label: How to write benchmarks in Go

children.title: pprof profiling
children.description: go test -cpuprofile generates a CPU profile you visualise with go tool pprof — the standard way to identify hot spots before optimising.

children.title: testify/assert
children.description: testify/assert provides expressive assertions (assert.Equal, assert.NoError) that print clear diffs on failure — the most popular Go test helper library.

children.title: httptest package
children.description: httptest.NewRecorder() captures HTTP responses in tests; httptest.NewServer() starts a real test HTTP server — both are in the stdlib with no setup needed.

children.title: Subtests with t.Run
children.description: t.Run(name, func) creates named subtests that can be run selectively with -run — table-driven tests use t.Run to give each case its own name and failure output.

children.title: testdata/ directory
children.description: The testdata/ directory is reserved for test input files and is excluded from builds — the convention for storing JSON fixtures, golden files, and test configs.

go.title: Go Modules and Dependency Management
go.description: Manage Go module dependencies with go.mod and go.sum, understand versioning semantics, and use popular third-party packages responsibly. The Go module system provides reproducible builds by design.
topics: go get, go tidy, go vendor
go.challenge: Add a third-party router (chi or gorilla/mux) and a structured logger (zerolog) to the URL shortener, managing versions with go.mod and tidying dependencies.

children.title: go.mod and go.sum
children.description: go.mod declares the module path and dependencies; go.sum records cryptographic hashes — both must be committed and go.sum prevents supply-chain attacks.

children.title: Semantic import versioning
children.description: Go requires major version suffixes (/v2) in import paths for v2+ modules — this makes it possible to use multiple major versions simultaneously.

children.title: GOPROXY
children.description: GOPROXY (default: proxy.golang.org) caches module versions — this ensures reproducible builds even if the original source disappears from VCS.

children.title: Module cache
children.description: Downloaded modules are stored in $GOPATH/pkg/mod — go clean -modcache clears it; GOFLAGS=-mod=vendor forces vendor mode.

children.title: go mod tidy
children.description: go mod tidy adds missing and removes unused dependencies from go.mod and go.sum — run it before committing after adding or removing imports.

children.title: go mod vendor
children.description: go mod vendor copies all dependencies into a vendor/ directory — useful for air-gapped builds and ensuring no network access is needed in CI.

children.title: GONOSUMCHECK
children.description: Set GONOSUMCHECK=*.internal.corp to skip checksum verification for private modules not in the public sum database.

children.title: GOFLAGS for persistent flags
children.description: GOFLAGS sets default flags for all go commands — useful for consistently enabling -mod=vendor or build tags across a project.

go.title: Advanced Go Patterns - Generics, Context, and sync
go.description: Use Go generics (added in 1.18) for reusable data structures, propagate cancellation with context.Context, and synchronize goroutines safely with the sync package. These are the tools of idiomatic production Go.
