go.challenge: Add a generic cache layer to the URL shortener with configurable TTL using context for cancellation, and protect concurrent access with sync.RWMutex.

children.title: Type parameters in Go 1.18+
children.description: Go generics use type parameters with constraints — func Map[T, U any](s []T, f func(T) U) []U replaces repetitive slice transformation functions.

children.title: Type constraints
children.description: Constraints are interfaces that restrict what types can be used as type parameters — the comparable constraint enables == in generic functions.

children.title: Context propagation
children.description: Pass context.Context as the first argument through every function that does I/O — this lets callers cancel in-flight operations across goroutine boundaries.

children.title: Context values — use sparingly
children.description: context.WithValue stores request-scoped values (trace IDs, auth tokens) — never use it for required function inputs that belong in the function signature.

children.title: sync.Once
children.description: sync.Once guarantees a function runs exactly once across all goroutines — the standard pattern for lazy initialisation of expensive resources.

children.title: sync.Pool
children.description: sync.Pool recycles temporary objects to reduce GC pressure — used extensively in encoding/json and net/http internally for buffer reuse.

children.title: Functional options pattern
children.description: Accept ...Option variadic arguments to configure a struct — each Option is a function that mutates a config struct, avoiding boolean parameter proliferation.

children.title: sync.Map for concurrent access
children.description: sync.Map is optimised for write-once, read-many workloads — use it when you know the access pattern; otherwise a plain map+mutex is clearer.

go.title: Production Go - Build, Deploy, and Ecosystem
go.description: Build production Go binaries with cross-compilation, profiling, and embed, and understand the broader Go ecosystem for web, CLI, and systems programming. Go's single-binary deployment model is one of its greatest production advantages.
topics: Go ecosystem overview (Gin, Echo, GORM, Cobra)

resources.label: 100 Go Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

go.challenge: Package the URL shortener as a cross-compiled binary with embedded static assets, a Dockerfile for deployment, graceful shutdown, and pprof endpoints for profiling.

children.title: go build flags
children.description: go build -ldflags='-X main.Version=1.0' injects version info at link time; -trimpath removes local paths from stack traces — both are standard for production binaries.

children.title: CGO_ENABLED=0 for static binaries
children.description: CGO_ENABLED=0 produces a fully static binary with no libc dependency — required for scratch Docker images and Alpine Linux deployments.

children.title: pprof CPU and memory profiling
children.description: go tool pprof visualises CPU and memory profiles from running applications — attach to a live server with net/http/pprof for production profiling.

children.title: Escape analysis
children.description: go build -gcflags='-m' prints which variables escape to the heap — heap allocations are GC pressure; keeping hot data on the stack improves throughput.

children.title: Multi-stage Docker builds
children.description: FROM golang:1.22 as builder then FROM scratch COPY binary produces images under 20MB with no shell, OS utilities, or attack surface.

children.title: Cross-compilation
children.description: GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build cross-compiles to any platform from any platform — no build server needed for different targets.

children.title: OpenTelemetry for Go
children.description: OpenTelemetry provides standardised tracing and metrics APIs — instrument once and export to Jaeger, Prometheus, or any OTEL-compatible backend.

children.title: Prometheus metrics
children.description: github.com/prometheus/client_golang provides counters, gauges, and histograms — expose them on /metrics and Prometheus scrapes them automatically.

rust.title: Rust Setup and Basic Syntax
rust.description: Install Rust via rustup, understand Cargo as the build system and package manager, and learn Rust basic syntax. Rust's compiler is unusually helpful - learning to read its error messages is a core skill.
rust.challenge: Build a Cargo project with a CLI task manager that can add and list tasks, reading compiler errors carefully to understand Rust's rules from the start.

children.title: Memory safety without GC
children.description: Rust guarantees memory safety at compile time through the ownership system — no garbage collector, no runtime overhead, and no null pointer dereferences or data races by design.

children.title: Zero-cost abstractions
children.description: Rust's iterators, generics, and traits compile to the same machine code as hand-written loops — you pay no runtime cost for the abstraction layer.

children.title: Static typing and type inference
children.description: Rust infers types throughout a function body — you rarely need to annotate locals, but function signatures require explicit types, which serves as documentation.

children.title: Immutability by default
children.description: Variables in Rust are immutable by default — you must explicitly declare mut to allow mutation. This forces deliberate thinking about which data needs to change.

children.title: rustup and toolchain management
children.description: rustup installs and manages Rust toolchains — switch between stable, beta, and nightly per project using a rust-toolchain.toml file.

children.title: Cargo — build system and package manager
children.description: Cargo is Rust's all-in-one tool — cargo new, cargo build, cargo test, cargo run, and cargo publish handle the entire development lifecycle.

children.title: rust-analyzer
children.description: rust-analyzer is the official Rust language server — provides autocomplete, inline type hints, go-to-definition, and refactoring in any LSP-compatible editor.

children.title: Clippy and rustfmt
children.description: clippy is a linter with 600+ idiomatic Rust checks (cargo clippy); rustfmt formats code to the community standard (cargo fmt) — run both in CI.

rust.title: Ownership, Borrowing, and Lifetimes
rust.description: Internalize Rust's ownership system - the most unique and important feature of the language - including moves, borrows, and lifetime annotations. Ownership is what makes Rust memory-safe without a garbage collector.
rust.challenge: Refactor the task manager to pass tasks by reference through helper functions, adding lifetime annotations where necessary and eliminating all unnecessary clones.

children.title: Move semantics by default
children.description: Assigning or passing a value transfers ownership — the original binding is invalidated. This is different from C++ move semantics which are opt-in; in Rust, move is the default.

children.title: Drop and destructor semantics
children.description: When a value goes out of scope, Rust automatically calls drop() — no manual memory management needed, and the Drop trait lets you customize cleanup logic.

children.title: Shared vs exclusive references
children.description: &T is a shared (immutable) reference — many can exist at once. &mut T is an exclusive (mutable) reference — only one can exist at a time. This enforces the aliasing XOR mutation rule at compile time.

children.title: Lifetime annotations
children.description: Lifetimes annotate how long references are valid — the compiler checks that no reference outlives its referent. Most lifetimes are inferred; explicit annotations are only needed when the compiler can't figure it out.

children.title: Clone vs Copy
children.description: Types implementing Copy are duplicated on assignment instead of moved (integers, bools, char) — Clone requires an explicit .clone() call for heap-allocated types like String and Vec.

children.title: Rc and RefCell for shared ownership
children.description: Rc<T> enables multiple owners with reference counting (single-threaded); RefCell<T> moves borrow checking to runtime — use sparingly when the ownership model is genuinely complex.

children.title: Restructuring to satisfy borrows
children.description: Most borrow checker errors are design feedback — splitting a struct, shortening a borrow's scope, or cloning a small value usually resolves them without unsafe.

children.title: The 'static lifetime
children.description: 'static means a reference is valid for the entire program — String literals are 'static. It's often required by thread-spawning APIs since threads may outlive the creating scope.

rust.title: Structs, Enums, and Pattern Matching
rust.description: Model domains using Rust structs and enums, and use pattern matching to exhaustively handle variants. Rust enums are algebraic data types - far more powerful than enums in most languages.

resources.label: The Rust Book - Enums and Pattern Matching

rust.challenge: Refactor the task manager to use a Task enum with variants (Todo, InProgress, Done) each carrying relevant data, and use match everywhere for status transitions.

children.title: Sum types with enum
children.description: Rust enums are algebraic sum types — each variant can hold different data. Option<T> and Result<T,E> are built-in enums that replace null and exceptions respectively.

children.title: Product types with struct
children.description: Structs are product types — they hold all their fields simultaneously. Tuple structs (struct Point(f64, f64)) and unit structs (struct Marker) are lightweight variants.

children.title: Exhaustive matching
children.description: Rust's match must handle every possible variant — the compiler errors if you miss a case. This makes adding new enum variants a safe refactoring: every match site must be updated.

children.title: Destructuring and binding
children.description: Patterns can destructure structs, tuples, and enum variants in place — let (x, y) = point; and if let Ok(value) = result are idiomatic Rust for concise data extraction.

children.title: Option<T> replacing null
children.description: Option<T> is either Some(value) or None — the compiler forces you to handle both cases, making null pointer dereferences impossible in safe Rust.

children.title: Result<T,E> for error handling
children.description: Result<T,E> is either Ok(value) or Err(error) — functions that can fail return Result, and the ? operator propagates errors up the call stack automatically.

children.title: Builder pattern in Rust
children.description: Builder structs with method chaining and a final .build() method are idiomatic Rust for constructing complex types — widely used in libraries like reqwest and tokio.

children.title: Newtype pattern
children.description: Wrapping a primitive in a single-field tuple struct (struct Meters(f64)) creates a distinct type — preventing mixing up values of the same underlying type and enabling custom trait implementations.

rust.title: Collections, Iterators, and Closures
rust.description: Use Vec, HashMap, and HashSet from the standard library, and master Rust's powerful iterator adapters combined with closures. Rust iterators are lazy and zero-cost - using them is idiomatic and efficient.
topics: Iterator trait and adapters (map, filter, fold)
rust.challenge: Add filtering, sorting, and grouping features to the task manager using iterator adapters, storing tasks in a HashMap keyed by project name.

children.title: Iterator trait and lazy evaluation
children.description: The Iterator trait requires only next() — all other methods (map, filter, take) are provided by default. Iterators are lazy and do nothing until consumed.

children.title: Into and from iterators
children.description: IntoIterator enables for loops on any type; FromIterator enables .collect() — implementing both makes your custom collection work seamlessly with the iterator ecosystem.

children.title: Fn, FnMut, FnOnce
children.description: Closures implement one or more of these traits based on how they capture variables — Fn for shared reference, FnMut for mutable reference, FnOnce for ownership. The compiler infers the right one.

children.title: move closures
children.description: move forces a closure to take ownership of captured variables — required when passing closures to threads that may outlive the creating scope.

children.title: Chaining iterator adapters
children.description: map, filter, flat_map, take, skip, zip, enumerate — chaining adapters builds a lazy transformation pipeline that compiles to the same code as a hand-written loop.

children.title: collect() into different types
children.description: .collect::<Vec<_>>() materialises an iterator into a Vec; .collect::<HashMap<_,_>>() into a map; .collect::<Result<Vec<_>,_>>() short-circuits on the first error.

children.title: Vec<T>, HashMap<K,V>, HashSet<T>
children.description: Vec is the go-to sequential collection; HashMap provides O(1) average lookup; HashSet tracks unique values — all in std::collections with no external dependencies.

children.title: Entry API for maps
children.description: map.entry(key).or_insert(default) inserts a default value only if the key is absent — avoiding a double lookup compared to checking contains_key then inserting.

rust.title: Traits and Generics
rust.description: Define and implement traits to share behavior across types, and write generic functions and structs constrained by trait bounds. Rust traits are the foundation of zero-cost abstraction.
rust.challenge: Add a generic Storage<T> trait to the task manager with in-memory and file-backed implementations, and a Display impl for pretty-printing all task types.

children.title: Traits as interfaces
children.description: Traits define shared behavior — implementing a trait for a type is explicit and opt-in, unlike structural typing. The orphan rule prevents implementing foreign traits on foreign types.

children.title: Dynamic dispatch with dyn Trait
children.description: Box<dyn Trait> enables runtime polymorphism through a vtable — use it when you need to store heterogeneous types in a collection. The size of dyn Trait objects is known (two pointers).

resources.label: dyn vs impl Trait in Rust

children.title: Static dispatch with impl Trait
children.description: impl Trait in function arguments means the compiler monomorphizes for each concrete type — zero-overhead abstraction with no vtable, but generates separate code per type.

children.title: Where clauses
children.description: where T: Display + Clone is equivalent to the angle bracket syntax but more readable for complex multi-bound generics — required when bounds reference associated types.

children.title: Display, Debug, and From/Into
children.description: Display formats for users ({}); Debug for developers ({:?}). From<T> and Into<T> are reciprocal conversion traits — implement From and you get Into for free.

children.title: PartialEq, Eq, Hash, Ord
children.description: Derive these with #[derive()] for most types — PartialEq enables ==, Hash enables use as HashMap keys, and Ord enables sorting. PartialEq and PartialOrd handle NaN-containing types.

children.title: Extension traits
children.description: Define a trait with new methods and implement it for existing types — adding methods to Vec<T> or &str without modifying the original type, the Rust equivalent of extension methods.

children.title: Associated types vs generics
children.description: Associated types (type Output) bind a single concrete type per implementation; generics allow multiple implementations for the same trait with different type parameters.

rust.title: Error Handling with Result and the ? Operator
rust.description: Write idiomatic Rust error handling using Result<T, E>, the ? operator for propagation, and custom error types. The thiserror and anyhow crates make production error handling ergonomic.
rust.challenge: Add comprehensive error handling to the task manager using thiserror for a library error type and anyhow in main, propagating all I/O errors with context.

children.title: Recoverable vs unrecoverable errors
children.description: Result<T,E> handles recoverable errors (file not found, parse failure); panic! handles unrecoverable programming errors (index out of bounds, logic invariant violation). The distinction drives API design.

children.title: The ? operator desugaring
children.description: expr? desugars to match expr { Ok(v) => v, Err(e) => return Err(e.into()) } — the .into() call converts the error type, enabling ? to work across different error types.

children.title: thiserror for library errors
children.description: thiserror derives the Error trait implementation with #[derive(Error)] and #[error("..")] — the standard for defining custom error types in libraries without boilerplate.

children.title: anyhow for application errors
children.description: anyhow::Result<T> is a boxed error type with context — ideal for application code where you want to propagate any error with additional context rather than defining custom error types.

children.title: Chaining context with anyhow
children.description: .context("failed to read config") wraps an error with a human-readable message — when an error propagates up, each level adds context, producing a readable error chain.

children.title: Converting between error types
children.description: impl From<IoError> for MyError lets ? convert automatically — or use #[from] with thiserror to derive the From implementation.

children.title: std::panic::catch_unwind
children.description: catch_unwind recovers from panics in specific scopes — used in test frameworks and FFI boundaries to prevent panics from crossing into C code.

children.title: unwrap() and expect() in production
children.description: unwrap() panics on None/Err — use expect("descriptive message") instead so panics have context. Reserve both for cases that are genuinely impossible to fail.

rust.title: Modules, Crates, and Cargo Deep Dive
rust.description: Organize Rust code into modules and crates, manage visibility, and use Cargo workspaces for multi-crate projects. Understanding the module system is essential for building maintainable Rust projects.
rust.challenge: Restructure the task manager as a Cargo workspace with a core library crate and a CLI crate, exposing a clean public API from the library.

children.title: Module tree and file layout
children.description: Rust's module tree mirrors the file system — mod foo; in main.rs loads src/foo.rs or src/foo/mod.rs. pub controls visibility at each level of the tree.

children.title: pub(crate) and pub(super)
children.description: pub(crate) makes an item visible within the crate but not to external consumers; pub(super) exposes to the parent module only — fine-grained visibility control without making things fully public.

children.title: Cargo.toml features and optional deps
children.description: Features enable conditional compilation — cargo build --features "async" activates feature-gated code. Optional dependencies become features automatically.

children.title: Workspaces for multi-crate projects
children.description: A Cargo workspace groups multiple crates under one Cargo.lock — shared dependency versions, unified build output, and cross-crate cargo test all in one directory.

children.title: cargo check vs cargo build
children.description: cargo check runs the borrow checker and type checker without producing a binary — 3-5x faster than a full build, ideal for the inner development loop.

children.title: cargo-watch for auto-rebuild
children.description: cargo-watch runs cargo check (or any command) on file save — the Rust equivalent of nodemon, giving instant compiler feedback without manual re-runs.

children.title: docs.rs for crate documentation
children.description: Every crate published to crates.io gets documentation auto-built on docs.rs — the central documentation hub for the entire Rust ecosystem.

children.title: cargo audit for security
children.description: cargo audit checks your dependencies against the RustSec advisory database — run it in CI to catch known vulnerabilities in third-party crates.

rust.title: Lifetimes Advanced and Smart Pointers
rust.description: Go beyond basic lifetimes into advanced scenarios involving structs with references, and learn smart pointers (Box, Rc, RefCell, Arc) for heap allocation and shared ownership. These tools unlock patterns that simple borrowing cannot express.
rust.challenge: Add a shared configuration system to the task manager using Arc<RwLock<Config>> so multiple components can read config concurrently with safe mutation.

children.title: Lifetime elision rules
children.description: The compiler infers lifetimes in most cases using three elision rules — explicit annotations are only needed when the rules don't uniquely determine the output lifetime.

children.title: Lifetime subtyping
children.description: 'a: 'b means 'a outlives 'b — lifetime subtyping expresses constraints between lifetimes, required in complex data structures where references have hierarchical relationships.

children.title: Box<T> for heap allocation
children.description: Box<T> allocates T on the heap and owns it — the simplest smart pointer, used for recursive types (where the size is unknown at compile time) and large values.

children.title: Arc<T> and Mutex<T> for shared mutable state
children.description: Arc<T> provides thread-safe reference counting; Mutex<T> provides interior mutability. Arc<Mutex<T>> is the standard pattern for shared mutable state across threads.

children.title: Cow<T> for clone-on-write
children.description: Cow<'a, T> (Clone on Write) holds either a borrowed reference or owned value — avoids cloning when read-only access is sufficient, allocating only when mutation is needed.

children.title: Pin<T> for self-referential types
children.description: Pin<T> prevents a value from being moved in memory — required for async state machines and self-referential structs where internal pointers must remain valid.

children.title: Cell<T> and RefCell<T>
children.description: Cell<T> enables mutation through a shared reference for Copy types; RefCell<T> does the same for non-Copy types with runtime borrow checking — panics instead of compile errors.

children.title: OnceCell and LazyLock
children.description: OnceCell<T> initializes a value exactly once without locking; LazyLock (Rust 1.80+) combines a OnceCell with a lazy initializer — the safe replacement for static mut.

rust.title: Concurrency and Async Rust
rust.description: Use Rust threads and message-passing channels for parallelism, and learn async/await with the Tokio runtime for high-performance I/O. Rust guarantees thread safety at compile time - the compiler prevents data races.
topics: Mutex<T> and Arc for shared state
rust.challenge: Add an async sync feature to the task manager using Tokio that fetches remote task updates from an HTTP endpoint concurrently with local operations.

children.title: Send and Sync traits
children.description: Send means a type can be transferred across thread boundaries; Sync means it can be shared by reference. The compiler enforces these automatically — data races are impossible in safe Rust.

children.title: Fearless concurrency
children.description: The ownership and type system prevent data races at compile time — sharing state between threads requires explicit synchronization (Arc, Mutex, channels), which the type system enforces.

children.title: Future trait and polling
children.description: An async fn compiles to a state machine implementing Future — the executor calls poll() which either returns Poll::Ready(value) or Poll::Pending and registers a waker for re-polling.

children.title: Executors and runtimes
children.description: Rust's async/await requires a runtime to poll Futures — tokio is the dominant choice for production; async-std and smol are lighter alternatives for simpler use cases.

children.title: tokio::spawn and task management
children.description: tokio::spawn launches an async task on the runtime — tasks are lightweight green threads. JoinHandle lets you await the result or cancel the task.

children.title: tokio::select! for concurrent waiting
children.description: select! awaits multiple futures simultaneously and runs the handler for whichever completes first — the idiomatic way to implement timeouts and cancellation in async Rust.

children.title: std::sync::mpsc
children.description: mpsc (multiple producer, single consumer) is Rust's built-in synchronous channel — simple and effective for thread communication without external dependencies.

children.title: tokio channels for async
children.description: tokio provides mpsc, oneshot, broadcast, and watch channels — each optimized for different async communication patterns without blocking the runtime.

rust.title: Production Rust - Testing, Performance, and Ecosystem
rust.description: Write unit and integration tests in Rust, benchmark with Criterion, profile binaries, and survey the production Rust ecosystem for web, CLI, and systems use cases. Rust in production rewards investment in tooling and ecosystem knowledge.
rust.challenge: Finalize the task manager with a full test suite, Criterion benchmarks for storage operations, a Clap-powered CLI, and Serde JSON serialization for data persistence.

children.title: #[test] and cargo test
children.description: #[test] marks unit test functions; cargo test discovers and runs them all. Test modules in the same file use #[cfg(test)] to exclude them from production builds.

children.title: Integration tests in tests/
children.description: Files in the tests/ directory are compiled as separate crates — they only access public APIs, making them true integration tests of your library's public interface.

children.title: Criterion for benchmarking
children.description: criterion is the standard Rust benchmarking library — statistical analysis, outlier detection, and HTML reports. Run with cargo bench.

children.title: Profiling with flamegraphs
children.description: cargo-flamegraph generates flamegraphs from perf data — the fastest way to identify which functions consume the most CPU in a Rust program.

children.title: Axum and Actix-web
children.description: Axum (from the Tokio team) uses tower middleware and type-safe extractors; Actix-web is battle-tested and fastest in benchmarks — both are production-ready async web frameworks.

children.title: Serde for serialization
children.description: serde is the universal Rust serialization framework — #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)] handles JSON, TOML, YAML, MessagePack, and dozens of other formats with zero boilerplate.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Rust
children.description: A standard Rust CI workflow runs cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, and cargo fmt --check — catching logic bugs, lint warnings, and formatting issues on every PR.

children.title: Cross-compilation with cross
children.description: cross is a wrapper around cargo that uses Docker to cross-compile for any target — essential for building Linux ARM binaries from macOS/Windows or targeting embedded platforms.

java.title: Java Syntax and JVM Basics
java.description: Set up the JDK, understand the Java compilation model and JVM, and learn core syntax including variables, control flow, and methods. Java's verbosity is intentional - readability at scale is a core design goal.
java.challenge: Build a command-line bank account simulator in Java with deposit, withdraw, and balance commands as a Maven or Gradle project.

children.title: Bytecode and class loading
children.description: Java compiles to platform-independent bytecode (.class files) that the JVM interprets or JIT-compiles — the class loader finds and loads classes on demand at runtime.

children.title: JIT compilation
children.description: The JVM profiles running code and JIT-compiles hot methods to native machine code — this is why Java warms up slowly but reaches near-native speed for long-running processes.

children.title: Primitive vs reference types
children.description: Java has 8 primitive types (int, long, double, boolean, etc.) stored by value, and reference types (objects) stored as heap pointers — autoboxing converts between them automatically but adds overhead.

children.title: Static vs dynamic typing
children.description: Java is statically typed — all variable types are known at compile time. Generics extend this to collections, preventing ClassCastException at runtime.

children.title: JDK installation and JAVA_HOME
children.description: Install a JDK (not just JRE) and set JAVA_HOME — use SDKMAN! to manage multiple JDK versions per project, like nvm for Java.

children.title: IDE setup — IntelliJ IDEA
children.description: IntelliJ IDEA is the industry-standard Java IDE — its refactoring tools, debugger, and code analysis are unmatched. The community edition is free and sufficient for learning.

children.title: Maven project structure
children.description: Maven's standard layout (src/main/java, src/test/java) and pom.xml are understood by every Java tool — start with mvn archetype:generate for a working skeleton.

children.title: Gradle as an alternative
children.description: Gradle uses Groovy/Kotlin DSL instead of XML — faster incremental builds than Maven and the build system of choice for Android and many modern Java projects.

java.title: Object-Oriented Programming in Java
java.description: Master Java OOP fundamentals - classes, inheritance, polymorphism, and encapsulation - which are the bedrock of the entire Java ecosystem. Every Java library and framework is built on these principles.
java.challenge: Refactor the bank simulator into an OOP design with Account, SavingsAccount, and CheckingAccount classes using inheritance and polymorphic transaction handling.

children.title: Encapsulation and access modifiers
children.description: private, protected, package-private, and public control visibility — encapsulation hides implementation details so internal changes don't break callers.

children.title: Inheritance vs composition
children.description: Java supports single class inheritance but multiple interface implementation — prefer composition (has-a) over deep inheritance (is-a) to avoid fragile base class problems.

children.title: Dynamic dispatch
children.description: Java uses virtual dispatch for all non-static, non-final, non-private methods — the JVM calls the method on the actual runtime type, not the declared type.

children.title: Abstract classes vs interfaces
children.description: Abstract classes share implementation across related classes; interfaces define contracts for unrelated classes. Since Java 8, interfaces can have default methods, blurring the line.

resources.label: When to use abstract class vs interface

children.title: Builder pattern
children.description: Builder separates object construction from representation — ideal for objects with many optional fields, replacing telescoping constructors.

children.title: Singleton pattern
children.description: Singleton ensures one instance per JVM — the enum singleton is the safest Java implementation, handling serialization and reflection attacks automatically.

children.title: @Data, @Builder, @Value
children.description: Lombok generates boilerplate (getters, setters, equals, hashCode, toString) at compile time — widely used in production Java to reduce noise.

children.title: Records (Java 16+)
children.description: Records are immutable data carriers with auto-generated constructor, accessors, equals, hashCode, and toString — the modern Java replacement for many Lombok uses.

java.title: Collections Framework and Generics
java.description: Use the Java Collections Framework effectively - List, Set, Map, and Queue - and write generic classes and methods for type-safe reusable code. The collections framework is one of Java's most-used APIs.
java.challenge: Add a transaction history using a LinkedList and a sorted index using TreeMap to the bank simulator, with generic utility methods for balance calculations.

children.title: ArrayList vs LinkedList
children.description: ArrayList is backed by an array — O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete. LinkedList is a doubly-linked list — O(1) insert at ends but poor cache locality. ArrayList wins for most use cases.

children.title: HashMap internals
children.description: HashMap uses an array of buckets with chaining for collisions — Java 8+ converts buckets to balanced trees when they exceed 8 entries, preventing O(n) worst-case lookups.

children.title: Type erasure
children.description: Java generics are erased at runtime — List<String> and List<Integer> are both just List at the bytecode level. This preserves binary compatibility but prevents runtime type checks.

children.title: Wildcards and bounds
children.description: ? extends T (upper bound, read-only) and ? super T (lower bound, write-only) enable flexible generic APIs — the PECS principle (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) guides usage.

children.title: Choosing the right collection
children.description: List for ordered sequences, Set for uniqueness, Map for key-value lookups, Queue/Deque for FIFO/LIFO — knowing the interface keeps code flexible.

children.title: Collections utility methods
children.description: Collections.sort(), Collections.unmodifiableList(), Collections.synchronizedList() — the Collections class provides algorithms and wrapper factories for all collection types.

children.title: List.of() and Map.of()
children.description: Java 9+ factory methods create truly unmodifiable collections — they throw UnsupportedOperationException on mutation and are more memory-efficient than wrapped mutable collections.

children.title: ConcurrentHashMap
children.description: ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and segmented writes without locking the whole map — the thread-safe HashMap replacement for multi-threaded code.

java.title: Exception Handling and File I/O
java.description: Handle exceptions correctly in Java using checked and unchecked exception hierarchies, and read and write files using the NIO.2 API. Java's explicit exception handling forces you to think about failure modes.
topics: Reading and writing text and binary files
java.challenge: Add persistent account storage to the bank simulator using java.nio.file and JSON serialization, with custom exceptions for insufficient funds and invalid accounts.

children.title: Checked vs unchecked exceptions
children.description: Checked exceptions (extend Exception) must be caught or declared — they model recoverable conditions. Unchecked (extend RuntimeException) indicate programming errors and don't require handling.

children.title: Exception chaining
children.description: Passing the cause to a new exception (throw new ServiceException("msg", e)) preserves the original stack trace — essential for debugging production errors without losing context.

children.title: NIO.2 vs old IO
children.description: java.nio.file (NIO.2, Java 7+) replaced java.io.File — Path, Files, and StandardOpenOption are the modern API with better error messages and atomic operations.

children.title: try-with-resources
children.description: try(Resource r = new Resource()) automatically calls r.close() on exit — any class implementing AutoCloseable works, eliminating finally blocks for resource cleanup.

children.title: Custom exception classes
children.description: Creating domain-specific exceptions (OrderNotFoundException extends RuntimeException) makes error handling more expressive and lets callers catch specific failure modes.

children.title: Optional for null avoidance
children.description: Optional<T> wraps a potentially-absent value and forces the caller to handle the absent case — the Java replacement for null returns that cause NullPointerException.

children.title: Files utility class
children.description: Files.readAllLines(), Files.write(), Files.walk() — the static utility methods on Files cover most file operations in one or two lines without manual stream management.

children.title: BufferedReader and BufferedWriter
children.description: Buffered streams reduce I/O syscalls by reading/writing chunks — wrap any Reader/Writer in a Buffered variant for large files to avoid performance issues.

java.title: Streams API and Lambdas
java.description: Transform, filter, and aggregate data using the Java Streams API and lambda expressions introduced in Java 8. Streams are the idiomatic way to process collections in modern Java and often replace explicit loops.
java.challenge: Rewrite all account querying and reporting logic in the bank simulator using Streams, grouping transactions by type using Collectors.groupingBy.

children.title: Function, Predicate, Consumer, Supplier
children.description: java.util.function defines the standard functional interface vocabulary — Function<T,R> maps, Predicate<T> tests, Consumer<T> processes, Supplier<T> produces.

children.title: Method references
children.description: Method references (ClassName::method) are a shorthand for lambdas that just call a method — cleaner and more readable when the lambda body is a single method call.

children.title: Lazy evaluation
children.description: Stream operations are lazy — intermediate operations (filter, map) are not executed until a terminal operation (collect, forEach) is called. This enables short-circuit optimization.

children.title: Parallel streams
children.description: stream().parallel() distributes work across the ForkJoinPool — beneficial for CPU-bound operations on large collections, but adds overhead for small or I/O-bound work.

children.title: collect() and Collectors
children.description: Collectors.toList(), groupingBy(), joining(), toMap() — the Collectors class provides all common terminal aggregations and is the primary way to materialise stream results.

children.title: flatMap for nested collections
children.description: flatMap flattens a stream of collections into a single stream — essential for processing nested data structures like lists of lists.

children.title: Stream vs for loop
children.description: Streams are more declarative and composable; for loops are more performant for simple operations — prefer streams for readability unless profiling shows a bottleneck.

children.title: Optional with streams
children.description: Optional.stream() (Java 9+) converts an Optional to a 0-or-1 element stream — enables flatMapping optionals inside stream pipelines cleanly.

java.title: Concurrency and Multithreading
java.description: Write concurrent Java programs using threads, the Executor framework, and concurrent collections. Java has one of the most mature concurrency APIs in any language, built into the standard library.
java.challenge: Add concurrent transaction processing to the bank simulator using a thread pool, protecting balance state with appropriate synchronization primitives.

children.title: Java Memory Model
children.description: The JMM defines when writes by one thread become visible to others — the volatile keyword and synchronization provide the happens-before guarantees needed to avoid visibility bugs.

children.title: synchronized and volatile
children.description: synchronized provides mutual exclusion and visibility; volatile provides visibility only — using volatile for a read-modify-write (i++) is still a data race.

children.title: Thread states
children.description: Java threads move through NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, and TERMINATED — understanding states helps diagnose deadlocks and starvation with thread dumps.

children.title: Deadlock detection
children.description: Deadlocks occur when two threads each hold a lock the other needs — thread dumps (kill -3 or jstack) reveal deadlocked threads with their lock acquisition chains.

children.title: ExecutorService and thread pools
children.description: ExecutorService manages a pool of threads for task submission — Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n) and newCachedThreadPool() cover most use cases without managing threads manually.

children.title: CompletableFuture
children.description: CompletableFuture enables async pipelines with thenApply, thenCompose, and thenCombine — the modern Java replacement for raw Future that supports non-blocking composition.

children.title: AtomicInteger and AtomicReference
children.description: java.util.concurrent.atomic classes provide lock-free thread-safe operations using CAS (compare-and-swap) — faster than synchronized for simple counters and flags.

children.title: ReentrantLock
children.description: ReentrantLock provides more flexibility than synchronized — tryLock(), lockInterruptibly(), and fairness policy give you control over lock acquisition that synchronized doesn't.

java.title: Design Patterns in Java
java.description: Apply classic Gang of Four design patterns in Java - singleton, builder, factory, observer, and strategy - and understand when each applies. Design patterns are the shared vocabulary of Java software architecture.
java.challenge: Refactor the bank simulator using the Builder pattern for account creation, Strategy for transaction fee calculation, and Observer for balance change notifications.

children.title: Single Responsibility and Open/Closed
children.description: SRP — a class should have one reason to change. OCP — open for extension, closed for modification. These two principles drive most refactoring decisions in Java codebases.

children.title: Dependency Inversion
children.description: High-level modules should depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete classes — this is the foundation of testable, loosely-coupled Java architecture.

children.title: Strategy pattern
children.description: Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms behind an interface — swapping implementations at runtime without if/else chains is its main purpose.

children.title: Observer pattern
children.description: Observer decouples event producers from consumers — Java's built-in PropertyChangeListener and modern reactive frameworks (RxJava, Spring Events) are both Observer implementations.

children.title: Factory Method and Abstract Factory
children.description: Factory Method delegates instantiation to subclasses; Abstract Factory creates families of related objects — both decouple callers from concrete classes.

children.title: Prototype pattern
children.description: Prototype clones existing objects instead of constructing new ones — implement Cloneable or a copy constructor. Useful for expensive object initialization.

children.title: Decorator pattern
children.description: Decorator wraps an object to add behaviour dynamically — Java's I/O streams (BufferedInputStream wrapping FileInputStream) are the classic real-world example.

children.title: Proxy and AOP
children.description: Proxy wraps an object to control access — Spring's @Transactional and @Cacheable use dynamic proxies under the hood to add cross-cutting concerns transparently.

java.title: Modern Java - Records, Sealed Classes, and Pattern Matching
java.description: Adopt modern Java features from Java 14-21 including records, sealed classes, text blocks, and pattern matching for switch. Modern Java is significantly more expressive than the Java 8 many developers learned.
java.challenge: Refactor transaction types in the bank simulator to sealed interfaces with record implementations, using pattern matching switch for all transaction dispatch logic.

children.title: Records internals
children.description: Records are final classes with auto-generated canonical constructor, accessors (not getters), equals, hashCode, and toString — the compiler enforces immutability by making fields final.

children.title: Sealed classes
children.description: Sealed classes restrict which classes can extend or implement them — combined with pattern matching, they enable exhaustive switch expressions over type hierarchies.

children.title: Pattern matching for instanceof
children.description: if (obj instanceof String s) eliminates the explicit cast after an instanceof check — the pattern variable s is in scope only in the true branch.

children.title: Switch expressions and pattern matching
children.description: Java 21 switch can match on types, guarded patterns, and deconstruct records — enabling exhaustive handling of sealed type hierarchies without manual casting.

children.title: Text blocks
children.description: Text blocks (Java 15+) are multi-line string literals with automatic indentation trimming — essential for embedding JSON, SQL, and HTML without escape hell.

children.title: var — local type inference
children.description: var infers the variable type from the initializer (Java 10+) — use it to reduce verbosity for long generic types, but keep it where the type is obvious from context.

children.title: Virtual threads (Project Loom)
children.description: Virtual threads are lightweight JVM-managed threads — you can create millions without exhausting OS thread limits, making blocking I/O practical again without async frameworks.

children.title: Structured concurrency
children.description: StructuredTaskScope (Java 21 preview) treats a group of concurrent tasks as a unit — if one fails, the others are cancelled automatically, preventing thread leaks.

java.title: Testing with JUnit 5 and Mockito
java.description: Write unit and integration tests using JUnit 5 with parameterized tests and nested test classes, and mock dependencies with Mockito. Testing is a first-class practice in Java - the ecosystem is among the best in any language.
java.challenge: Write a complete JUnit 5 test suite for the bank simulator with Mockito mocks for external storage, parameterized tests for edge cases, and JaCoCo coverage above 80%.

children.title: JUnit 5 architecture
children.description: JUnit 5 is split into JUnit Platform (launcher), JUnit Jupiter (API), and JUnit Vintage (legacy runner) — this separation enables multiple testing frameworks on one platform.

children.title: Test lifecycle annotations
children.description: @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, @AfterAll manage test setup and teardown — @BeforeAll and @AfterAll require static methods unless the test class is @TestInstance(PER_CLASS).

children.title: How Mockito generates mocks
children.description: Mockito uses bytecode generation (via ByteBuddy or cglib) to create subclass-based mocks at runtime — this is why you can't mock final classes without the MockMaker extension.

children.title: Argument matchers
children.description: any(), eq(), argThat() let you match method arguments flexibly in when() and verify() — mixing matchers and raw values requires wrapping all args in matchers.

children.title: Given-When-Then structure
children.description: Organizing tests into Given (setup), When (action), Then (assertion) sections makes tests self-documenting and maps directly to BDD-style specifications.

children.title: Parameterized tests
children.description: @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource, @CsvSource, or @MethodSource runs the same test logic with multiple inputs — replacing copy-pasted test methods.

children.title: Spring Boot Test slice annotations
children.description: @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, @JsonTest load only the relevant slice of the Spring context — faster than @SpringBootTest for focused integration tests.

children.title: Testcontainers
children.description: Testcontainers starts real Docker containers (Postgres, Redis, Kafka) for integration tests — eliminating in-memory database mismatch bugs.

java.title: Production Java - Build Tools, Performance, and Ecosystem
java.description: Ship production Java with Maven or Gradle, profile JVM performance with JFR and JMC, and navigate the Java ecosystem including Spring, Micronaut, and Jakarta EE. Java's mature ecosystem means there is a well-tested library for nearly every problem.
java.challenge: Package the bank simulator as a fat JAR with Maven, profile it with JFR under simulated load, tune the GC settings, and add a Spring Boot HTTP layer as a bonus extension.

children.title: Maven lifecycle and plugins
children.description: Maven's build lifecycle (validate, compile, test, package, install, deploy) runs plugins in order — understanding phases lets you hook custom tasks at the right point.

children.title: Gradle incremental builds
children.description: Gradle tracks task inputs and outputs — if nothing changed, tasks are UP-TO-DATE and skipped. Build caching shares results across machines, making CI dramatically faster.

children.title: GC tuning basics
children.description: The JVM has multiple GC algorithms (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah) — G1 is the default since Java 9; ZGC targets sub-millisecond pauses for large heaps. Tune with -Xms, -Xmx, and GC flags.

children.title: JVM profiling with async-profiler
children.description: async-profiler samples CPU and allocations with low overhead — the go-to tool for finding hot methods and memory pressure in production JVM applications.

children.title: Spring Boot
children.description: Spring Boot is the de-facto Java backend framework — auto-configuration, embedded servers, and production-ready starters let you ship REST APIs without XML configuration.

children.title: Quarkus and Micronaut
children.description: Quarkus and Micronaut compile to native binaries with GraalVM — millisecond startup times and tiny memory footprint for microservices and serverless deployments.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Java
children.description: A Maven or Gradle workflow with actions/setup-java handles JDK installation, caching, and parallel matrix builds across multiple Java versions in CI.

children.title: Docker for Java apps
children.description: Multi-stage Docker builds compile with a JDK image and run with a JRE image — use distroless or Eclipse Temurin JRE images to minimize attack surface and image size.

cpp.title: C++ Syntax and Compilation Model
cpp.description: Set up a C++ compiler (GCC or Clang), understand the preprocessing, compilation, and linking pipeline, and learn basic syntax. C++ is a compiled systems language where understanding the build model is as important as the syntax.
cpp.challenge: Build a command-line contact book in C++ with add, search, and list commands, compiled with CMake and organized into header and source files.

children.title: Preprocessing, compiling, linking
children.description: C++ source goes through three phases: the preprocessor expands macros and includes, the compiler produces object files, and the linker combines them into an executable.

children.title: Translation units
children.description: Each .cpp file is a separate translation unit compiled independently — this is why you include headers (declarations) not other .cpp files (definitions).

children.title: C++ additions over C
children.description: C++ adds classes, templates, references, operator overloading, exceptions, and the STL on top of C — understanding C first makes C++ semantics clearer.

children.title: Name mangling
children.description: C++ mangles function names to encode types for overloading — use extern "C" when interfacing with C code to suppress mangling.

children.title: g++ flags
children.description: Key flags: -std=c++20, -O2 (optimize), -Wall -Wextra (warnings), -g (debug info) — always compile with warnings enabled and treat them as errors in production.

children.title: CMake basics
children.description: CMake is the de-facto build system for C++ — cmake_minimum_required, project(), add_executable(), and target_compile_features() cover most project setups.

children.title: VS Code + clangd
children.description: clangd provides C++-aware autocomplete, diagnostics, and go-to-definition — it reads compile_commands.json generated by CMake for accurate project-wide analysis.

children.title: Compiler Explorer
children.description: godbolt.org shows the assembly output of any C++ snippet across multiple compilers and optimization levels — essential for understanding what your code actually compiles to.

cpp.title: Pointers, References, and Memory Management
cpp.description: Understand pointers and references, manual memory management with new and delete, and why unmanaged memory is dangerous. This layer is where C++ diverges most sharply from other modern languages.
cpp.challenge: Rewrite the contact book using raw pointers for dynamic storage, then intentionally introduce and diagnose a memory leak using Valgrind.

children.title: Stack vs heap
children.description: Local variables live on the stack and are automatically destroyed when they go out of scope; heap allocations with new persist until delete — mismanaging them causes leaks.

children.title: Pointer arithmetic
children.description: Incrementing a pointer moves it by sizeof(T) bytes — this is how array indexing works under the hood, and why pointer+offset is equivalent to array subscript.

children.title: Dangling pointers and use-after-free
children.description: Using a pointer after the object it pointed to was destroyed is undefined behaviour — the program may appear to work, crash, or be exploited silently.

children.title: UB and compiler optimizations
children.description: Compilers assume UB never happens and optimize accordingly — this can silently remove safety checks. -fsanitize=address catches many UB cases at runtime.

resources.label: What Every C Programmer Should Know About UB

children.title: Valgrind memcheck
children.description: Valgrind detects heap corruption, use-after-free, and memory leaks at runtime — run with valgrind --leak-check=full on any program handling dynamic memory.

children.title: AddressSanitizer
children.description: ASan is a fast compiler-based sanitizer (compile with -fsanitize=address) that detects buffer overflows and use-after-free with minimal overhead.

children.title: RAII intro
children.description: Wrapping a resource (file, mutex, connection) in a class whose destructor releases it guarantees cleanup even on exceptions — the core C++ resource management pattern.

children.title: Rule of Three
children.description: If you define a destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment operator, you probably need all three — missing one leads to double-free or shallow copy bugs.

cpp.title: Classes, OOP, and RAII
cpp.description: Define C++ classes with constructors, destructors, and operator overloading, and learn RAII - the pattern of tying resource lifetimes to object lifetimes. RAII is the fundamental C++ idiom for safe resource management.
cpp.challenge: Refactor the contact book into a RAII-safe class design where the ContactBook destructor releases all memory, with copy and move constructors implemented correctly.

children.title: Resource acquisition is initialization
children.description: RAII binds resource lifetime to object lifetime — the constructor acquires the resource and the destructor releases it, making leaks structurally impossible.

children.title: Destructor guarantees
children.description: Destructors are called automatically when an object goes out of scope or is deleted — this makes them the right place to release any resource the constructor acquired.

children.title: vtable mechanism
children.description: Virtual functions are dispatched through a vtable — a per-class array of function pointers. Each virtual call is a two-pointer dereference, which is why virtual dispatch has a (small) cost.

children.title: Virtual destructor
children.description: Always declare destructors virtual in base classes — without it, deleting a derived class through a base pointer calls only the base destructor, leaking the derived part.

children.title: Pure virtual interfaces
children.description: A class with only pure virtual methods (= 0) is an abstract interface — the standard C++ way to define a contract without any implementation.

children.title: Composition over inheritance
children.description: Deep inheritance hierarchies are fragile in C++ — prefer composing objects and using interfaces over long inheritance chains.

children.title: Copy and move semantics
children.description: The Rule of Five extends Rule of Three with the move constructor and move assignment operator — implementing move avoids unnecessary copies for expensive resources.

children.title: noexcept specifier
children.description: Marking move constructors noexcept allows STL containers to use them for reallocation instead of falling back to copying — a key performance optimization.

cpp.title: STL Containers and Algorithms
cpp.description: Use the C++ Standard Template Library containers (vector, map, set, unordered_map) and algorithms (sort, find, transform) to write efficient, idiomatic C++. The STL is one of the most powerful standard libraries in any language.
cpp.challenge: Replace all raw arrays in the contact book with vector and unordered_map, and use STL algorithms for all searching and sorting operations.

children.title: Big-O for STL ops
children.description: std::vector provides O(1) random access and amortized O(1) push_back; std::map provides O(log n) ops; std::unordered_map provides O(1) average — choosing the right container matters.

children.title: Iterator categories
children.description: Iterators come in 6 categories (input, output, forward, bidirectional, random access, contiguous) — algorithms require specific categories, and using the wrong one causes compile errors.

children.title: Predicate-based algorithms
children.description: STL algorithms like std::sort and std::find_if accept predicates — passing lambdas as predicates makes algorithms reusable for any comparison logic.

children.title: Range concepts (C++20)
children.description: C++20 ranges provide lazy, composable views over containers — std::ranges::filter(v, pred) | std::ranges::transform(f) replaces manual iterator loops.

children.title: vector vs deque vs list
children.description: vector is contiguous and cache-friendly for sequential access; deque supports efficient front/back insertion; list has O(1) splicing but poor cache performance.

children.title: unordered_map vs map
children.description: unordered_map provides O(1) average lookup using hashing; map provides O(log n) with sorted keys — use unordered_map unless you need ordering or a custom comparator.

children.title: std::sort and std::stable_sort
children.description: std::sort provides introsort (O(n log n) worst case); std::stable_sort preserves relative order of equal elements — prefer sort unless stability is required.

children.title: std::transform and std::for_each
children.description: transform applies a function to each element and writes results to another range; for_each applies a function for side effects — both replace explicit loops.

cpp.title: Templates and Generic Programming
cpp.description: Write function templates and class templates to create generic, reusable code that works across types without runtime overhead. C++ templates are Turing-complete and enable zero-cost abstractions unique to the language.
cpp.challenge: Add a generic Cache<K, V> template class to the contact book with configurable eviction policy, specializing it for string keys with case-insensitive lookup.

children.title: Compile-time code generation
children.description: Templates generate code at compile time for each type they're instantiated with — this is zero-runtime-overhead abstraction, but can increase binary size.

children.title: Template bloat
children.description: Instantiating the same template with many types creates many copies of the generated code — explicit instantiation declarations and the pimpl idiom mitigate this.

children.title: Substitution failure is not an error
children.description: When template argument deduction fails for one overload, the compiler silently tries the next — this is the mechanism behind type-based function selection.

children.title: std::enable_if
children.description: enable_if<condition, T>::type disables a template overload when condition is false — the classic SFINAE tool, largely replaced by concepts in C++20.

children.title: CRTP — Curiously Recurring Template Pattern
children.description: CRTP passes the derived class as a template argument to the base — enabling static polymorphism (no vtable) and mixin-style code reuse.

children.title: Policy-based design
children.description: Policy classes are template parameters that inject behaviour — separating concerns like allocation strategy from container logic for zero-cost customisation.

children.title: requires clauses
children.description: requires(T t) { t.size(); } specifies what operations a type must support — much clearer template errors than SFINAE, with better IDE support.

children.title: Standard concepts
children.description: The <concepts> header provides ready-made concepts (std::integral, std::floating_point, std::invocable) — use them instead of writing your own SFINAE checks.

cpp.title: Smart Pointers and Modern Memory Management
cpp.description: Replace raw pointers with smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr) for automatic, safe memory management. Modern C++ code should rarely if ever use new and delete directly.

resources.label: Effective Modern C++ - Smart Pointer Items

cpp.challenge: Refactor the entire contact book to use smart pointers exclusively, eliminating all raw new/delete, and demonstrate correct use of weak_ptr for observer relationships.

children.title: unique_ptr ownership model
children.description: unique_ptr represents exclusive ownership — it cannot be copied, only moved. When it goes out of scope, it automatically deletes the managed object.

children.title: shared_ptr reference counting
children.description: shared_ptr uses atomic reference counting — when the last copy is destroyed, the managed object is deleted. The shared control block has overhead; use unique_ptr when ownership is exclusive.

children.title: Breaking reference cycles
children.description: shared_ptr cycles prevent deletion — weak_ptr observes a shared_ptr without owning it, breaking the cycle and allowing proper cleanup.

children.title: lock() pattern
children.description: weak_ptr::lock() returns a shared_ptr if the managed object still exists, or an empty shared_ptr if it was already destroyed — always check before using.

children.title: make_unique and make_shared
children.description: Always use make_unique<T>(args) instead of new — it's exception-safe (no leak if constructor throws) and more readable. make_shared allocates the object and control block together.

children.title: Passing smart pointers
children.description: Pass by raw reference/pointer when ownership isn't transferred; pass unique_ptr by value to transfer ownership; pass shared_ptr by value to share ownership — passing by const& just to observe.

children.title: File handle RAII with unique_ptr
children.description: unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(&fclose)> wraps a C FILE* and calls fclose automatically — any custom cleanup can be injected as a deleter.

children.title: Array deleters
children.description: unique_ptr<T[]> automatically uses delete[] instead of delete for array allocations — use it when you must manage raw arrays (rare with std::vector).

cpp.title: Lambdas, Ranges, and Modern C++ Idioms
cpp.description: Use C++11/14/17/20 modern idioms including lambdas, range-based for, structured bindings, and the Ranges library. Modern C++ reads very differently from legacy C++ - knowing the new idioms is essential.
cpp.challenge: Rewrite all contact book filtering and querying using C++20 Ranges with view pipelines, structured bindings for result unpacking, and std::optional for nullable results.

children.title: Closure object
children.description: A lambda creates an anonymous struct (closure type) with operator() — capturing variables copies or references them as data members of this struct.

children.title: Capture modes
children.description: [=] captures all locals by value; [&] by reference; [this] captures the this pointer — mixing them ([=, &x]) is allowed. Reference captures dangle if the lambda outlives the local.

children.title: Lazy evaluation
children.description: Range views are lazy — they don't compute anything until you iterate. Chaining views composes into a single pass over the data without intermediate containers.

children.title: Views vs actions
children.description: Views are lazy and don't modify data; actions (range-v3 concept) eagerly consume and produce new containers. The distinction matters for performance and lifetime.

children.title: Structured bindings (C++17)
children.description: auto [x, y] = pair; binds multiple variables from a struct, pair, or tuple in one line — cleaner than std::tie and works with custom types implementing get<>.

children.title: if constexpr
children.description: if constexpr(condition) evaluates the condition at compile time and discards the dead branch entirely — enabling template specialization logic without enable_if.

children.title: std::views::filter and transform
children.description: std::views::filter(pred) | std::views::transform(f) composes into a lazy pipeline — iterate once, no intermediate allocations, and composable with any range.

children.title: std::ranges algorithms
children.description: std::ranges::sort, std::ranges::find_if, and std::ranges::copy replace their iterator-pair counterparts — accepting containers directly and projections for member access.

cpp.title: Concurrency in C++
cpp.description: Write multi-threaded C++ programs using std::thread, mutexes, condition variables, and atomics from the C++11 concurrency library. C++ concurrency requires explicit management - the memory model is the foundation.
cpp.challenge: Add a background auto-save thread to the contact book that periodically serializes data, using a mutex-protected queue for pending write operations.

children.title: Happens-before and memory orderings
children.description: C++'s memory model defines happens-before — std::memory_order controls how atomic operations synchronize between threads without locks.

children.title: Data races are UB
children.description: A data race (unsynchronized access where at least one is a write) is undefined behaviour in C++ — the compiler and CPU may reorder operations in ways that make the race worse.

children.title: Thread sanitizer (TSan)
children.description: Compile with -fsanitize=thread to detect data races at runtime — TSan instruments memory accesses and reports races with the exact thread and location.

children.title: std::atomic for lock-free programming
children.description: std::atomic<T> provides lock-free operations on simple types — use it instead of mutex for hot counters and flags where lock overhead matters.

children.title: std::thread
children.description: std::thread launches a function on a new OS thread — always join() or detach() before the thread object is destroyed to avoid std::terminate being called.

children.title: std::async and std::future
children.description: std::async launches a task asynchronously (or deferred) and returns a future — a higher-level alternative to raw threads for tasks that produce a result.

children.title: std::mutex and lock_guard
children.description: std::lock_guard<std::mutex> is an RAII wrapper that locks on construction and unlocks on destruction — the standard pattern for protecting shared state.

children.title: std::condition_variable
children.description: condition_variable allows one thread to wait until a condition is true — use with a unique_lock and a predicate to avoid spurious wakeups.

cpp.title: Error Handling and Robust C++
cpp.description: Handle errors in C++ using exceptions, noexcept, std::error_code, and defensive programming techniques. C++ offers multiple error-handling strategies - choosing the right one for each context is a mark of expertise.
cpp.challenge: Add strong exception safety guarantees to all ContactBook mutating operations and use std::error_code for file I/O error reporting without throwing.

children.title: Basic, strong, nothrow guarantees
children.description: Exception safety levels: basic (invariants hold), strong (commit-or-rollback), nothrow (never throws). Most well-written C++ targets the strong guarantee for mutating operations.

children.title: RAII for exception safety
children.description: RAII objects clean up automatically when an exception unwinds the stack — this is what makes C++ exception handling practical without finally blocks.

children.title: Result type pattern
children.description: std::expected<T, E> either holds a value or an error — a zero-overhead alternative to exceptions for recoverable errors in performance-critical paths.

children.title: Error handling without exceptions
children.description: Embedded and real-time C++ often disables exceptions — std::expected, error_code, and output parameters are the alternatives with deterministic performance.

children.title: try/catch hierarchies
children.description: Catch exceptions from most specific to least specific — catching std::exception& first handles most standard library errors with a readable .what() message.

children.title: Custom exception classes
children.description: Derive from std::exception or std::runtime_error to create domain-specific exceptions — override what() to return a meaningful error description.

children.title: noexcept in move operations
children.description: Move constructors and move assignment operators should be noexcept — STL containers check this to decide whether to move or copy during reallocation.

children.title: Performance implications
children.description: noexcept functions allow the compiler to skip generating exception-handling table entries — measurable improvement in binary size and sometimes in performance.

cpp.title: Production C++ - Build Systems, Profiling, and Ecosystem
cpp.description: Build production C++ with CMake, profile with perf or Instruments, use sanitizers to catch bugs, and understand the C++ ecosystem including Boost, Conan, and vcpkg. Production C++ requires mastery of the full toolchain.
topics: CMake targets and modern CMake patterns
cpp.challenge: Finalize the contact book with a CMake build, Google Test suite, AddressSanitizer clean run, Conan-managed dependencies, and a perf profile showing no hot spots in critical paths.

children.title: Modern CMake patterns
children.description: target_* commands (target_include_directories, target_link_libraries) with PRIVATE/PUBLIC/INTERFACE propagate dependencies correctly — never use global include_directories().

children.title: Conan package manager
children.description: Conan manages C++ dependencies with versioning and binary caching — integrates with CMake via conan_cmake_run() or the modern CMakeDeps generator.

children.title: gprof and perf
children.description: gprof instruments binaries at compile time; perf samples CPU counters with no recompilation — perf is more accurate for production code.

children.title: Compiler Sanitizers
children.description: -fsanitize=address,undefined,thread catches memory errors, UB, and data races respectively — run all three in your CI pipeline before shipping.

children.title: UBSan and ASan in CI
children.description: Running tests with -fsanitize=address,undefined in CI catches memory and UB issues that only show up in specific inputs — the highest ROI compiler flag for correctness.

children.title: ThreadSanitizer
children.description: -fsanitize=thread detects data races at runtime with about 5-15x slowdown — run on the concurrent parts of your test suite to catch races before they reach production.

children.title: GitHub Actions for C++
children.description: A CMake + CTest workflow builds and tests in CI — add separate jobs for each sanitizer and for multiple compilers (GCC, Clang) to catch compiler-specific issues.

children.title: clang-tidy static analysis
children.description: clang-tidy provides hundreds of C++ lint checks — run it in CI with -warnings-as-errors to enforce coding style and catch common bugs before review.

csharp.title: C# Syntax and .NET Setup
csharp.description: Set up the .NET SDK, create and run C# projects with the dotnet CLI, and learn basic C# syntax. C# is a modern, statically typed language that runs on the .NET runtime across Windows, Linux, and macOS.
csharp.challenge: Build a command-line expense tracker in C# using the dotnet CLI, with add, list, and total commands.

children.title: CLR and managed execution
children.description: C# compiles to CIL (Common Intermediate Language) bytecode executed by the CLR — the runtime handles JIT compilation, garbage collection, and type safety across all .NET languages.

children.title: Value vs reference types
children.description: Value types (struct, int, bool) live on the stack and are copied on assignment; reference types (class, string, arrays) live on the heap with a pointer — understanding this drives boxing/unboxing awareness.

children.title: Unified type system
children.description: In C#, all types including primitives ultimately derive from System.Object — int is an alias for System.Int32, enabling methods like .ToString() on any value.

children.title: Nullable value types
children.description: int? is shorthand for Nullable<int> — a struct that wraps a value type to allow null. C# 8+ nullable reference types extend this safety to reference types.

children.title: .NET SDK and CLI
children.description: dotnet new, dotnet build, dotnet run, dotnet test — the .NET CLI handles everything from project scaffolding to publishing without an IDE.

children.title: Visual Studio vs VS Code
children.description: Visual Studio (Windows/Mac) provides the richest C# experience with full debugging and designer tools; VS Code + C# Dev Kit is cross-platform and lighter for modern .NET projects.

children.title: .csproj and solution files
children.description: .csproj is an MSBuild XML file defining targets, dependencies, and build options — .sln groups multiple projects together for building and IDE navigation.

children.title: NuGet package management
children.description: NuGet is the .NET package manager — add packages with dotnet add package or the NuGet Package Manager in Visual Studio. packages.lock.json pins exact versions for reproducible builds.

csharp.title: Object-Oriented C#
csharp.description: Use C# classes, inheritance, interfaces, and properties to build structured object-oriented programs. C# OOP has several features not found in Java - properties, partial classes, and explicit interface implementation among them.
csharp.challenge: Refactor the expense tracker into an OOP design with Expense, Category, and Budget classes using properties and interface-based storage.

children.title: Class vs struct semantics
children.description: Classes are reference types with heap allocation and inheritance; structs are value types with stack allocation and copy semantics — structs are ideal for small, immutable data like coordinates.

children.title: Virtual, override, and sealed
children.description: virtual declares a method overridable; override replaces it in a subclass; sealed prevents further overriding or class inheritance — together they control the extensibility contract.

children.title: Explicit interface implementation
children.description: When a class implements two interfaces with the same method name, explicit implementation disambiguates — the method is only accessible through the interface type, not the class type.

children.title: Default interface methods (C# 8+)
children.description: Interfaces can now have default method implementations — enabling interface evolution without breaking existing implementations, similar to Java 8 default methods.

children.title: Properties vs fields
children.description: Properties encapsulate fields with get/set accessors — auto-properties ({ get; set; }) generate a backing field automatically, while computed properties derive from other state.

children.title: Init-only properties and records
children.description: init accessors (C# 9+) allow setting a property only during object initialization — records combine init properties, value equality, and with-expressions for immutable data models.

children.title: Constructor injection pattern
children.description: Declaring dependencies as constructor parameters makes them explicit and testable — the .NET built-in DI container (IServiceCollection) resolves them automatically.

children.title: Service lifetimes
children.description: Transient (new instance per request), Scoped (per HTTP request), and Singleton (one instance per app) — choosing the wrong lifetime causes subtle bugs like disposed DbContext.

csharp.title: Collections, Generics, and LINQ Basics
csharp.description: Use .NET generic collections (List<T>, Dictionary<K,V>, HashSet<T>) and write your first LINQ queries to filter and project data. LINQ is one of C# most distinctive and powerful features.
csharp.challenge: Use LINQ to add reporting features to the expense tracker - top categories, monthly totals, and expenses above a threshold - using method chaining.

children.title: Deferred vs immediate execution
children.description: LINQ query operators like Where and Select are lazy — they return IEnumerable<T> without executing until iterated. ToList(), Count(), and First() trigger immediate execution.

children.title: Expression trees
children.description: LINQ to SQL and Entity Framework translate LINQ queries to SQL by building expression trees — an in-memory representation of code as data that can be analyzed and transformed.

children.title: Reified generics vs type erasure
children.description: Unlike Java, .NET generics are reified — List<int> and List<string> are genuinely different types at runtime. This enables runtime typeof(T) checks and avoids boxing for value types.

children.title: Covariance and contravariance
children.description: out T makes a generic type covariant (IEnumerable<string> assignable to IEnumerable<object>); in T makes it contravariant — only applicable to interfaces and delegates.

children.title: Method syntax vs query syntax
children.description: LINQ has two syntaxes — method chaining (.Where().Select()) and SQL-like query expressions (from x in xs where ... select). Method syntax is more common and composable.

children.title: GroupBy, Join, and SelectMany
children.description: GroupBy groups elements by a key; Join correlates two sequences; SelectMany flattens nested collections — the three LINQ operators that handle relational-style data transformation.

children.title: IEnumerable<T> vs IQueryable<T>
children.description: IEnumerable<T> executes LINQ in memory; IQueryable<T> translates LINQ to a query language (SQL) — mixing them accidentally causes the N+1 query problem in Entity Framework.

children.title: List<T>, Dictionary<K,V>, HashSet<T>
children.description: List<T> for ordered sequences, Dictionary<K,V> for key-value lookups, HashSet<T> for unique elements — all implement generic interfaces enabling LINQ and polymorphic code.

csharp.title: Exception Handling and File I/O
csharp.description: Write robust exception handling using C# try/catch/finally, custom exceptions, and the using statement for resource management. Use System.IO for file operations and System.Text.Json for serialization.
csharp.challenge: Add JSON file persistence to the expense tracker using System.Text.Json with custom exceptions for invalid data and a using statement for file handle safety.

children.title: Exception hierarchy
