children.description: All C# exceptions derive from System.Exception — System.ApplicationException (app errors) and System.SystemException (runtime errors) are the two main branches, though the distinction is rarely enforced.

children.title: Exception filters
children.description: catch (IOException e) when (e.HResult == -2147024784) — catch filters conditionally handle exceptions without catching and rethrowing, which preserves the original stack trace.

children.title: Stream hierarchy
children.description: Stream is the abstract base for all I/O in .NET — FileStream, MemoryStream, NetworkStream all share the same Read/Write interface enabling composable stream pipelines.

children.title: IDisposable and using
children.description: Resources implementing IDisposable must be disposed — the using statement calls Dispose() automatically on scope exit, even on exceptions. C# 8+ using declarations remove the nesting.

children.title: Custom exceptions
children.description: Derive from Exception (or a specific subtype) and add properties for domain context — throw new OrderNotFoundException(orderId) is more informative than throw new Exception("not found").

children.title: Global exception handling
children.description: ASP.NET Core's UseExceptionHandler middleware catches unhandled exceptions globally — the right place to map exceptions to HTTP status codes and log them centrally.

children.title: File and Directory classes
children.description: System.IO.File provides static methods for common operations (File.ReadAllText, File.WriteAllLines, File.Exists) — the quickest path for simple file tasks without stream management.

children.title: StreamReader and StreamWriter
children.description: StreamReader/Writer wrap streams with text encoding support — use them with using for reading/writing text files line by line without loading everything into memory.

csharp.title: Async/Await and the Task Parallel Library
csharp.description: Write asynchronous C# code using async/await and the Task Parallel Library for non-blocking I/O and parallel CPU work. C# async/await is one of the most ergonomic async models in any mainstream language.
csharp.challenge: Add async file I/O and an async HTTP export feature to the expense tracker using async/await throughout with CancellationToken support.

children.title: State machine compilation
children.description: The C# compiler transforms async methods into state machines — each await point is a state, and the runtime schedules continuation without blocking a thread.

children.title: SynchronizationContext
children.description: SynchronizationContext captures the current thread context — in UI apps, await resumes on the UI thread by default. ConfigureAwait(false) avoids this capture for library code.

children.title: Task vs Thread
children.description: Task represents a unit of work scheduled on the ThreadPool — more efficient than raw Thread for short operations, and composable with async/await unlike Thread.

children.title: CancellationToken
children.description: CancellationToken is the standard way to cancel async operations in .NET — pass it through the call chain and check IsCancellationRequested or use ThrowIfCancellationRequested.

children.title: async void — avoid it
children.description: async void methods can't be awaited and swallow exceptions silently — only use it for event handlers. Prefer async Task for everything else.

children.title: Task.WhenAll and Task.WhenAny
children.description: Task.WhenAll runs multiple async operations in parallel and waits for all; Task.WhenAny completes when the first finishes — the async equivalents of Promise.all and Promise.race.

children.title: IHttpClientFactory
children.description: Register IHttpClientFactory in DI instead of newing HttpClient — it manages connection pooling and lifetime, preventing socket exhaustion from too many short-lived HttpClient instances.

children.title: Typed HTTP clients
children.description: A typed client wraps IHttpClientFactory with a strongly typed API for a specific backend — encapsulating base URL, auth headers, and retry policies in one place.

csharp.title: Advanced LINQ and Functional Patterns
csharp.description: Go deeper with LINQ including grouping, joining, and custom operators, and apply functional patterns like immutable records and expression trees. LINQ's composability enables a functional style that integrates naturally with C# OOP.
csharp.challenge: Replace all expense filtering with advanced LINQ pipelines using GroupBy for category reports, switch expressions for expense classification, and immutable record types.

children.title: Custom LINQ operators
children.description: Extension methods on IEnumerable<T> create reusable LINQ operators — yield return enables lazy, composable sequences without loading all data into memory.

children.title: Queryable vs Enumerable
children.description: Enumerable extension methods operate in memory; Queryable methods build expression trees translated to SQL by ORMs — accidentally calling AsEnumerable() too early loads all rows into memory.

children.title: Immutability with records and init
children.description: record and init-only properties enable immutable data models with value semantics — the with expression creates a modified copy without mutating the original.

children.title: Pattern matching switch expressions
children.description: C# 8+ switch expressions with type patterns and when guards enable concise, exhaustive transformation logic without if/else chains.

children.title: Avoiding N+1 queries
children.description: Using Include() for eager loading and selecting only needed columns with Select() prevents the N+1 query problem and large data over-fetching in Entity Framework.

children.title: AsNoTracking for read-only queries
children.description: AsNoTracking() skips change tracking for read-only queries — significantly faster for queries where you don't need to save changes back to the database.

children.title: LanguageExt
children.description: LanguageExt brings Haskell-inspired types (Option, Either, Try) to C# — enabling railway-oriented programming and eliminating null reference exceptions structurally.

children.title: Memoization and lazy evaluation
children.description: Lazy<T> defers expensive initialization until first access and caches the result — the built-in .NET solution for lazy singletons and expensive computations.

csharp.title: Dependency Injection and Architecture Patterns
csharp.description: Understand dependency injection as built into .NET, and apply common architecture patterns (repository, service layer, options pattern) used throughout the .NET ecosystem. DI is not a framework choice in .NET - it is a core platform feature.
csharp.challenge: Refactor the expense tracker to use the .NET DI container with constructor-injected services, a repository interface, and ILogger for structured logging.

children.title: IServiceCollection and IServiceProvider
children.description: IServiceCollection is the registration container; IServiceProvider resolves instances — the entire .NET ecosystem (ASP.NET Core, EF Core, logging) is built around this abstraction.

children.title: Decorator pattern with DI
children.description: Registering a service multiple times lets you wrap implementations with decorators — Scrutor library adds TryDecorate<IService, Decorator>() for cleaner registration.

children.title: Onion / Hexagonal architecture
children.description: Domain logic at the center, infrastructure at the edges — dependencies point inward, making the domain layer framework-agnostic and easily testable.

children.title: CQRS pattern
children.description: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write models — MediatR is the standard .NET library for dispatching commands and queries without direct service references.

children.title: Repository pattern
children.description: Repository wraps data access behind an interface — swapping EF Core for Dapper or an in-memory store for testing requires only a new implementation, not changing business logic.

children.title: Unit of Work
children.description: Unit of Work groups multiple repository operations into a single transaction — EF Core's DbContext already implements this pattern; explicit UoW is only needed for complex multi-context scenarios.

resources.label: Unit of Work Pattern in C#

children.title: ASP.NET Core middleware pipeline
children.description: Middleware components form an ordered pipeline — each calls next() to pass to the next component. Order matters: authentication before authorization, logging before everything.

children.title: MediatR pipeline behaviors
children.description: IPipelineBehavior<TRequest, TResponse> wraps every MediatR request — use it for cross-cutting concerns like validation, logging, and caching without modifying handlers.

csharp.title: Testing with xUnit and Moq
csharp.description: Write unit and integration tests using xUnit, mock dependencies with Moq, and measure coverage. The .NET testing ecosystem is mature and well-integrated with the dotnet CLI and CI systems.
csharp.challenge: Write an xUnit test suite for the expense tracker using Moq for storage dependencies, Theory tests for edge cases, and FluentAssertions for readable assertions.

children.title: xUnit design philosophy
children.description: xUnit was designed with testability in mind — no [SetUp]/[TearDown], just constructor/IDisposable. Each test gets a fresh instance, preventing shared state bugs.

children.title: Test isolation
children.description: Tests should be isolated — no shared state between test methods. Use fresh instances, in-memory databases, and mock dependencies to ensure tests are deterministic.

resources.label: Unit Testing Best Practices in C#

children.title: Castle DynamicProxy
children.description: Moq uses Castle DynamicProxy to generate mock implementations at runtime — this is why you can only mock virtual methods and interfaces; sealed/non-virtual methods can't be intercepted.

children.title: Setup and Verify
children.description: Setup() configures mock return values and behaviors; Verify() asserts that a method was called with specific arguments — both use lambda expressions for type-safe configuration.

children.title: FluentAssertions
children.description: FluentAssertions replaces Assert.Equal with result.Should().Be(expected) — producing human-readable failure messages with rich diff output for collections and objects.

children.title: AutoFixture for test data
children.description: AutoFixture generates anonymous test data automatically — eliminating manual test object construction and keeping tests focused on the behavior being tested.

children.title: WebApplicationFactory
children.description: WebApplicationFactory<Program> creates an in-memory test server for ASP.NET Core — enables full integration tests against real HTTP endpoints without a running server.

children.title: Respawn for database cleanup
children.description: Respawn resets a test database to a clean state between tests by intelligently deleting rows in dependency order — faster than recreating the schema for each test.

csharp.title: Modern C# Features - Nullable Reference Types, Spans, and Channels
csharp.description: Enable nullable reference types to eliminate null reference exceptions at compile time, use Span<T> for allocation-free data processing, and use System.Threading.Channels for async producer-consumer patterns. These features characterize modern, performance-conscious C#.
csharp.challenge: Enable nullable reference types across the expense tracker with zero warnings, add an IAsyncEnumerable export stream, and use Channels for a background notification pipeline.

children.title: Nullable reference types (C# 8+)
children.description: Enabling <Nullable>enable</Nullable> makes the compiler track null flow — string is non-nullable by default, string? is nullable, eliminating most NullReferenceExceptions at compile time.

children.title: Span<T> and Memory<T>
children.description: Span<T> is a stack-only slice over any contiguous memory (arrays, stack, unmanaged) — enables zero-copy string parsing and buffer manipulation without heap allocation.

children.title: System.Threading.Channels
children.description: Channel<T> provides a bounded or unbounded async-friendly producer-consumer queue — the modern replacement for BlockingCollection<T> in async pipelines.

children.title: IAsyncEnumerable<T>
children.description: async foreach iterates over an IAsyncEnumerable<T> — the async-native way to stream results from a database or API without buffering the entire result set.

children.title: ArrayPool<T>
children.description: ArrayPool<T>.Shared.Rent(size) returns a pooled array and Shared.Return() gives it back — eliminating GC pressure for hot paths that repeatedly allocate temporary arrays.

children.title: ref struct and stack allocation
children.description: ref struct types (like Span<T>) can only live on the stack — preventing heap allocation and GC interaction entirely for temporary high-performance operations.

children.title: Compile-time code generation
children.description: Source generators add code to a compilation at compile time — used by System.Text.Json, regex, and logging for zero-overhead serialization and pattern matching.

children.title: Incremental generators
children.description: Incremental source generators only re-run when their inputs change — critical for IDE performance, as classic generators re-ran on every keystroke.

csharp.title: Production C# - NuGet, Performance, and Ecosystem
csharp.description: Publish NuGet packages, profile .NET apps with dotnet-trace and BenchmarkDotNet, and survey the .NET ecosystem for web, desktop, and cloud development. Production .NET requires understanding the full platform beyond the language itself.
topics: Ecosystem overview (ASP.NET Core, EF Core, MAUI, Blazor)
csharp.challenge: Package the expense tracker core library as a NuGet package, run BenchmarkDotNet benchmarks on LINQ queries, publish results, and add a GitHub Actions workflow with test and publish steps.

children.title: Publishing modes
children.description: dotnet publish -c Release --self-contained true --runtime linux-x64 creates a self-contained executable; -p:PublishSingleFile=true bundles everything into one binary.

children.title: Native AOT compilation
children.description: .NET 7+ Native AOT compiles directly to native code at build time — instant startup, minimal memory, and no JIT overhead. Required for some performance-critical and embedded scenarios.

children.title: dotnet-trace and dotnet-counters
children.description: dotnet-trace collects CPU and allocation profiles; dotnet-counters monitors live metrics — both work on running processes without restarting or modifying the application.

children.title: BenchmarkDotNet
children.description: BenchmarkDotNet is the standard .NET micro-benchmarking library — it handles warm-up, statistical analysis, and hardware counter measurements for reliable performance comparisons.

children.title: ASP.NET Core
children.description: ASP.NET Core is the cross-platform web framework — minimal APIs for microservices, MVC for full-stack apps, SignalR for real-time, and gRPC for high-performance RPC.

children.title: Entity Framework Core
children.description: EF Core is the ORM for .NET — code-first migrations, LINQ queries translated to SQL, and support for SQL Server, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and MySQL.

children.title: GitHub Actions for .NET
children.description: actions/setup-dotnet installs the SDK; dotnet restore, build, test, and publish form the standard CI pipeline with NuGet caching for fast builds.

children.title: Docker for .NET apps
children.description: Multi-stage builds with the official mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk builder and mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet runtime image produce lean production containers.

ruby.title: Ruby Syntax and the Ruby Way
ruby.description: Install Ruby with rbenv or rvm, learn the fundamental syntax, and begin developing a feel for Ruby's philosophy of developer happiness and expressiveness. Ruby is designed to be read like natural language - idiomatic Ruby embraces this.
ruby.challenge: Build a command-line quiz game in Ruby that reads questions from a YAML file, tracks score, and runs in a tight game loop.

children.title: Principle of Least Surprise
children.description: Ruby is designed to minimize surprise for experienced programmers — Matz optimized for developer happiness, meaning code reads like natural language and behaves predictably.

children.title: Everything is an object
children.description: In Ruby, every value including integers, booleans, and nil is an object with methods — 1.class returns Integer, nil.nil? returns true, true.&(false) works fine.

resources.label: Everything is an Object in Ruby

children.title: Duck typing
children.description: Ruby checks if an object responds to a method, not what class it belongs to — respond_to?(:each) is the Ruby way to test capability rather than inheritance.

children.title: Open classes
children.description: Any class in Ruby can be reopened and modified at runtime — adding methods to String or Integer is valid and widely used (though dangerous if overused).

children.title: rbenv and version management
children.description: rbenv manages multiple Ruby versions per project via .ruby-version — use it alongside bundler so every project has isolated, reproducible gem dependencies.

children.title: IRB and Pry
children.description: IRB is Ruby's built-in REPL; Pry adds syntax highlighting, navigation, and debugging capabilities — binding.pry drops into a REPL at any point in your code.

children.title: RuboCop for linting
children.description: RuboCop enforces the community Ruby style guide with auto-correct — configure it with .rubocop.yml to adopt the standard gradually without rewriting everything at once.

children.title: Idiomatic Ruby patterns
children.description: Prefer unless over if !condition, use symbol-to-proc (&:method), favour map/select/reduce over loops — these patterns signal Ruby fluency.

ruby.title: Collections - Arrays, Hashes, and Enumerables
ruby.description: Master Ruby arrays, hashes, and the Enumerable module which provides an incredibly rich set of collection methods. Enumerable is one of the most elegant parts of the Ruby language and is used throughout idiomatic Ruby code.
ruby.challenge: Add leaderboard tracking to the quiz game using hashes, sort and filter results with Enumerable methods, and export scores to a CSV using map.

children.title: Enumerable module
children.description: Including Enumerable in any class that implements each gives it 50+ methods (map, select, sort, group_by, etc.) — this is how Ruby's mixin system provides behaviour composition.

children.title: Lazy enumerators
children.description: array.lazy.map { }.select { }.first(5) evaluates only enough elements to satisfy the request — essential for working with infinite sequences or large data sets.

children.title: Symbol vs string keys
children.description: Symbols are immutable and interned — the same symbol object is reused everywhere, making them more memory-efficient than strings as hash keys.

children.title: Hash as keyword arguments
children.description: Ruby 2.0+ uses hashes as keyword arguments; Ruby 3.0 made the separation strict — understanding the distinction prevents ArgumentError in modern Ruby.

children.title: map, select, reduce, flat_map
children.description: These four methods cover most data transformation needs — map transforms, select filters, reduce aggregates, flat_map transforms and flattens in one pass.

children.title: group_by and tally
children.description: group_by partitions a collection by a computed key; tally (Ruby 2.7+) counts occurrences — both replace common manual reduce patterns.

children.title: Range objects
children.description: (1..10) creates an inclusive range; (1...10) excludes the end — ranges work with integers, strings, and any Comparable object, and include the Enumerable module.

children.title: Endless and beginless ranges
children.description: (1..) and (..10) are Ruby 2.6+ endless/beginless ranges — used in case expressions and array slicing without needing Float::INFINITY.

ruby.title: Blocks, Procs, and Lambdas
ruby.description: Understand Ruby blocks as first-class constructs, the difference between Proc and Lambda, and how to write methods that yield to blocks. Blocks are the foundation of Ruby's DSL capabilities and iterative APIs.
ruby.challenge: Refactor the quiz game to use blocks for question rendering, add a timing feature using yield with a timer block, and create a configurable scoring lambda.

children.title: yield and block_given?
children.description: yield passes control to the caller's block; block_given? checks if one was provided — the foundation of Ruby's iterator and DSL patterns.

children.title: Explicit block capture with &
children.description: def method(&block) captures the block as a Proc object — store it, pass it to other methods, or call it later with block.call or block.().

children.title: Return behavior difference
children.description: return inside a Proc returns from the enclosing method; return inside a Lambda returns from the lambda only — this makes lambdas safer for use as callbacks.

children.title: Arity checking
children.description: Lambdas enforce the correct number of arguments (like methods); Procs silently accept wrong arity — use lambdas when argument count correctness matters.

children.title: Symbol to proc (&:method)
children.description: [1,2,3].map(&:to_s) converts the :to_s symbol to a Proc calling to_s on each element — a concise shorthand for single-method lambdas.

children.title: Enumerator and external iteration
children.description: enum = [1,2,3].each creates an Enumerator — call enum.next manually for external iteration, or pass it to methods expecting a block.

children.title: Method objects
children.description: method(:puts) returns a Method object that wraps a method as a callable — enables passing methods where blocks are expected without a wrapper lambda.

children.title: Curry for partial application
children.description: add = ->(a,b){ a+b }; add5 = add.curry.(5) — curry returns a new lambda with some arguments pre-filled, enabling partial application.

ruby.title: Object-Oriented Ruby
ruby.description: Build Ruby classes with the initialize method, attr_accessor, modules for mixins, and understand Ruby's open class model. Ruby OOP is dynamic and flexible - classes can be reopened and modified at any time.
ruby.challenge: Refactor the quiz game into a full OOP design with Quiz, Question, Player, and Leaderboard classes, using the Comparable mixin for player ranking.

children.title: Method lookup path
children.description: Ruby searches the object's singleton class, then its class, then each included module (in reverse inclusion order), then the superclass chain — understanding this prevents method resolution surprises.

children.title: Singleton classes
children.description: Every Ruby object has a singleton class (eigenclass) that holds object-specific methods — class methods on a class are instance methods of that class's singleton class.

children.title: include vs extend vs prepend
children.description: include adds module methods as instance methods; extend adds them as class methods on the receiver; prepend inserts the module before the class in the lookup path for method wrapping.

children.title: Comparable and Enumerable modules
children.description: Including Comparable and implementing <=> gives you <, >, <=, >=, between?, and clamp for free. Including Enumerable and implementing each gives 50+ collection methods.

children.title: attr_accessor, attr_reader, attr_writer
children.description: These macros generate getter/setter methods — attr_accessor :name defines both name and name=, eliminating boilerplate instance variable accessors.

children.title: Struct for data classes
children.description: Struct.new(:name, :age) creates a class with accessors, ==, to_s, and to_a for free — lighter than a full class for simple value objects.

children.title: super keyword
children.description: super calls the same-named method in the parent class — super with no args passes the current method's args, super() passes none, super(args) passes explicit args.

children.title: method_missing and respond_to_missing?
children.description: method_missing intercepts calls to undefined methods — powerful for DSLs and proxies, but always implement respond_to_missing? alongside it for correct respond_to? behavior.

ruby.title: Metaprogramming Fundamentals
ruby.description: Begin exploring Ruby's metaprogramming capabilities - method_missing, define_method, send, and class macros - which enable Ruby's expressive DSLs and frameworks. Metaprogramming is where Ruby becomes truly unique.
ruby.challenge: Add a DSL to the quiz game that allows defining questions declaratively using class macros (e.g., question 'What is...?' do ... end) powered by define_method.

children.title: define_method and class_eval
children.description: define_method(:name) { body } creates a method dynamically at runtime; class_eval evaluates a string or block in the context of the class — the building blocks of all Ruby DSLs.

children.title: send and public_send
children.description: send(:method_name, args) calls any method including private ones; public_send respects visibility — both enable dynamic dispatch when the method name is only known at runtime.

children.title: included, extended, prepended hooks
children.description: These hooks fire when a module is mixed in — used by frameworks (ActiveSupport, Devise) to set up class-level configuration when a module is included.

children.title: inherited and method_added hooks
children.description: inherited(subclass) fires when a class is subclassed; method_added(name) fires when a method is defined — both enable framework-level introspection and automation.

children.title: instance_eval for DSLs
children.description: instance_eval executes a block in the context of an object — enabling the clean { } DSL syntax used by frameworks like RSpec and Rake.

children.title: Proc and block-based configuration
children.description: Accepting a block for configuration (MyClass.new { |c| c.option = value }) is idiomatic Ruby for optional setup — popular in test frameworks and gem configuration.

children.title: instance_variables and methods
children.description: instance_variables returns all @ variables; methods returns all available methods; instance_method(:name) retrieves an unbound method object for inspection.

children.title: ObjectSpace for runtime introspection
children.description: ObjectSpace.each_object(ClassName) iterates over all live instances of a class — used by test frameworks and memory profilers to inspect the object graph.

ruby.title: Exception Handling and File I/O
ruby.description: Handle exceptions idiomatically in Ruby using rescue, ensure, retry, and custom exception classes, and read and write files and JSON. Ruby exception handling is concise - many operations have built-in rescue shorthand.
ruby.challenge: Add JSON persistence to the quiz game leaderboard with custom exceptions for file corruption, retry logic for transient I/O errors, and ensure for cleanup.

children.title: Exception vs StandardError
children.description: rescue with no class catches StandardError and below — never rescue Exception as it catches SignalException and SystemExit, preventing clean shutdown.

children.title: ensure and else clauses
children.description: ensure runs whether or not an exception was raised (like finally); else runs only when no exception was raised — both complement rescue in begin/end blocks.

children.title: IO and File hierarchy
children.description: File inherits from IO — all IO methods (read, write, gets, puts, seek) are available on File objects. File.open with a block auto-closes the file via ensure.

children.title: StringIO for in-memory streams
children.description: StringIO acts as a File-compatible IO object backed by a String — useful for testing code that writes to IO without creating actual files.

children.title: retry in rescue blocks
children.description: retry re-executes the begin block after a rescue — use with a counter to limit retries for transient errors like network timeouts.

children.title: raise with custom exceptions
children.description: Create domain exceptions with class OrderNotFoundError < StandardError; end — rescue OrderNotFoundError is more precise than rescuing all StandardErrors.

children.title: File.read, write, and foreach
children.description: File.read(path) returns the whole file as a string; File.foreach(path) iterates line by line without loading everything into memory — choose based on file size.

children.title: Pathname for path manipulation
children.description: Pathname wraps path strings with a rich API (/, +, glob, children, exist?) — cleaner than string concatenation for file path manipulation.

ruby.title: Gems and Bundler
ruby.description: Create and publish Ruby gems, manage dependencies with Bundler and Gemfile, and understand gem versioning. The Ruby gem ecosystem is vast - learning to navigate it and create gems yourself is essential.
topics: gem dependencies and runtime vs development
ruby.challenge: Package the quiz game as a gem with a Gemfile, gemspec, and a command-line binary, and publish it to a local gem server.

children.title: Gem structure and gemspec
children.description: A gem is a packaged Ruby library with a .gemspec defining its name, version, dependencies, and files — understanding the structure is essential for creating and maintaining gems.

children.title: Load path and require
children.description: require adds a file to the load path and executes it once; require_relative loads relative to the current file — Bundler manages the load path so gem requires work in any environment.

children.title: Gemfile and Gemfile.lock
children.description: Gemfile declares gem dependencies with version constraints; Gemfile.lock pins exact resolved versions — commit Gemfile.lock for applications, but not for libraries.

children.title: bundle exec
children.description: bundle exec runs a command in the context of the current bundle — ensures the correct gem versions are loaded rather than whatever is installed globally.

children.title: Faraday and HTTParty for HTTP
children.description: Faraday is a composable HTTP client with middleware support; HTTParty is simpler for straightforward API clients — both are widely used alternatives to net/http.

children.title: Dry-rb ecosystem
children.description: dry-validation, dry-types, and dry-monads bring functional programming concepts to Ruby — popular for building robust, well-typed service objects in non-Rails contexts.

children.title: Semantic versioning for gems
children.description: Follow semver strictly — patch for bug fixes, minor for new features, major for breaking changes. Users rely on ~> constraints which only allow patch/minor updates.

children.title: rake release and gem push
children.description: rake release tags the version in git and pushes to rubygems.org; configure trusted publishing with GitHub Actions for automated, secure releases.

ruby.title: Testing with RSpec
ruby.description: Write expressive, behavior-driven tests using RSpec with describe, context, and it blocks, and mock with RSpec mocks or Mocha. RSpec's DSL makes tests read almost like documentation.
ruby.challenge: Write a complete RSpec suite for the quiz game covering all classes, using doubles for file I/O, shared examples for game rules, and SimpleCov for coverage reporting.

children.title: describe, context, and it blocks
children.description: describe groups tests by subject; context groups by scenario; it describes expected behavior — three levels of nesting produce readable, hierarchical test output.

children.title: Subject and let memoization
children.description: let(:name) { expr } creates a memoized lazy helper — evaluated once per example and reset between examples. let! forces eager evaluation; subject is the implicit test target.

children.title: Mocks vs stubs vs spies
children.description: double creates a strict mock that only responds to configured messages; allow sets up stubs; expect sets up message expectations — RSpec's terminology matches Fowler's definitions.

children.title: instance_double and verified doubles
children.description: instance_double(:ClassName) verifies that stubbed methods actually exist on the class — prevents tests from silently passing after a method rename.

children.title: Shared examples
children.description: shared_examples_for 'a collection' { } defines reusable test groups — include them with it_behaves_like to verify that multiple classes fulfill the same contract.

children.title: FactoryBot for test data
children.description: FactoryBot defines factory templates for ActiveRecord models — create(:user, role: :admin) produces a valid instance without specifying every attribute.

children.title: DatabaseCleaner for test isolation
children.description: DatabaseCleaner resets the database between tests using truncation or transaction strategies — essential for keeping integration tests independent and repeatable.

children.title: VCR for HTTP interactions
children.description: VCR records real HTTP interactions to cassette files and replays them in tests — eliminating flaky tests that depend on external APIs being available.

ruby.title: Advanced Metaprogramming and DSL Design
ruby.description: Build internal DSLs using instance_eval, class_eval, and the full suite of Ruby metaprogramming tools. Understanding how Rails, RSpec, and Sinatra build their DSLs will make you a far more capable Ruby developer.
ruby.challenge: Build a configuration DSL for the quiz game using instance_eval so game settings can be expressed as a clean Ruby block without hash syntax.

children.title: BasicObject and blank slate
children.description: BasicObject has almost no methods — subclassing it creates a 'blank slate' object ideal for proxies and DSLs where inherited methods like class or inspect would interfere.

children.title: Kernel methods and mixins
children.description: Kernel is mixed into Object — methods like puts, p, require, and lambda are actually Kernel instance methods available everywhere, not top-level functions.

children.title: Fluent interfaces with method chaining
children.description: Returning self from methods enables chaining — query.where(active: true).order(:name).limit(10). This pattern is used by ActiveRecord, Arel, and most Ruby query builders.

children.title: Refinements for scoped monkey patching
children.description: Refinements activate monkey patches only within a specific scope (using MyRefinement) — safer than global open classes for adding methods to core types.

children.title: Frozen string literals
children.description: # frozen_string_literal: true at the top of a file makes all string literals immutable — reducing object allocations significantly in hot paths.

children.title: StackProf for CPU profiling
children.description: StackProf is a sampling profiler for Ruby — run your code with StackProf.run { } and call .report to see which methods consume the most CPU time.

children.title: Rails vs Sinatra vs Hanami
children.description: Rails is full-stack and convention-heavy; Sinatra is minimal for small APIs; Hanami is a clean-architecture alternative — choose based on project scale and team conventions.

children.title: Concurrent-ruby for threading
children.description: concurrent-ruby provides thread-safe data structures (Concurrent::Array, Concurrent::Hash) and higher-level concurrency primitives (Future, Promise) for production Ruby apps.

ruby.title: Production Ruby - Concurrency, Performance, and Ecosystem
ruby.description: Understand Ruby's GIL and concurrency options (threads, Ractors, async gem), profile Ruby code with ruby-prof and stackprof, and survey the production Ruby ecosystem. Ruby's production maturity is demonstrated by decades of Rails applications running at scale.
ruby.challenge: Add concurrent question fetching to the quiz game using Ruby threads with Mutex protection, profile the hot path with stackprof, and document an upgrade path to Ractors.

children.title: GIL and thread limitations
children.description: MRI Ruby has a Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) that prevents true parallel thread execution — threads are useful for I/O-bound work but not CPU-bound parallelism. Use Ractors or JRuby for CPU parallelism.

children.title: Ractors for true parallelism
children.description: Ractors (Ruby 3.0+) are actor-model concurrency units that run in parallel without the GIL — each Ractor has isolated state, communicating only via message passing.

children.title: MJIT and YJIT compilers
children.description: YJIT (Ruby 3.1+, written in Rust) is a lazy basic block versioning JIT compiler — delivers 20-40% speedups on real Rails applications with no code changes required.

children.title: memory_profiler and derailed_benchmarks
children.description: memory_profiler tracks per-line allocations; derailed_benchmarks measures memory usage of Rails apps at startup and per request — the standard toolkit for Ruby memory optimization.

children.title: Sidekiq for background jobs
children.description: Sidekiq processes background jobs using Redis and threads — the standard Ruby background job library with a web UI, retries, and scheduling built in.

children.title: Puma for web serving
children.description: Puma is the default Rails web server — multi-threaded, handles concurrent requests efficiently, and supports clustered mode for multi-process deployment.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Ruby
children.description: actions/setup-ruby with a .ruby-version file and bundle cache gives you a fast, reproducible CI pipeline — add rubocop and rspec jobs to catch style and logic issues on every PR.

children.title: Docker for Ruby apps
children.description: Multi-stage builds with ruby:slim images produce lean production containers — use BUNDLE_DEPLOYMENT=true to install only production gems in the final image.

php.title: PHP Syntax and Basics
php.description: Learn PHP syntax, data types, operators, and control flow structures. Understand how PHP scripts execute in a server context.
php.challenge: Build a PHP script that reads a list of student names and scores from an array, calculates the average, and prints a formatted report.

children.title: Mental models and first principles
children.description: Strong programming languages practitioners build mental models before touching a keyboard. Understanding the why behind php syntax and basics lets you adapt when tools change.

children.description: Every technology in programming languages makes specific trade-offs. Knowing what was sacrificed for what helps you choose the right tool and avoid footguns.

children.description: Every component should have exactly the permissions it needs — no more. Scoping permissions tightly limits the blast radius when any component is compromised.

php.description: Harden your PHP application against common vulnerabilities including XSS, CSRF, and injection. Apply caching strategies to improve throughput.

resources.label: Survive the Deep End: PHP Security

php.challenge: Audit your project for OWASP Top 10 issues, add CSRF protection to all forms, hash passwords, and implement Redis caching on expensive queries.

children.title: Mental models and first principles
children.description: Strong programming languages practitioners build mental models before touching a keyboard. Understanding the why behind security and performance lets you adapt when tools change.

children.title: Key abstractions and trade-offs
children.description: Every technology in programming languages makes specific trade-offs. Knowing what was sacrificed for what helps you choose the right tool and avoid footguns.

children.title: Ecosystem toolchain
children.description: The programming languages ecosystem has converged on specific toolchains for this layer. Mastering them removes friction so you can focus on solving problems rather than fighting infrastructure.

children.title: Debugging and observability
children.description: Production issues are inevitable. Instrumenting your system before things break — logs, metrics, traces — determines how fast you can diagnose and recover.

children.title: Security considerations
children.description: Security in programming languages is not a feature to add later — it must be designed in. Understanding the threat model for this layer prevents the most common breaches.

children.title: Principle of least privilege
children.description: Every component should have exactly the permissions it needs — no more. Scoping permissions tightly limits the blast radius when any component is compromised.

Background queues with database or Redis
php.challenge: Deploy your complete project as a Dockerized PHP-FPM and Nginx stack with a REST API, background job queue, Redis caching, and a CI pipeline that runs your PHPUnit suite on every push.
