children.title: Portfolio and practice
children.description: The fastest path to proficiency in programming languages is solving Advent of Code and exercism.io challenges. Real feedback from users and reviewers surfaces gaps that no tutorial can.

c.title: C Syntax and Compilation
c.description: Learn C syntax, primitive types, operators, and the compilation pipeline from source to executable. Write and compile your first programs using GCC.
topics: Control flow: if, for, while, switch
Standard I/O with printf and scanf
c.challenge: Write a C program that reads integers from stdin, computes min, max, and average, then prints a formatted summary.

children.title: Preprocessor, compiler, assembler, linker
children.description: C source goes through four stages: the preprocessor expands macros and #includes, the compiler produces assembly, the assembler produces object files, and the linker combines them into an executable.

children.title: Translation units and linkage
children.description: Each .c file is a separate translation unit compiled independently — external linkage (default for functions/globals) makes symbols visible across units; static restricts them to the current file.

children.title: Trust the programmer
children.description: C gives you direct memory access, pointer arithmetic, and manual resource management — it assumes you know what you're doing and provides no safety nets by default.

children.title: Portable assembly
children.description: C maps closely to machine operations — assignments, arithmetic, and pointer dereferences correspond directly to CPU instructions, making C performance predictable and measurable.

children.title: GCC and Clang flags
children.description: Essential flags: -std=c17, -Wall -Wextra -Werror (warnings as errors), -O2 (optimize), -g (debug info), -fsanitize=address (ASan) — always compile with warnings enabled.

children.title: Make basics
children.description: A minimal Makefile with CC, CFLAGS, and a rule for each .o file handles most C project builds — make tracks dependencies and only recompiles changed files.

children.title: VS Code with clangd
children.description: clangd provides C-aware autocomplete, diagnostics, and go-to-definition — it reads compile_commands.json (generated by CMake or bear) for accurate project-wide analysis.

children.title: Compiler Explorer for C
children.description: godbolt.org shows the assembly output of any C snippet across GCC, Clang, and MSVC at different optimization levels — essential for understanding what your code compiles to.

c.title: Pointers and Memory Model
c.description: Understand the C memory model, pointer arithmetic, and the relationship between arrays and pointers. Write functions that operate on data via pointers.
c.challenge: Extend your statistics program to process data through pointer-based functions, passing arrays by pointer and returning results via output parameters.

children.title: Stack, heap, BSS, and data segments
children.description: C programs have four memory regions: stack (local variables, LIFO), heap (dynamic allocation), data segment (initialized globals), and BSS (zero-initialized globals) — knowing which region holds what prevents common bugs.

children.title: Pointer size and representation
children.description: A pointer holds a memory address — its size is 4 bytes on 32-bit systems, 8 bytes on 64-bit. Pointer arithmetic increments by sizeof(pointed-to type), not by 1 byte.

children.title: Dereferencing NULL and dangling pointers
children.description: Accessing memory through a NULL or freed pointer is undefined behavior — the program may crash, silently corrupt data, or behave differently under optimization.

children.title: Buffer overflows
children.description: Writing past the end of a stack or heap buffer overwrites adjacent memory — source of security vulnerabilities (stack smashing, heap corruption) and the #1 C bug class.

resources.label: Smashing the Stack for Fun and Profit

children.title: Valgrind for memory errors
children.description: valgrind --leak-check=full detects heap leaks, use-after-free, and invalid reads/writes at runtime — run it on every C program that does dynamic memory allocation.

children.title: AddressSanitizer (ASan)
children.description: Compile with -fsanitize=address to catch buffer overflows, use-after-free, and stack corruption at runtime with minimal overhead — faster feedback than Valgrind.

children.title: Pointer to pointer
children.description: char **argv is a pointer to a pointer — used for arrays of strings, output parameters, and linked data structures where you need to modify a pointer itself.

children.title: Function pointers
children.description: void (*callback)(int) declares a pointer to a function taking int — used for callbacks, dispatch tables, and implementing polymorphism in C without a class system.

c.title: Strings and Standard Library
c.description: Work with C strings as char arrays and use the standard library for string manipulation, memory allocation, and sorting. Understand null-termination and buffer safety.
c.challenge: Add string-based input to your project: read a CSV of names and scores, parse with sscanf, dynamically allocate storage, and sort entries by score using qsort.

children.title: Null-terminated strings
children.description: C strings are arrays of char terminated by '\0' — no length field is stored, so every string operation (strlen, strcpy) must scan for the null terminator, making O(n) a hidden cost.

children.title: String literals and const char*
children.description: String literals live in read-only memory — char *s = "hello"; should be const char *s. Modifying a string literal is undefined behavior; modifying a char array copy is fine.

children.title: errno for error signaling
children.description: Standard library functions signal errors via errno (a thread-local global) and return -1 or NULL — always check the return value and errno immediately after a failing call.

children.title: Header-only declarations
children.description: C standard library headers declare functions but don't define them — the definitions live in libc, linked automatically. Understanding this explains why you include <string.h> but link nothing explicitly.

children.title: Safe string functions
children.description: strncpy, strncat, snprintf are the safer alternatives to strcpy, strcat, sprintf — always pass the buffer size to prevent overflows. strlcpy/strlcat are even safer on BSD/macOS.

children.title: strtol and strtod for parsing
children.description: strtol converts strings to integers with error detection via endptr and errno — safer than atoi which silently returns 0 on failure.

children.title: qsort and bsearch
children.description: qsort sorts any array with a custom comparator function pointer; bsearch performs binary search — both work on any data type through void* and size parameters.

children.title: malloc, calloc, realloc, free
children.description: malloc allocates uninitialized memory; calloc zero-initializes; realloc resizes — always check for NULL return and always free every allocated block exactly once.

c.title: Structs, Unions, and Enums
c.description: Group related data using structs, model variant types with unions, and improve readability with enums and typedef. Build linked data structures.
c.challenge: Refactor your data model into structs representing students and score records, link them in a singly-linked list, and implement insert and search operations.

children.title: Struct padding and alignment
children.description: The compiler inserts padding between struct members to satisfy alignment requirements — a struct with char, int can be 8 bytes, not 5. Use offsetof() to measure layout.

children.title: Flexible array members
children.description: struct { int len; char data[]; } uses a flexible array member — the array has no size in the struct definition, and malloc(sizeof(struct) + n) allocates n extra bytes for it.

children.title: Union memory layout
children.description: All union members share the same memory — a union is as large as its largest member. Used for type-punning, variant types, and memory-efficient data representation.

children.title: Tagged unions for sum types
children.description: Combining a union with an enum tag creates a discriminated union (variant type) — the standard C pattern for representing values that can be one of several types.

children.title: Opaque pointers for encapsulation
children.description: Exposing only typedef struct MyType* MyType in a header (with implementation in the .c file) hides the struct layout — callers can't access fields directly, enforcing encapsulation.

children.title: Designated initializers
children.description: struct Point p = { .x = 1.0, .y = 2.0 } initializes by field name — safer than positional initialization, immune to reordering bugs, and self-documenting.

children.title: Bit field structs
children.description: struct Flags { unsigned int ready: 1; unsigned int error: 1; } packs multiple flags into a single int — used in hardware registers, network protocol headers, and memory-constrained embedded systems.

children.title: __attribute__((packed))
children.description: GCC's packed attribute removes all struct padding — useful for serialization and hardware register mapping where layout must exactly match a specification.

c.title: File I/O and Error Handling
c.description: Read and write files using FILE streams and handle errors robustly using errno and return-code conventions. Implement resource cleanup on all exit paths.
topics: Cleanup with goto or nested ifs
c.challenge: Persist your linked list to a binary file, reload it on startup, and handle all fopen and fread errors with descriptive messages and clean exits.

children.title: FILE* and buffered I/O
children.description: FILE* is a buffered I/O stream — fread/fwrite transfer data through an internal buffer, batching syscalls for performance. fflush forces the buffer to the OS.

children.title: Binary vs text mode
children.description: Text mode translates line endings (\r\n on Windows); binary mode transfers bytes unchanged — always use binary mode for non-text data to prevent silent corruption.

children.title: Return value conventions
children.description: C functions signal errors through return values — 0 for success (or -1 for failure) for most functions, NULL for pointer-returning functions. Always check every return value.

children.title: setjmp/longjmp for non-local jumps
children.description: setjmp saves the stack state; longjmp restores it — used for error recovery in C (analogous to exceptions) but dangerous: longjmp skips destructors and resource cleanup.

children.title: fseek, ftell, rewind
children.description: fseek positions the file cursor; ftell returns the current position; rewind resets to the beginning — used together for random access within files.

children.title: POSIX file descriptors vs FILE*
children.description: POSIX open/read/write/close operate on integer file descriptors (unbuffered); FILE* wraps them with buffering — use FILE* for most file operations, fd for sockets and low-level I/O.

children.title: perror and strerror
children.description: perror("context") prints the error message for the current errno to stderr; strerror(errno) returns it as a string — always use them to give context to error messages.

children.title: Cleanup on error with goto
children.description: The goto cleanup pattern — allocate resources, jump to a cleanup label on any error — is the idiomatic C way to ensure all resources are freed without deeply nested if checks.

c.title: Makefiles and Build Systems
c.description: Organize a multi-file C project with header files and a Makefile that supports incremental builds, compiler warnings, and release and debug targets.
topics: Makefile rules, variables, and phony targets
c.challenge: Split your project into modules (io, list, stats) each with its own .c and .h file, and write a Makefile with debug and release targets and automatic dependency tracking.

children.title: Rules, targets, and prerequisites
children.description: A Makefile rule has the form target: prerequisites; recipe — Make rebuilds a target only when a prerequisite is newer, tracking dependencies through file modification times.

children.title: Automatic variables
children.description: $@ is the target name, $< is the first prerequisite, $^ is all prerequisites — these variables make generic pattern rules reusable across all source files.

children.title: Optimization levels -O0 to -O3
children.description: -O0 no optimization (debug); -O1 basic optimizations; -O2 standard production; -O3 aggressive (may increase size). -Os optimizes for size, -Og for debug-friendly optimization.

children.title: Link-time optimization (LTO)
children.description: -flto enables link-time optimization — the linker can inline and optimize across translation unit boundaries, catching dead code that intra-file analysis misses.

children.title: Dependency generation with -MMD
children.description: gcc -MMD -MP generates .d files containing header dependencies — include them in the Makefile so changing a header triggers recompilation of all dependent .c files.

children.title: CMake for cross-platform builds
children.description: CMake generates native build files (Makefile, Ninja, Visual Studio) from a platform-independent CMakeLists.txt — the standard for C projects that need to build on multiple platforms.

children.title: Include guards and #pragma once
children.description: #ifndef MY_HEADER_H / #define MY_HEADER_H / #endif prevents double-inclusion of headers — #pragma once is simpler and supported by all major compilers.

children.title: X-macros for code generation
children.description: X-macros use a macro list to generate repetitive code (enum values + string names + switch cases) from a single source — eliminating out-of-sync boilerplate.

c.title: Debugging with gdb and Valgrind
c.description: Debug C programs using gdb for step-through inspection and Valgrind for memory error detection. Eliminate leaks, use-after-free, and buffer overruns.
c.challenge: Intentionally introduce a memory bug into your project, then use Valgrind and gdb to locate and fix it, documenting each step.

children.title: How gdb works
children.description: gdb attaches to a process via ptrace, sets breakpoints by replacing instructions with INT3 (trap), and reads program state through /proc — understanding this helps you use gdb effectively.

children.title: Debug information and DWARF
children.description: The -g flag embeds DWARF debug info in the binary — source line numbers, variable names, and type information that gdb uses to map machine instructions back to C source.

children.title: Valgrind internals
children.description: Valgrind runs your program on a synthetic CPU that shadows every memory operation — it tracks which bytes are initialized and allocated, catching errors that ASan might miss.

children.title: Core dumps for post-mortem debugging
children.description: A core dump captures the full process state at crash time — gdb program core loads it for post-mortem analysis, examining the stack trace and variable values at the moment of failure.

children.title: Essential gdb commands
children.description: break, run, next, step, continue, print, backtrace, watch — these ten commands cover 95% of interactive debugging sessions in C.

children.title: TUI mode and gdb dashboard
children.description: gdb -tui shows source alongside commands; gdb-dashboard is a Python extension that adds a rich terminal UI with registers, memory, and variables — both dramatically improve debugging speed.

children.title: UBSan for undefined behavior
children.description: -fsanitize=undefined catches integer overflow, null dereference, misaligned access, and dozens of other UB cases at runtime — run alongside ASan for comprehensive coverage.

children.title: ThreadSanitizer for data races
children.description: -fsanitize=thread detects data races in multithreaded C programs — instruments all memory accesses and reports races with the thread IDs and stack traces involved.

c.title: Dynamic Data Structures and Algorithms
c.description: Implement common data structures from scratch in C: dynamic arrays, hash tables, and binary trees. Analyze time and space complexity of each.
c.challenge: Replace the linked list in your project with a hash table keyed by student ID, and add a binary search tree for sorted traversal by score.

children.title: Intrusive linked lists
children.description: Embedding a struct node { struct node *next; } directly in data structs (instead of a node wrapping a void*) avoids double allocation — used in the Linux kernel and many embedded systems.

children.title: Hash table design
children.description: A simple open-addressing hash table needs a hash function, a probe sequence for collisions, and a load factor limit for rehashing — understanding these choices explains STL and Redis internals.

children.title: void* for type-erased generics
children.description: C achieves generics with void* — qsort and bsearch use void* with a size parameter and function pointer comparator. The caller casts to the correct type, accepting the loss of type safety.

children.title: _Generic for type-safe dispatch
children.description: C11's _Generic macro dispatches to different expressions based on type at compile time — enables type-safe generic APIs without void* casts.

children.title: Sorting algorithms in C
children.description: Implementing quicksort with a median-of-three pivot and insertion sort fallback for small arrays matches the quality of most standard library implementations — a practical study in C optimization.

children.title: Memory pools for allocation
children.description: Allocating a large block upfront and carving it into fixed-size chunks (a memory pool) avoids malloc overhead and fragmentation — essential for embedded systems and high-performance servers.

children.title: Binary search tree in C
children.description: struct Node { int val; struct Node *left, *right; } is the recursive BST definition — implementing insert, search, and delete in C teaches pointer manipulation and recursive thinking.

children.title: Graph adjacency list
children.description: Representing a graph as an array of linked lists (adjacency list) is memory-efficient for sparse graphs — the standard C representation for BFS, DFS, and shortest path algorithms.

c.title: Systems Programming - Processes and Sockets
c.description: Use POSIX APIs to spawn processes, handle signals, and communicate over TCP sockets. Understand file descriptors and the Unix process model.
c.challenge: Turn your stats project into a client-server app: a server that accepts TCP connections and returns student statistics, and a client that sends queries.

children.title: fork and exec
children.description: fork creates a copy of the current process; exec replaces the current process image with a new program — together they implement shell pipelines and process spawning in Unix.

children.title: Signals and signal handlers
children.description: Signals (SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGSEGV) are asynchronous notifications — signal handlers must be async-signal-safe (no malloc, printf, or locks) as they can interrupt any point in the program.

children.title: TCP socket lifecycle
children.description: socket → bind → listen → accept (server) / socket → connect (client) → send/recv → close — each step has specific error conditions and blocking behavior to understand.

children.title: select and poll for I/O multiplexing
children.description: select/poll monitor multiple file descriptors for readiness — enabling a single-threaded server to handle many connections without blocking on any one of them.

children.title: Pipes and FIFOs
children.description: pipe(fds) creates a unidirectional byte stream between parent and child — the kernel buffers data between writes and reads, making pipes the simplest IPC mechanism.

children.title: Shared memory and semaphores
children.description: mmap with MAP_SHARED or POSIX shm_open shares memory between processes — combine with sem_wait/sem_post for synchronization to prevent races on shared data.

children.title: Beej's Guide to Network Programming
children.description: The definitive free resource for C socket programming — covers TCP/UDP, IPv4/IPv6, getaddrinfo, and non-blocking sockets with working C examples throughout.

children.title: epoll for high-performance servers
children.description: epoll (Linux) is O(1) for event notification across thousands of file descriptors — the foundation of nginx, Redis, and Node.js's event loops. kevent on BSD/macOS is equivalent.

c.title: Production C - Portability, Profiling, and Embedding
c.description: Write portable, production-quality C using CMake, profiling tools, and sanitizers. Understand embedding C as a scripting host and interfacing with other languages.
topics: Calling C from Python via ctypes
Defensive coding and static analysis with cppcheck
c.challenge: Package your client-server project as a CMake build, profile it with gprof to identify bottlenecks, expose the stats engine as a shared library, and call it from a Python script via ctypes.

children.title: C standards: C89, C99, C11, C17, C23
children.description: C89 is the baseline; C99 added // comments, VLAs, and stdint.h; C11 added _Atomic, _Generic, and threads.h; C17 is a bug-fix release — target C11 or C17 for new projects.

children.title: Fixed-width integer types
children.description: stdint.h provides int8_t, int32_t, uint64_t etc. — use them instead of int or long when the exact size matters (network protocols, file formats, hardware registers).

children.title: gprof for function-level profiling
children.description: Compile with -pg and run the program — gprof generates a call graph and per-function timing. Less accurate than perf but requires no root access or kernel support.

children.title: perf for hardware counter profiling
children.description: perf stat counts CPU cycles, cache misses, and branch mispredictions — perf record + perf report generates flamegraphs. No recompilation needed; works on any binary.

children.title: Static vs dynamic linking
children.description: Static linking (-static) bundles all libraries into the binary — portable, no runtime dependencies. Dynamic linking uses shared .so/.dll files — smaller binaries, shared memory, but deployment complexity.

children.title: Embedding C in other languages
children.description: C is the universal FFI target — Python's ctypes, Lua's C API, Ruby's FFI, and Rust's FFI all call C functions. Designing a clean C API enables your library to be used from any language.

children.title: GitHub Actions for C
children.description: A CMake + CTest workflow with sanitizer matrix builds (ASan, UBSan, TSan) and Valgrind catches the most C bugs in CI — use multiple compiler jobs (GCC + Clang) to catch compiler-specific issues.

children.title: cppcheck for static analysis
children.description: cppcheck finds memory leaks, null dereferences, and undefined behavior statically — runs without compilation and complements runtime sanitizers for pre-commit checking.

kotlin.title: Kotlin Syntax and Basics
kotlin.description: Learn Kotlin syntax, type inference, and basic constructs. Understand how Kotlin differs from Java with its concise and expressive style.
topics: Control flow: if, when, for, while
kotlin.challenge: Write a Kotlin command-line app that reads a list of task strings, filters by a keyword, sorts alphabetically, and prints them with indices.

children.title: 100% Java interoperability
children.description: Kotlin compiles to JVM bytecode and can call Java code directly — every Java library works in Kotlin without wrappers, and Kotlin can be adopted incrementally in existing Java projects.

children.title: Conciseness and safety
children.description: Kotlin eliminates boilerplate (no semicolons, type inference, data classes) while adding null safety and immutability — the same logic typically requires 40% fewer lines than Java.

children.title: Nullable and non-nullable types
children.description: String is non-null by default; String? is nullable — the type system enforces null safety at compile time, eliminating NullPointerExceptions structurally.

children.title: Type inference
children.description: Kotlin infers variable types from initializers — val name = "Alice" infers String. Function return types can also be inferred for single-expression functions.

children.title: IntelliJ IDEA and Kotlin plugin
children.description: IntelliJ IDEA has first-class Kotlin support built in — the same IDE that created Kotlin. The free Community edition is sufficient for Kotlin development outside Android.

children.title: Kotlin REPL and Scratch files
children.description: IntelliJ's Kotlin REPL and scratch files let you experiment with Kotlin expressions without creating a project — ideal for learning new APIs or testing snippets quickly.

children.title: Gradle with Kotlin DSL
children.description: Kotlin DSL (build.gradle.kts) replaces Groovy for Gradle builds — statically typed, with autocomplete and refactoring support in the IDE.

children.title: Maven with Kotlin
children.description: kotlin-maven-plugin compiles Kotlin in Maven projects — useful for migrating existing Maven Java projects to Kotlin without switching build tools.

kotlin.title: Null Safety and Type System
kotlin.description: Master Kotlin null safety with nullable types, the Elvis operator, and safe calls. Understand smart casts and sealed classes for exhaustive type handling.
topics: Nullable types and safe call operator
Smart casts with is and as
kotlin.challenge: Add optional metadata to your task app using nullable types, handle missing fields with Elvis operators, and model task status as a sealed class.

children.title: Platform types from Java
children.description: Java types interoperating with Kotlin are 'platform types' (String!) — neither nullable nor non-null. Annotating Java with @Nullable/@NonNull makes the boundary type-safe.

children.title: Safe calls vs non-null assertion
children.description: ?. returns null if the receiver is null (safe); !! throws NPE if null (non-null assertion, should be rare) — prefer safe calls and let/also/run scope functions over !!.

children.title: Compiler-tracked type narrowing
children.description: After an is check, Kotlin automatically casts the variable to the checked type — no explicit cast needed, unlike Java's (Type) cast.

children.title: when expressions as smart cast triggers
children.description: when(x) { is String -> x.length } uses smart casting inside each branch — combined with sealed classes, when becomes exhaustive type dispatch.

children.title: Elvis operator ?:
children.description: val name = user?.name ?: "Guest" returns the right side if the left is null — the Kotlin equivalent of Java's Optional.orElse() without the wrapper type.

children.title: let, run, also, apply scope functions
children.description: ?.let { } executes a block only when non-null and receives the value as it — the idiomatic way to transform nullable values without null checks.

children.title: filterNotNull()
children.description: listOfNullables.filterNotNull() returns a List<T> from a List<T?> — type-safe filtering that removes nulls and narrows the element type at compile time.

children.title: requireNotNull and checkNotNull
children.description: requireNotNull(value) { "message" } throws IllegalArgumentException on null; checkNotNull throws IllegalStateException — use them to assert preconditions clearly.

kotlin.title: OOP - Classes, Data Classes, and Objects
kotlin.description: Use Kotlin classes, data classes, object declarations, and delegation to build concise and idiomatic OOP code. Understand primary constructors and init blocks.
kotlin.challenge: Model your task app entities as data classes, use a companion object for factory methods, and implement interface delegation for pluggable storage backends.

children.title: Generated methods
children.description: data class generates equals(), hashCode(), toString(), copy(), and componentN() functions based on constructor properties — replacing hundreds of lines of boilerplate Java.

children.title: copy() for immutable updates
children.description: val updated = user.copy(name = "Bob") creates a new instance with one field changed — the idiomatic Kotlin way to work with immutable data without mutation.

children.title: object for singletons
children.description: object declarations create thread-safe singletons lazily — no getInstance() or double-checked locking needed, the compiler guarantees a single instance.

children.title: companion object vs static
children.description: companion object {} groups class-level functions and constants — accessed as Class.method(), they compile to Java static members for interoperability.

children.title: Primary and secondary constructors
children.description: Primary constructor params can be val/var to define properties directly — secondary constructors must delegate to the primary with this().

children.title: Sealed classes for ADTs
children.description: sealed class restricts subclasses to the same file — with when expressions, sealed classes enable exhaustive pattern matching over a fixed set of types.

children.title: Class delegation with by
children.description: class LoggingList<T>(inner: MutableList<T>) : MutableList<T> by inner automatically delegates all interface methods to inner — implementing the decorator pattern with zero boilerplate.

children.title: Property delegation with by lazy
children.description: val heavy by lazy { expensiveInit() } defers initialization until first access and caches the result — thread-safe by default, with configurable synchronization modes.

kotlin.title: Functional Programming and Collections
kotlin.description: Apply functional programming with Kotlin lambdas, higher-order functions, and the collections API. Use sequences for lazy evaluation of large datasets.
topics: Scope functions: let, run, with, apply, also
kotlin.challenge: Rewrite all data manipulation in your task app using Kotlin collection operations - group tasks by category, compute stats, and chain operations with sequences.

children.title: Higher-order functions
children.description: Functions that accept or return functions are first-class in Kotlin — inline modifier eliminates the lambda object allocation overhead for performance-critical higher-order functions.

children.title: inline functions and reified types
children.description: inline copies the function body to the call site; reified T makes the generic type available at runtime — enabling typeOf<T>() and is T checks inside inline generics.

children.title: Sequence vs Collection operations
children.description: Collection operations are eager — each step creates an intermediate list. Sequence operations are lazy — they chain into a single pass, like Java Streams.

children.title: generateSequence for infinite sequences
children.description: generateSequence(0) { it + 1 } creates an infinite sequence — take(n) or takeWhile limits consumption. Useful for pagination, fibonacci, or any recursive pattern.

children.title: map, filter, flatMap, groupBy
children.description: Kotlin's collection stdlib has 150+ extension functions — map transforms, filter selects, flatMap flattens, groupBy partitions into a Map<K, List<V>>.

children.title: associate, zip, and partition
children.description: associate transforms to a Map; zip pairs two lists element-wise; partition splits into a Pair<List, List> — three operations that replace common manual iteration patterns.

children.title: Trailing lambda syntax
children.description: When the last parameter is a function, the lambda can go outside the parentheses — list.filter { it > 0 } is cleaner than list.filter({ it > 0 }).

children.title: it and destructuring in lambdas
children.description: Single-parameter lambdas use it by default; multi-parameter lambdas can destructure: map.forEach { (k, v) -> } — both reduce verbosity for common patterns.

kotlin.title: Extension Functions and DSLs
kotlin.description: Extend existing classes without inheritance using extension functions and properties. Build internal DSLs with lambda receivers and infix functions.
kotlin.challenge: Create a task-builder DSL using lambda receivers so tasks can be constructed with a clean block syntax, and add extension functions on List<Task> for common queries.

children.title: Static dispatch at compile time
children.description: Extension functions are compiled to static utility functions — they don't modify the class, have no access to private members, and are dispatched statically not virtually.

children.title: Extension properties
children.description: val String.lastChar: Char get() = this[length - 1] adds computed properties to existing classes — same static dispatch rules as extension functions.

children.title: Function literals with receivers
children.description: A lambda with receiver (block: StringBuilder.() -> Unit) makes this inside the lambda refer to the receiver — the foundation of Kotlin DSLs like buildString, Ktor routing, and Gradle.

resources.label: Function Literals with Receivers — Kotlin Docs

children.title: @DslMarker to prevent nesting
children.description: @DslMarker annotation prevents accidentally calling outer DSL methods inside a nested DSL block — ensuring HTML DSL tags can't nest incorrectly.

children.title: infix notation for DSLs
children.description: infix fun Int.times(task: () -> Unit) enables 3 times { doWork() } — makes domain logic read like natural language, widely used in test DSLs and configuration builders.

children.title: to infix function for Pairs
children.description: 1 to "one" creates a Pair<Int, String> using the built-in to infix function — the idiomatic way to create map entries and pairs without Pair(1, "one") verbosity.

children.title: buildString and buildList
children.description: buildString { append("hello"); append(" world") } uses a StringBuilder receiver — the stdlib DSL builders cover strings, lists, maps, and sequences.

children.title: Ktor routing DSL
children.description: Ktor's routing { get("/path") { call.respond(...) } } is a Kotlin DSL built on function literals with receivers — the clean API demonstrates real production DSL design.

kotlin.title: Coroutines and Async Programming
kotlin.description: Write non-blocking async code using Kotlin coroutines with suspend functions, structured concurrency, and Flow for reactive streams. Replace callbacks and threads.
kotlin.challenge: Add async I/O to your task app: load tasks from a simulated remote source using coroutines, expose updates as a Flow, and collect them in a coroutine scope.

children.title: Continuation passing style
children.description: Kotlin compiles suspend functions to state machines using continuations — each suspension point is a state, and the runtime resumes execution without blocking a thread.

children.title: Coroutine context and dispatchers
children.description: CoroutineContext holds the dispatcher (which thread pool), job, and other elements — Dispatchers.IO for I/O, Dispatchers.Default for CPU work, Dispatchers.Main for UI.

children.title: Coroutine scope and job hierarchy
children.description: Every coroutine belongs to a scope — if the scope is cancelled, all child coroutines are cancelled. This prevents goroutine/thread leaks and makes cancellation predictable.

children.title: supervisorScope for independent failures
children.description: supervisorScope lets child coroutines fail independently — one failing child doesn't cancel siblings, unlike coroutineScope where any failure cancels all children.

children.title: launch vs async
children.description: launch fires a coroutine and returns a Job (no result); async returns a Deferred<T> — call .await() to get the result. Use async for parallel work that produces values.

children.title: withContext for switching dispatchers
children.description: withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { } switches dispatcher for the block and returns to the original — use it to call blocking I/O from a coroutine without blocking the calling thread.

children.title: Flow for async streams
children.description: Flow<T> is a cold async stream — it doesn't produce values until collected. Use flow { emit(value) } to create and .collect { } to consume.

children.title: StateFlow and SharedFlow
children.description: StateFlow holds a current value and emits updates (like LiveData); SharedFlow broadcasts to multiple collectors — both are hot flows backed by coroutines.

kotlin.title: Testing Kotlin Applications
kotlin.description: Test Kotlin code idiomatically using JUnit 5 with Kotlin extensions, MockK for mocking, and Kotest for expressive specs. Test coroutine-based code with runTest.
kotlin.challenge: Write a full test suite for your task app covering domain logic, storage backends (using MockK), and the async Flow pipeline using runTest.

children.title: Kotest architecture
children.description: Kotest provides multiple test styles (FreeSpec, BehaviorSpec, StringSpec) and a rich assertion library — more flexible than JUnit for Kotlin-idiomatic tests.

children.title: Coroutine testing with runTest
children.description: kotlinx-coroutines-test provides runTest { } which controls virtual time — test suspend functions and delay() calls without real-time waiting.

children.title: Kotlin-native mocking
children.description: MockK uses Kotlin coroutine and reflection APIs to mock final classes, extension functions, and suspend functions — things Mockito can't handle natively.

children.title: coEvery and coVerify for coroutines
children.description: coEvery { mock.suspend() } returns value stubs suspend functions; coVerify verifies suspend function calls — MockK's coroutine-aware counterparts to every and verify.

children.title: Data-driven tests with parameterized
children.description: JUnit 5 @ParameterizedTest works in Kotlin; Kotest's forAll { a, b -> } integrates data-driven and property-based testing in a Kotlin-idiomatic style.

children.title: AssertJ with Kotlin extensions
children.description: assertThat(result).isEqualTo(expected) with AssertJ's Kotlin extensions provides rich, readable assertions with detailed failure messages for complex objects.

children.title: Spring Boot Test with Kotlin
children.description: @SpringBootTest works seamlessly with Kotlin — use MockkBean instead of MockBean for Kotlin-native mocking of Spring components.

children.title: Testcontainers with Kotlin
children.description: Testcontainers' Kotlin DSL (withContainers { }) provides a clean API for starting Docker containers in tests — database, Redis, or any service your integration tests need.

kotlin.title: Kotlin Multiplatform and Stdlib Internals
kotlin.description: Explore the Kotlin standard library deeply and understand Kotlin Multiplatform for sharing code across JVM, native, and JavaScript targets.
kotlin.challenge: Extract the core domain logic of your task app into a KMP shared module and provide JVM and JS platform-specific implementations for storage.

children.title: expect/actual mechanism
children.description: expect declares a platform-agnostic API; actual provides the platform-specific implementation — KMP compiles the same business logic for JVM, iOS, JS, and native targets.

children.title: Shared code and platform targets
children.description: commonMain holds shared business logic; jvmMain, iosMain, jsMain hold platform implementations — KMP's source set structure isolates platform-specific code cleanly.

children.title: Contracts for smart cast hints
children.description: kotlin.contracts lets functions advertise to the compiler that a lambda runs exactly once or that null implies a return — enabling smart casts after custom check functions.

children.title: Reflection in Kotlin
children.description: KClass, KFunction, and KProperty provide Kotlin-aware reflection — myClass::class.memberProperties enumerates properties with full type information including nullability.

children.title: Ktor as a multiplatform HTTP client
children.description: ktor-client works on JVM, Android, iOS, and JS — the same API for all platforms with platform-specific engines (OkHttp for Android, Darwin for iOS).

children.title: SQLDelight for multiplatform databases
children.description: SQLDelight generates type-safe Kotlin APIs from SQL queries and supports SQLite on Android, iOS, and JVM — zero-compromise SQL with multiplatform support.

children.title: Kotlin bytecode inspection
children.description: IntelliJ's Show Kotlin Bytecode → Decompile shows the Java equivalent of Kotlin code — essential for understanding boxing, object creation, and the cost of language features.

children.title: Java Interop best practices
children.description: @JvmStatic, @JvmField, and @JvmOverloads generate Java-friendly APIs from Kotlin — essential when writing Kotlin libraries consumed by Java callers.

kotlin.title: Kotlin on the JVM - Performance and Interop
kotlin.description: Understand how Kotlin compiles to JVM bytecode and how to interoperate cleanly with Java libraries. Analyze performance characteristics and avoid common pitfalls.
topics: Calling Java from Kotlin and vice versa
kotlin.challenge: Integrate a Java library into your task app, expose Kotlin code to Java consumers using @JvmStatic annotations, and use inline reified functions for type-safe serialization.

children.title: Kotlin data classes and bytecode
children.description: Data classes generate equals/hashCode based on constructor properties — fields declared in the class body are excluded, which surprises developers coming from Java records.

children.title: Boxing of nullable primitives
children.description: Int is compiled to int (primitive); Int? is compiled to Integer (boxed) — nullable primitives cause boxing overhead in hot paths, use IntArray instead of Array<Int>.

children.title: Calling Java from Kotlin
children.description: Java code is consumed naturally in Kotlin with platform types — annotate Java with JSR-305 (@Nullable/@NonNull) to get null safety across the Java-Kotlin boundary.

children.title: Kotlin functional interfaces (SAM)
children.description: fun interface MyCallback { fun invoke(value: Int) } defines a SAM interface — Java lambdas can implement it and Kotlin lambdas can be passed where it's expected.

children.title: Sequences vs Lists for large data
children.description: Chaining collection operations on large lists creates intermediate lists — switching to asSequence() avoids allocations by processing lazily in a single pass.

children.title: inline classes for value wrappers
children.description: @JvmInline value class Meters(val value: Double) wraps a primitive with zero overhead at runtime — the compiler substitutes the underlying type directly in bytecode.

children.title: Kotlin Spring extensions
children.description: Spring provides Kotlin extensions for a more idiomatic API — context.getBean<MyService>() instead of context.getBean(MyService::class.java) using reified generics.

children.title: Coroutine support in Spring WebFlux
children.description: Spring WebFlux supports suspend functions natively — annotate controller methods with suspend and return values directly instead of wrapping in Mono/Flux.

kotlin.title: Production Kotlin - Ktor, DI, and Deployment
kotlin.description: Build and deploy a production Kotlin backend using Ktor for HTTP, Koin for dependency injection, and Docker for containerization. Apply structured concurrency throughout.
kotlin.challenge: Deploy your task app as a Ktor REST API with Koin DI, kotlinx.serialization, coroutine-based handlers, a Docker container, and a GitHub Actions CI pipeline.

children.title: Plugin-based architecture
children.description: Ktor uses installable plugins (Authentication, Serialization, Logging) applied to the Application pipeline — each plugin intercepts the request/response pipeline at a specific phase.

children.title: Content negotiation and serialization
children.description: Ktor's ContentNegotiation plugin with kotlinx.serialization handles JSON, XML, and CBOR — @Serializable data classes are automatically encoded and decoded.

children.title: Koin for Kotlin DI
children.description: Koin uses Kotlin DSL (val appModule = module { single { MyService() } }) instead of annotation processing — simpler to reason about, faster startup, and no reflection overhead.

children.title: Koin vs Hilt vs Dagger
children.description: Hilt/Dagger use annotation processing for compile-time DI (zero runtime overhead, verbose setup); Koin uses runtime DSL (simpler, small overhead) — Hilt is standard for Android, Koin for multiplatform.

children.title: Arrow for functional programming
children.description: Arrow brings Either, Option, Validated, and Effect types to Kotlin — enabling railway-oriented error handling and functional domain modeling with full coroutine support.

children.title: Exposed for database access
children.description: Exposed is JetBrains' Kotlin SQL framework — offers both a type-safe DSL (for query building) and DAO (for ORM) with coroutine support.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Kotlin
children.description: actions/setup-java with Kotlin Gradle projects — cache ~/.gradle for fast builds, run ./gradlew test and detekt for quality gates on every PR.

children.title: Detekt for static analysis
children.description: Detekt is Kotlin-specific static analysis — catches complexity, style, and potential bugs. Configure with detekt.yml and run in CI to enforce code quality standards.

swift.title: Swift Syntax and Basics
swift.description: Learn Swift syntax, constants, variables, and basic control flow in Xcode Playgrounds. Understand Swift type safety and type inference.
topics: Basic types: Int, Double, String, Bool
Control flow: if, switch, for-in, while
swift.challenge: Write a Swift playground that models a simple inventory: add items, remove them, compute total value, and print a formatted report.

children.title: Safety first
children.description: Swift eliminates entire classes of bugs by design — no implicit nil, no uninitialized variables, bounds-checked arrays, and overflow-checked arithmetic by default.

children.title: ARC memory management
children.description: Swift uses Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) instead of a garbage collector — memory is freed deterministically when the last strong reference is released, with no GC pauses.

children.title: Strong static typing with inference
children.description: Swift infers types from context — let name = "Alice" infers String, but function signatures require explicit types for clarity and API stability.

children.title: Value semantics by default
children.description: Structs, enums, and tuples are value types — assignment copies the value. This prevents shared mutable state bugs that are common with reference types everywhere.

resources.label: Value and Reference Types — Swift Blog

children.title: Xcode and Swift Playgrounds
children.description: Xcode is the primary Swift IDE — Swift Playgrounds provides instant feedback without a full project setup, ideal for learning and prototyping.

children.title: Swift on Linux and Windows
children.description: Swift is open source and runs on Linux and Windows — swift.org provides downloadable toolchains for server-side development without macOS.

children.title: let vs var
children.description: let declares immutable constants; var declares mutable variables — prefer let by default and only use var when mutation is necessary.

children.title: String interpolation
children.description: "Hello, \(name)!" embeds any expression directly in a string — more powerful than format strings, supporting arbitrary expressions inside \().

swift.title: Optionals and Error Handling
swift.description: Master Swift optionals with optional binding, guard statements, and nil coalescing. Handle errors with throwing functions and do-catch blocks.
swift.challenge: Add file-based persistence to your inventory app, using throwing functions for I/O errors, guard for validation, and Result for operation outcomes.

children.title: Optional as an enum
children.description: Optional<T> is literally enum Optional { case none; case some(T) } — every optional operation (map, flatMap, ??) is just pattern matching on this enum.

children.title: Optional chaining semantics
children.description: user?.address?.city returns Optional<String> — the whole chain short-circuits to nil if any link is nil, without nested if-let chains.

children.title: Typed throws
children.description: Swift errors are typed — func parse() throws means the caller must handle errors with try/catch. Swift 6 introduces typed throws (throws(ParseError)) for precise error types.

children.title: Result<T,E> type
children.description: Result<Success, Failure> wraps a value or error for async callbacks and cases where you can't use throws — .get() converts it back to a throwing expression.

children.title: if let and guard let
children.description: if let binds an optional in the true branch only; guard let binds it for the rest of the scope — guard is preferred in functions to reduce nesting.

children.title: Nil coalescing ?? and if let shorthand
children.description: value ?? default returns the unwrapped value or a fallback — Swift 5.7 added if let name { } shorthand that reuses the variable name without repetition.

children.title: try, try?, and try!
children.description: try propagates errors; try? converts to nil on failure; try! crashes on failure — use try? when failure is expected, try! only when failure is genuinely impossible.

children.title: defer for cleanup
children.description: defer executes code when the scope exits, regardless of how — the Swift replacement for finally blocks, ensuring resources are always cleaned up.

swift.title: Structs, Classes, and Value Semantics
swift.description: Understand the distinction between value types (structs, enums) and reference types (classes) in Swift. Apply value semantics for predictable state management.
swift.challenge: Refactor your inventory app to use structs for Item and value-semantic collections, ensuring mutations are explicit and testable.

children.title: Copy-on-write for collections
children.description: Swift arrays, dictionaries, and strings use copy-on-write — they share storage until one copy is mutated, making value semantics efficient without eager copying.

children.title: Mutating methods on structs
children.description: Methods that modify struct properties must be marked mutating — this makes mutation explicit and prevents accidental modification through let-bound struct instances.

children.title: Identity vs equality
children.description: === checks if two class instances are the same object in memory; == checks value equality — structs only support ==, classes support both.

children.title: Weak and unowned references
children.description: weak references become nil when the object is deallocated (optional); unowned references don't (non-optional, crash if accessed after deallocation) — both break ARC retain cycles.

children.title: When to use struct
children.description: Use struct for data models, view models, and any type that benefits from value semantics — most Swift types should be structs. SwiftUI views are structs by requirement.

children.title: When to use class
children.description: Use class for objects that need identity, shared mutable state, or inheritance — UIViewController, NSObject subclasses, and objects managed by Objective-C runtimes require classes.

resources.label: When to Use Classes in Swift

children.title: Computed and stored properties
children.description: Stored properties hold values; computed properties calculate them on access with get/set — computed properties appear identical to callers, enabling future internal refactoring.

children.title: Property observers willSet/didSet
children.description: willSet fires before a stored property changes; didSet fires after — useful for triggering side effects like UI updates without subclassing.

swift.title: Protocols and Generics
swift.description: Define behavior with protocols and write reusable generic types and functions. Understand protocol-oriented programming as the Swift paradigm.
swift.challenge: Define a generic Repository protocol with associated types for your inventory entities, provide an in-memory and a file-backed conformance.

children.title: Existentials vs generics
children.description: any Protocol (existential) uses a box with a vtable — dynamic dispatch with overhead. some Protocol (opaque type) is statically dispatched. Prefer generics/opaque types for performance.

children.title: Protocol witness tables
children.description: Protocols with associated types (PAT) can't be used as existentials directly — they require generics or opaque return types (some Protocol) because the concrete type must be known at compile time.

children.title: Type constraints and where clauses
children.description: func max<T: Comparable>(_ a: T, _ b: T) -> T constrains T to types supporting <. where clauses extend constraints: where T: Collection, T.Element: Hashable.

children.title: Opaque types (some)
children.description: some View hides the concrete return type while preserving type identity for the compiler — SwiftUI uses this to enable static dispatch on view hierarchies.

children.title: Protocol extensions for default implementations
children.description: extension MyProtocol { func method() { } } provides default implementations — conforming types get the method for free and can override it selectively.

children.title: Conditional conformances
children.description: extension Array: Equatable where Element: Equatable makes Array<Int> equatable when its element is — conditional conformance applies a protocol only when constraints are met.

children.title: Generic data structures
children.description: struct Stack<T> { var items: [T]; mutating func push(_ item: T) } — generics make data structures fully reusable without Any casts or runtime type checks.

children.title: Phantom types for type safety
children.description: struct Validated<T>: a phantom type parameter T never appears in stored properties but distinguishes Validated<Email> from Validated<URL> at compile time.

swift.title: Collections, Functional Patterns, and Closures
swift.description: Use Swift collections idiomatically with map, filter, reduce, and compactMap. Write expressive closures with capture lists and trailing syntax.
swift.challenge: Rewrite all query logic in your inventory app using Swift collection operations, grouping items by category and computing aggregates with reduce.

children.title: Higher-order functions on collections
children.description: map, filter, reduce, flatMap, compactMap — all available on Swift collections with full type inference. compactMap combines map and nil-filtering in one pass.

children.title: Closure capture semantics
children.description: Swift closures capture values by reference by default for reference types; capture lists ([weak self], [unowned self]) break retain cycles and control ownership explicitly.

children.title: lazy for deferred evaluation
children.description: array.lazy.map { }.filter { } creates a lazy sequence — operations are applied only when elements are accessed, not upfront. Essential for large collections.

children.title: Sequence and IteratorProtocol
children.description: Implementing IteratorProtocol (next()) and Sequence (makeIterator()) makes any type work with for loops, map, filter, and all Sequence algorithms.

children.title: Trailing closure syntax
children.description: When the last argument is a closure, it can go outside the parentheses — array.sort { $0 < $1 } is idiomatic Swift for concise closure-based APIs.

children.title: @escaping closures
children.description: @escaping marks closures that outlive the function call (stored or dispatched async) — the compiler requires this annotation to ensure callers know the closure may execute later.

children.title: zip and enumerated
children.description: zip(a, b) pairs elements from two sequences; enumerated() adds indices — both avoid manual index tracking while iterating.

children.title: Dictionary grouping and merging
children.description: Dictionary(grouping: array) { $0.category } groups an array into a dictionary by key — Dictionary(uniqueKeysWithValues: pairs) builds a dictionary from a sequence of (key, value) tuples.

swift.title: Concurrency - Async/Await and Actors
swift.description: Write safe concurrent code using Swift structured concurrency with async/await, actors, and task groups. Eliminate data races at compile time.
swift.challenge: Convert your repository layer to use async/await for simulated network fetches, protect shared state with an actor, and load items concurrently using TaskGroup.

children.title: Cooperative thread pool
children.description: Swift's concurrency runtime uses a cooperative thread pool sized to the number of CPU cores — tasks yield at await points instead of blocking threads, enabling millions of concurrent tasks.

resources.label: Swift Concurrency Behind the Scenes — WWDC21

children.title: Task tree and structured concurrency
children.description: async let and TaskGroup create structured concurrency — child tasks are automatically cancelled if the parent is cancelled, preventing resource leaks.

children.title: Actor isolation
children.description: Actors serialize access to their mutable state — only one task executes actor code at a time, eliminating data races on actor properties without manual locking.

children.title: @MainActor for UI updates
children.description: @MainActor guarantees code runs on the main thread — annotate view models and UI-updating methods to prevent "UI update from background thread" crashes.

children.title: async let for parallel work
children.description: async let x = fetch(); async let y = fetch() starts both concurrently — let (a, b) = await (x, y) waits for both results without sequential blocking.

children.title: withTaskGroup for dynamic parallelism
children.description: withTaskGroup(of: T.self) { group in group.addTask { } } enables adding a dynamic number of concurrent tasks and collecting their results.

children.title: for await in async sequences
children.description: for await item in asyncSequence { } iterates over values produced asynchronously — used for streaming data, notifications, and paginated API responses.

children.title: AsyncStream for bridging callbacks
children.description: AsyncStream<T> { continuation in } bridges callback-based APIs to async/await — call continuation.yield(value) in the callback and the stream delivers values to the for await loop.

swift.title: Swift Package Manager and Ecosystem
swift.description: Manage Swift packages with the Swift Package Manager, create reusable libraries, and integrate third-party dependencies. Understand manifest authoring.
swift.challenge: Extract the domain and repository layer of your inventory app into a standalone Swift package and depend on it from the main app target.

children.title: Package.swift manifest
children.description: Package.swift is a Swift source file describing targets, dependencies, and products — it's compiled and executed by SPM, enabling dynamic configuration with Swift code.

children.title: Targets, products, and dependencies
children.description: A package can have multiple targets (library, executable, test) — targets declare dependencies on other targets or external packages, and products expose targets to consumers.

children.title: Access control levels
children.description: open > public > internal (default) > fileprivate > private — internal is the default, making symbols visible within the module. Explicit public is required for framework APIs.

children.title: @testable import
children.description: @testable import MyModule gives tests access to internal symbols — the testing module can see all internal types and functions without making them public.

children.title: Adding and resolving dependencies
children.description: swift package update resolves and pins dependency versions to Package.resolved — commit this file to ensure reproducible builds across team members and CI.

children.title: Local and binary packages
children.description: path: "../MyLocalPackage" uses a local package for development; binaryTarget bundles pre-compiled XCFrameworks — useful for closed-source dependencies.

children.title: Combine for reactive programming
children.description: Apple's Combine framework provides Publishers and Subscribers for reactive event streams — integrated with SwiftUI, URLSession, and NotificationCenter across Apple platforms.

children.title: Popular SPM packages
children.description: Alamofire (networking), SnapKit (Auto Layout DSL), Kingfisher (image loading), and SwiftLint (linting) are widely used — most iOS libraries now ship as SPM packages.

swift.title: Testing with XCTest and Swift Testing
swift.description: Test Swift code with XCTest and the new Swift Testing framework using macros. Write async tests, mock protocols, and achieve meaningful coverage.
topics: Testing async code with async tests
swift.challenge: Write a complete test suite for your inventory package using Swift Testing macros, covering repository operations, async fetch, and actor-protected state.

children.title: XCTest architecture
children.description: XCTest is Apple's built-in testing framework — XCTestCase classes with test methods, setUp/tearDown lifecycle, and XCTAssert* functions. Integrates directly with Xcode's Test navigator.

children.title: Swift Testing framework
children.description: Swift Testing (Swift 5.9+) uses @Test, #expect(), and #require() macros — more expressive than XCTest with parameterized tests, tags, and parallel execution by default.

children.title: Protocol-based mocking
children.description: Defining dependencies as protocols and creating mock implementations in tests is the standard Swift mocking approach — no reflection needed, full type safety.

children.title: Swift Mocking with Mockingbird
children.description: Mockingbird generates mock classes at compile time from protocols — reducing manual mock boilerplate significantly.

children.title: Given-When-Then in Swift
children.description: Structuring tests with descriptive names (test_fetchUser_whenNetworkFails_returnsError) and clear given/when/then phases makes tests readable documentation.

children.title: Parameterized tests in Swift Testing
children.description: @Test(arguments: [1, 2, 3]) func testValue(_ n: Int) { } runs the same test for each argument — replacing multiple repetitive test methods.

children.title: XCUITest for UI automation
children.description: XCUITest records and replays UI interactions — use accessibility identifiers to find elements reliably, and prefer UI tests for critical user flows.

children.title: Snapshot testing
children.description: pointfreeco/swift-snapshot-testing captures view renderings and fails when they change unexpectedly — the standard approach for visual regression testing in Swift.

swift.title: Advanced Swift - Macros, Result Builders, and Reflection
swift.description: Use Swift macros and result builders to reduce boilerplate and create expressive APIs. Explore runtime reflection with Mirror for debugging and serialization.
topics: Writing a Swift macro with SwiftSyntax
Custom string convertible and debug descriptions
swift.challenge: Add a result builder to your inventory DSL for constructing item lists declaratively, and write a macro to auto-generate equality and description implementations.

children.title: Attached and freestanding macros
children.description: Swift 5.9 macros run at compile time to generate code — @Observable, #Preview, and #expect are macros. They're type-checked and debuggable, unlike C preprocessor macros.

children.title: SwiftSyntax for macro development
children.description: Swift macros use SwiftSyntax to parse and transform the AST — macros are ordinary Swift programs that receive syntax nodes and return new syntax.

children.title: @resultBuilder for DSLs
children.description: @resultBuilder transforms a block of statements into a single value — the mechanism behind SwiftUI's body { } syntax and any embedded DSL in Swift.

children.title: Building custom DSLs
children.description: Implementing @resultBuilder with buildBlock, buildOptional, and buildArray enables custom HTML builders, query builders, and layout DSLs identical to SwiftUI's style.

children.title: KeyPath for type-safe property access
children.description: \User.name is a KeyPath<User, String> — a first-class reference to a property usable for sorting, filtering, and key-value observation without stringly-typed APIs.

children.title: Dynamic member lookup
children.description: @dynamicMemberLookup enables subscript-style access to arbitrary members — used by SwiftUI's environment values and Python/JS bridge libraries.

children.title: Mirror for runtime introspection
children.description: Mirror(reflecting: value) provides runtime reflection — enumerate properties, get display style, and inspect child values. Used by debugging tools and serialization libraries.

children.title: CustomReflectable for custom mirrors
children.description: Implementing CustomReflectable controls how your type appears in debuggers and Mirror — useful for hiding implementation details and showing meaningful debug representations.

swift.title: Production Swift - Server-Side and Cross-Platform
swift.description: Deploy Swift as a server-side language using Vapor, run it on Linux inside Docker, and apply production patterns including logging, metrics, and CI.
topics: CI with GitHub Actions on Linux runners
swift.challenge: Deploy your inventory app as a Vapor REST API on Linux via Docker, with Fluent persistence, async handlers, structured logging, and a GitHub Actions CI pipeline.

children.title: Vapor framework
children.description: Vapor is the most popular server-side Swift framework — routing, middleware, Fluent ORM, Leaf templating, and WebSockets with async/await throughout.

children.title: Hummingbird as a lightweight alternative
children.description: Hummingbird is a lightweight HTTP framework built on Swift NIO — minimal core with middleware extensions, suitable for microservices where Vapor's full stack is overkill.

children.title: Swift NIO for non-blocking I/O
children.description: Swift NIO is the asynchronous networking library underlying Vapor and Hummingbird — event-loop based, similar to Netty, enabling high-throughput servers with minimal threads.

children.title: Instruments for profiling
children.description: Xcode Instruments provides CPU, memory, network, and energy profilers — the Time Profiler and Allocations instruments are essential for optimizing Swift apps.

children.title: Docker for server-side Swift
children.description: swift:slim Docker images compile and run Swift on Linux — multi-stage builds with swift:slim builder and ubuntu:jammy runtime keep production images under 100MB.

children.title: Swift on AWS Lambda
children.description: swift-aws-lambda-runtime enables Swift functions on AWS Lambda — cold starts under 100ms and tiny binaries from static compilation make Swift competitive for serverless.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Swift
children.description: actions/setup-xcode pins the Xcode version; xcodebuild test runs the test suite — add SwiftLint and swift build for a complete quality pipeline.

children.title: fastlane for iOS automation
children.description: fastlane automates building, testing, and releasing iOS apps — lanes for match (certificates), scan (tests), and deliver (App Store submission) handle the entire release pipeline.

dart.title: Dart Syntax and Basics
dart.description: Learn Dart syntax, types, and control flow using DartPad. Understand Dart as a strongly-typed, compiled language designed for fast development.
topics: Built-in types: int, double, String, bool, List, Map
Control flow: if, for, while, switch
dart.challenge: Write a Dart script that manages a list of contacts (name, email, phone), supports add/remove/search, and prints a formatted directory.

children.title: Mental models and first principles
children.description: Strong programming languages practitioners build mental models before touching a keyboard. Understanding the why behind dart syntax and basics lets you adapt when tools change.

elixir.title: OTP - GenServer, Supervisor, and Agent
